additional_info: Synergies are a critical component to an M&A case, and should be divided into revenue synergies and cost synergies.

Cost Synergies include Fixed (IT Integration, Headcount Reduction, Headquarters Consolidation, Back-office / Redundant Site Consolidation) and Variable (Purchasing Power of Raw Materials, Manufacturing Costs, Distribution Efficiencies).
Revenue Synergies include New potential markets (selling into new channels or geographies) and Cross-selling opportunities (selling to one another's customers).
solution: The company should consider both revenue and cost synergies. Cost synergies can be fixed or variable, including IT integration, headcount reduction, consolidation of headquarters and back-office sites, purchasing power, manufacturing, and distribution efficiencies. Revenue synergies include entering new markets and cross-selling opportunities.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate understanding of synergy categories, ability to structure synergies into revenue and cost buckets, and provide relevant examples.

title: Chemical Consolidation: Question 3
description: The Dowling management team is comfortable with the financial analysis for the merger, but is concerned about risks. What risks should Dowling be considering that may pose challenges to the merger?
additional_info: Culture: do the cultures align and if not, will that present challenges? Will reducing headcount cause culture issues?
Management: who will run the combined company?
Headquarters: Where will new HQ be and will employees be willing to relocate?
Systems: Will IT integration be complex and expensive?
Scale: will a bigger company be harder to manage?
Regulatory: There are frequently anti-trust issues with mega-mergers; the top four producers account for 90% of the market.
solution: Risks include cultural misalignment, management structure uncertainty, HQ location and employee relocation issues, IT integration complexity, challenges managing a larger scale company, and regulatory/anti-trust concerns.
evaluation: Candidate should identify a broad range of risks including cultural, operational, managerial, regulatory, and integration challenges.

title: Steel Co.
description: Our client is a steel manufacturing company in the Netherlands. They are considering investing in an advanced analytics tool that will help them better predict inventory schedule. They are also looking at immediate methods to save costs and would like your help with the following: A) Steel coils are currently transported from the factory to customers via Railways, Trucks, Vessels and Barges. They would like us to analyze these four methods, and suggest any opportunities for cost saving. B) Suggest any alternative techniques to save cost in the immediate future.
additional_info: Operates internationally in 100+ countries.
Customers are predominantly large B2B manufacturers with short and long-term contracts.
Steel is only manufactured as coils.
Objective is to identify cost saving with no particular target.
Exhibit 1 provides data on deliveries/week, volume/delivery, utilization %, cost per ton, and frequency for each transport method.
Exhibit 2 shows transfer of goods from pier to trucks over weeks.
Utilization refers to % of vehicle capacity used for steel coils; remaining space is empty.
Truck usage is high due to vessels requiring 100% capacity before departure, causing delays and truck backlogs.
solution: The client can save approximately $7.84M by optimizing capacity utilization: $7.3M from better utilization of transport methods and $540K from improved inventory management (e.g., adjusting vessel KPIs to 95% utilization instead of 100%). They should continue developing the advanced analytics tool to improve inventory management and gradually reduce truck usage to improve utilization.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze transport methods quantitatively, identify underutilization and cost drivers, propose operational improvements, and consider both immediate and longer-term cost-saving techniques.

title: All Night Long
description: Your client is CNB Movies, a national movie theater company with $2 billion in annual revenue. In recent years, movie theaters have seen record high demand for Marvel and DC Comics movies, and CNB is considering ways to capitalize on this demand. One option is to offer 24-hour opening weekend screenings for these movies. The company has hired you to advise on the decision. Should they offer this experience?
additional_info: Opening weekend starts Thursday night and ends Sunday at 11:59 PM.
CNB wants to test one theater first; if successful, roll out to all 400 theaters in the US.
Ticket prices are $15 adults / $10 children/seniors.
None of CNB’s competitors currently offer this experience.
CNB can finance this on its own.
Exhibit 1 shows expected seat utilization per showing by time and demographic.
Average concession revenue is $10 per person.
Exhibit 2 shows current daily costs per theater: staff $2,600, concessions $2,000, concession maintenance $400, projector maintenance $5,000.
Under the new model, costs increase by 50% for staff and concessions, 25% for concession maintenance, and 10% for projector maintenance.
additional_questions: What are some additional factors that our client should consider before making their final decision?
solution: The 24-hour experience is expected to generate incremental profits of $10,800 per theater over the weekend (22% profit margin). It provides a competitive advantage for high-grossing movies. CNB should implement the 24-hour model starting with a test theater, develop a marketing strategy, conduct sensitivity analyses on demand and costs, and plan an optimal rollout strategy.
evaluation: Candidate should structure revenue and cost analysis accurately, incorporate mental math, consider concessions and operational costs, brainstorm qualitative factors including competitive response and customer behavior, and provide a clear recommendation.

title: Is Teleconferencing a Good Call?
description: Your client is a national wealth management firm within a larger bank. The current CIO has created a test pilot of a cutting-edge virtual conferencing software in the NYC office. The firm currently employs many Wealth Advisors who meet clients at satellite offices around the country and is deciding whether to roll-out this software across all locations. The aim is to reduce Advisor travel costs, which is currently borne by the firm, at cost. How should the client proceed?
additional_info: Time frame: as soon as possible.
Geography: US only; Advisors located in NYC, Houston, Chicago, San Francisco; satellite offices scattered in Midwest and southern US.
Current state: primarily in-person meetings; no virtual conferencing except 6-month pilot in NYC.
Technology: virtual conferencing includes instant messaging, video chat, screen sharing, conference calling, call forwarding.
Goal: reduce costs; revenue growth not in scope.
Travel costs per city and number of advisors provided (Exhibit 1).
Two teleconferencing implementation options: Outside Vendor and Build In-House with associated costs and payback periods (Exhibit 2).
additional_questions: What would you include in your data request to address this problem?
How much is spent on travel per year? On average, how much travel cost does each employee incur per year? What insights can you draw?
What is the annual cost of each teleconferencing solution? How long will each solution take to reach break-even? How much would each solution save in travel costs annually?
What other factors should we consider before making a decision?
solution: Travel costs are approximately $48.2M annually. Teleconferencing can reduce travel expenses by about 10% (~$4.82M). Both Outside Vendor and Build In-House options have similar payback periods (~2-3 months). The choice depends on qualitative factors such as internal manpower, customization, cybersecurity, client experience, and compliance. The recommendation is to proceed with teleconferencing but further study qualitative impacts and employee/customer feedback before finalizing the solution.
evaluation: Candidate should request relevant data, perform cost-benefit analysis including payback period, compare options quantitatively and qualitatively, and consider operational and compliance risks.

title: Apple of My Eye
description: Bob is a master brewer who opened a brewery called Something Witty featuring Belgian Witbier styled brews. He has noticed an increase in customers asking for gluten-free alternatives, such as cider. Bob has hired us to help him analyze his options.
additional_info: Bob’s goal is to continue to be profitable regardless of expansion option.
Currently, Bob only sells bottled beer direct to consumers.

Exhibit 1 shows production impacts if renovating brewery to produce cider: beer production decreases from 30 to 24 cases/day; cider production starts at 12 cases/day; selling prices are $5 for beer and $6 for cider.
Bob sells by the bottle; 30 bottles per case; production runs Monday-Friday (20 days/month).
15.5 million bottles of cider are expected to be sold in NYC next year.
additional_questions: What are your thoughts on these two options?
What will be the change in Bob’s annual revenue if he begins to produce cider?
Besides potential revenue, what other factors should Bob consider before expanding into the cider market?
Bob has determined he needs to capture at least 0.5% of the NYC take-home cider market annually to break-even. Will he be able to produce enough cider to capture the 0.5%?
solution: Bob should invest in cider production. He will see an increase in revenues of approximately $302,400 annually and can produce enough cider to capture the 0.5% market share needed to break-even. However, he should consider market demand, competition, cannibalization of beer sales, and costs related to labor, equipment, marketing, and distribution.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue impact quantitatively, brainstorm costs and market factors, assess production capacity vs market size, and provide a balanced recommendation.

title: Jimmy’s Dilemma
description: Jimmy Smith, an MBA2 at NYU Stern, has two job offers from competing firms (Firm A and Firm B) and needs to decide which offer to accept. He seeks to maximize financial value over 3 years.
additional_info: Jimmy only cares about money and plans to work 3 years at either firm.
Firm A is second tier but competes with top-tier firms; Firm B is top-tier and more prestigious.
Compensation packages include salary, signing bonus, annual bonus, and tuition reimbursement (Firm A only).
Exhibits provide detailed compensation data and exit opportunity salary distributions.
Exit opportunities after 3 years differ, with Firm B offering higher expected salaries.
Discount rate for NPV calculations is 14%.
additional_questions: What other things should Jimmy consider before making a decision?
What do you make of the exit opportunities data Jimmy collected?
Which offer is best based on financial and exit opportunity analysis?
solution: Firm A has a higher 3-year NPV ($440k vs $430k), but Firm B offers better exit opportunities with higher expected salaries after 3 years. Discounting exit opportunities to present value shows Firm B is financially better overall. Jimmy should choose Firm B considering long-term value.
evaluation: Candidate should perform DCF/NPV calculations, compare compensation packages, incorporate exit opportunity valuations, brainstorm financial and non-financial factors, and provide a structured recommendation.

title: Apartment Co.
description: Apartment Co is a residential real estate company owning 10 buildings in NYC boroughs. Despite $5M invested in infrastructure upgrades and increased rent prices, profitability has not improved as expected. The company seeks ways to achieve higher long-term profitability.
additional_info: Revenue source is tenant rent; 50 units per building; 5 buildings in Manhattan, 3 in Brooklyn, 2 in Queens.
Buildings fully owned; 30 maintenance staff contracted.
Tenants mostly young working professionals.
Since 2017, management pays utilities as part of rent to stay competitive.
Exhibits provide financial data, electricity rates, utility spending, and salary info.
Two investment options to reduce utilities: energy efficient lighting and solar panels with associated costs and savings.
Energy efficient lighting: $1.5M cost, $750K annual savings, 2-year payback, 10-year bulb lifespan.
Solar panels: $3.2M cost, $960K annual savings, ~3-year payback, 20-year lifespan.
additional_questions: What are some ways Apartment Co. can reduce their utilities expense?
Evaluate the two investment options: energy efficient lighting and solar panels. Which should be chosen?
solution: Apartment Co. should consider installing solar panels for greater long-term savings and tax benefits, energy efficient lighting for quicker payback and lower upfront cost, or both if financially feasible. Doing nothing is also an option if risks outweigh benefits. Utilities are the main cost driver affecting profitability.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze profitability drivers, identify utilities as key cost, perform investment payback calculations, brainstorm cost reduction strategies, and weigh risks and benefits of each option.

title: Great Burger (GB) Acquisition of Heavenly Donuts (HD)
description: Our client is Great Burger (GB), a fast food chain competing with McDonald’s, Wendy’s, Burger King, and KFC. GB is the fourth largest fast food chain worldwide, operating mostly via franchising. GB is considering acquiring Heavenly Donuts (HD), a growing doughnut producer with US and international presence, also franchising but with a different model. GB’s CEO has hired McKinsey to advise whether they should acquire HD.
additional_info: GB operates under a franchising model with 85% of stores owned by franchisees.
HD franchises areas or regions requiring franchisees to open a certain number of stores.
Areas to explore include stand-alone value of HD, growth in doughnut market, HD’s sales growth (store count and same-store sales), and competition.
additional_questions: What areas would you want to explore to determine whether GB should acquire HD?
solution: Candidate should analyze HD’s market growth, sales trends, competitive landscape, franchise model differences, synergies with GB, financial performance, and strategic fit. The recommendation depends on whether HD adds value through growth potential, market expansion, and operational synergies.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure an M&A evaluation, consider market and competitive factors, assess financial and strategic fit, and identify key risks and opportunities.

title: Great Burger: Question 3
description: The team thinks that with synergies, it should be possible to double HD’s US market share in the next 5 years, and that GB’s access to capital will allow it to expand number HD of stores by 2.5 times. What sales/store will HD require in 5 years in order for GB to achieve these goals?
additional_info: Doughnut consumption/capita in the US is $10/year today, and is projected to grow to $20/year in 5 years
For ease of calculation, assume US population is 300 M
Use any data from Exhibit #1 you need
Current HD sales: $700M
US market size today: $3B
HD market share today: 23% (round to 25%)
US market in 5 years projected: $6B
HD sales in 5 years if double market share: $3B
Number of stores in 5 years: 2,500 (current # stores x 2.5)
Sales/store in 5 years = HD sales in 5 years / new number of stores = $1.2M
solution: HD sales in 5 years need to be $3B with 2,500 stores, resulting in sales per store of $1.2M. This is reasonable given it implies less than double same store sales growth and per capita consumption is predicted to double.
evaluation: Candidate should correctly calculate the projected market size, HD sales, number of stores, and sales per store. They should reason about the feasibility of the sales/store figure in context of market growth and store expansion.

title: Great Burger: Question 4
description: One of the synergies that the team thinks might have a big potential is the idea of increasing the businesses’ overall profitability by selling doughnuts in GB stores. How would you assess the profitability impact of this synergy?
additional_info: Calculate incremental revenues by selling doughnuts in GB stores (number of doughnuts per store x price per doughnut x number of GB stores)
Calculate incremental costs by selling doughnuts in GB stores (production costs, incremental employees, training, software changes, marketing, distribution costs if doughnuts not produced in-house)
Calculate incremental investments (need for more space, store layout changes for in-house doughnut production)
Consider cannibalization effects: doughnut cannibalization higher with breakfast products than lunch/dinner products
Cannibalization can be estimated from historic rates within GB stores or impact on HD stores nearby GB stores
solution: Assess profitability by calculating incremental revenues, costs, and investments, while factoring in cannibalization effects. Use historic data to estimate cannibalization rates and consider operational changes needed to produce and sell doughnuts in GB stores.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to identify and quantify revenue, cost, and investment impacts, and incorporate cannibalization effects into profitability assessment.

title: Great Burger: Question 5
description: What would be the incremental profit per store if we think we are going to sell 50 thousand doughnuts per store at a price of $2 per doughnut at a 60% margin with a cannibalization rate of 10% of GB’s sales?
additional_info: Sales and Profitability per store data from Exhibit #2
Cannibalization Rate means percentage of GB sales lost due to introduction of HD doughnuts
Incremental Profit = contribution from HD sales less contribution lost due to cannibalized GB sales
Calculation: 50,000 units x $2/unit x 60% margin – 300,000 units x 10% cannibalization x $3/unit x 50% margin = $60K – $45K = $15K incremental profit/store
solution: Incremental profit per GB store from selling HD doughnuts is $15K after accounting for cannibalization.
evaluation: Candidate should correctly perform the incremental profit calculation including the impact of cannibalization and margins.

title: Great Burger: Question 6 (Synthesis)
description: You run into the CEO of GB in the hall. He asks you to summarize McKinsey’s perspective so far on whether GB should acquire HD. Pretend I am the CEO - What would you say?
additional_info: Early findings suggest acquiring HD would create significant value for GB
Selling HD doughnuts in GB stores could add $15K in profit per GB store, potentially $50 million incremental profit in North America
Other potential revenue and cost synergies need to be quantified
Recommendation on acquisition price will be made after quantifying incremental revenues, cost savings, and investments
Recommendations on integration to capture synergies and manage franchise structures and cultures will be provided
solution: Acquiring HD is likely to create significant value due to incremental profits from selling HD doughnuts in GB stores and other synergies. Further analysis is needed to quantify all synergies and determine acquisition price and integration approach.
evaluation: Candidate should synthesize prior analysis into a clear, concise recommendation, demonstrating understanding of value creation, synergies, and next steps.

title: The Pricing Games
description: Your client is Next Level Gaming (NLG), a start-up in the E-sports and computer gaming industry based in Los Angeles, California. NLG is planning to launch its first game – an online, multiplayer role playing game that is unlike any existing franchise. Being a new player in the industry, NLG’s CEO, Bobby Beck, has asked for your help in deciding its business model. The company is considering 3 alternatives: a subscription model where players pay a monthly fee; a retail model where players pay full price at initial purchase, and a free-to-play model where the game is free to play but charges players for in-game merchandise. How would you advise NLG to proceed?
additional_info: Time frame: NLG is looking to launch immediately.
Competition: The online gaming industry is dominated by 3 major players who control 35%, 25%, and 20% share respectively. There is a long tail of smaller gaming companies.
Target Market: Based on preliminary market research, NLG expects the majority of players to be between the ages of 21 to 40.
Geography: NLG is planning to launch its game in the US only.
Platform: NLG’s game is compatible for both Apple and Windows computers.
R&D Cost: NLG spent 10 million USD to develop its first game (the candidate should recognize this as a sunk cost and not factor it into the decision).
additional_questions: What factors would you consider to estimate the number of players NLG can target with its first game? How large is this player base in the US?
What is NLG’s expected first year profitability with each of the 3 pricing models?
What non-financial factors would you consider in the subscription vs. free-to-play business models?
How does this information impact your outlook on the 3 different business models?
solution: The candidate may recommend either the subscription or the free-to-play business model. A strong candidate will display consistency based on their brainstorm and cite relevant insights to support the recommendation, along with mentioning profit margins. For example, recommending the free-to-play model because it captures the largest player base, positioning NLG strongly against competitors and allowing future expansion. Key risks include generating sufficient paying players and growing revenue per player, mitigated by designing different types of in-game purchases.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate on ability to structure profitability drivers for each business model, perform quantitative profitability calculations accurately, brainstorm qualitative factors in a structured manner, and provide a clear, supported recommendation with risk assessment and mitigation.

title: Fungicide
description: Funguy Corporation is a small producer and wholesaler of agricultural fungicides. After a yearly financial review, they find one of their proprietary products, Formula X, is likely to be operating at a loss. They ask you to quantify the exact loss and find options on how to return Formula X to profitability.
additional_info: Competitors: Large international competitors for all products.
Market Growth: Market is mature and growing at rate of inflation. Demand for Formula X is at a greater rate.
Product Benefits: New product very effective since fungi have not yet developed resistances (primarily used on soybeans and other legumes). Patented, with 10 years left on patent.

Client Characteristics: Primarily producer and wholesaler. Owns a production plant and several distribution centers. Business is conducted with several-year long contracts with both suppliers and buyers.
additional_questions: Quantify our current profitability loss.
Brainstorm on ways to increase profitability.
solution: Formula X operations is currently losing $5000 per batch or $10000 per day. Candidate should analyze revenue and cost drivers including price, volume, fixed and variable costs, and consider legal, patent, competition, market growth, geography, and cannibalization. Recommendations may include pricing adjustments, cost reductions, operational improvements, or strategic changes to return product to profitability.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate on ability to structure profitability framework, perform quantitative analysis accurately, integrate operational and market factors, brainstorm creative and practical solutions, and provide a clear recommendation.

title: Men’s Extra Comfortable Essentials
description: Our client, Men’s Extra Comfortable Essentials, is a US-based manufacturer of basic apparel including socks, tanks, tees, and underwear. Revenues in 2016 were $60M, and the CEO has promised shareholders 4x growth by 2020, at which point, she promised 10.5% profit margin. She has hired us to determine whether these are realistic revenue targets, and if so, how her firm could go about achieving them.
additional_info: The firm purchases the fabrics through contracts with suppliers across the US.
Firm receives uncut fabrics, and must process them into each apparel line in-house.
Firm sells products through traditional channels.
The firm currently only produces products for men.
Current Product Mix and Market Share data by product line (Socks, Tanks, Tees, Underwear) with 2016 and 2020 estimates.
Profitability details including variable and fixed costs.
Market growth rates and margins by product.
Gross margins expected to remain constant through 2020.
additional_questions: Given the data you have, what will be our client’s 2020 revenue by product type?
Given the following gross margin information what is the gross margin for the firm in 2020?
How can the firm reach the targets promised to shareholders?

The numbers make sense, but of the remaining two product lines, only socks market share can remain constant (at 15%). Underwear’s market share must increase from 1% to 10%; what are some ways the firm can attempt to do this?
solution: The candidate should calculate 2020 revenues by product and observe that total revenue exceeds the CEO's target but gross margin is below target. The candidate should recommend dropping the Tanks and Tees product lines to improve margin while still exceeding revenue targets. To increase underwear market share, the candidate should brainstorm strategies including product line expansion, marketing, new distribution channels, and operational improvements.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to perform market sizing and margin calculations, identify unprofitable product lines to cut, and creatively brainstorm growth strategies for underwear. Structured thinking, quantitative accuracy, and business intuition are key.

title: WiFi in the Sky
description: Your firm has won an RFP to help a domestic airline carrier examine their in-flight connectivity (IFC) strategy. With 80% of US-based aircraft already outfitted with IFC technology and competitive pressures rising, offering WiFi service is becoming table-stakes. Your client has yet to enter the game, but they know it’s something they need to consider to stay competitive. What are some of the key things the client should think about when assessing their go-to-market strategy for IFC?
additional_info: The airline flies primarily domestic routes within the continental US, as well as select flights to Canada, Mexico, and the Caribbean.
IFC includes only WiFi connectivity; in-flight entertainment is separate but can be integrated.
The airline has 90 planes and services ~30K flights per year.
IFC is typically installed and managed by an outside vendor, either branded or white-label, with contracts typically 10+ years long.
Different vendors use different technologies with varying quality.
Total bandwidth is shared across passengers; higher usage slows service.
Capex is $250K per plane; airline covers 40%.
Margins are $10 per session.
Seat occupancy assumed 100%.
1 WiFi session covers entire flight.
additional_questions: There is a wide range of potential business models and your client wants to better understand them before making a vendor decision. Your analyst pulled these benchmarks on what two competitors are doing today. What are your thoughts on these two models?

After some initial analysis, the client has decided to partner with a white-label IFC vendor to outfit all 90 of its planes. The airline and vendor will share the initial investment, and then the airline will pay the vendor a per-session fee to cover operating expenses. The airline wants you to figure out what percentage of their passengers need to purchase a session for the airline to break even in 2 years.

Market research tells us that take rates are only between 5 and 10% today for paid connectivity, so your client is concerned about being able to hit the break-even targets. What do you think are the key drivers of IFC take rate?
solution: Recommend partnering with a wholesale, white-label vendor to retain control over pricing and user experience. Roll out gradually, prioritizing newer planes and routes with higher expected take rates. Break-even take rate is approximately 12.9%. Candidate should discuss pros and cons of different business models and brainstorm drivers of take rate.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate understanding of vendor models, perform break-even math accurately, and brainstorm factors influencing customer adoption. Ability to balance operational, financial, and strategic considerations is key.

title: Tres Burritos
description: Your client is a national burrito chain with 100 locations. There has been a widespread flu virus growing in the U.S. which has the potential to hurt their business. They need your help deciding how they should respond to the virus in NYC specifically, and what the implications will be for their brand.
additional_info: There are 10 locations in NYC.
The restaurants sell 3 types of burritos, along with guacamole, salsa, and other sides.
All sales come from grab-and-go takeout orders; no delivery currently.
Main goal is maintaining net positive profit and protecting national brand.
Virus projected to last 3 months.
Governor has mandated restaurants can only offer delivery.
Revenue will drop by 10%, COGS will increase by 10%, delivery setup costs $20,000 one-time.
Monthly income statement and customer location data provided.
additional_questions: What should they consider in responding to the virus?
How would you evaluate the options for responding to the virus?
Governor Andy Hamilton has decided that restaurants cannot stay open as usual – they can only offer delivery. What will the virus's impact be on Tres Burritos' profitability per location?
What are some possible ways to improve EBITDA if they choose to keep restaurants open and offer delivery?
solution: Candidate should evaluate options including staying open, closing, modifying hours/services, and offering delivery. Math shows offering delivery results in a ~60% EBITDA decline but remains positive over 3 months, better than closing. Recommendations include offering delivery, exploring partnerships with third-party delivery apps, and cost-cutting measures.
evaluation: Candidate should structure options with pros and cons, perform profitability math accurately, and brainstorm creative solutions to improve EBITDA. Consideration of brand impact and operational feasibility is important.

title: FlashPro
description: Your client is FlashPro Inc., a San Francisco based technology company that develops and sells wearable and mountable action cameras. FlashPro’s CEO believes that the company’s growth has stagnated and has hired your firm to remedy that issue.
additional_info: The company is public and sells products in the US and internationally.
Main product is CamPro, a mounted camera mainly used in action sports and other recreational activities.
FlashPro contracts manufacturing to China.
Sales channels include Distributors, Retail, and FlashPro’s Website.
Industry is highly competitive with loyal customers.
Camera price is $250; gross margin steady at 50%.
60% of sales come from the US.
Market share and penetration data provided.
Expansion options include new geographies with data on Argentina, Japan, Australia, and Sweden.
additional_questions: What is FlashPro’s expected Gross Profit for 2018?
FlashPro wants to understand what are their revenue growth prospects at this moment (keeping everything constant) and analyze other alternatives that could help boost their top line.
From an economic perspective what is the most attractive country to enter and what things should FlashPro consider before entering?
solution: Candidate should calculate expected gross profit using market share and pricing data. Growth prospects are limited in the US due to market saturation and competition. Recommend geographic expansion, with Argentina as the most attractive market due to higher expected revenue and favorable sales channel distribution. Candidate should consider market entry factors such as customer habits, competition, regulation, and logistics.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate strong quantitative skills, structured growth strategy thinking, and ability to evaluate market entry considerations. Creativity and commercial awareness are important.

title: Uranus Co.
description: Uranus Co., founded in 1969 by Jack Kanof, is an established aerospace company looking to penetrate the luxury space travel market. It is looking for advice on the global launch of its new travel service, which will be a 2-week cruise experience on a spaceship orbiting earth. Uranus is trying to determine whether they should enter the market and if they can be profitable. How would you advise Uranus Co.?
additional_info: Uranus works with governments and private companies to provide aerospace equipment and assets.
Competitors include SpaceX and MoonShine.
Objective is to assess market size, profitability, and breakeven within 2 years.
Global market sizing data provided including millionaire population and survey data.
Market share and competitive landscape data provided.
Operational capacity and financial data including fixed and variable costs, ticket pricing, and contribution margin provided.
Capacity: 150 ships, 2 trips/month, 400 seats per ship.
Ticket price $400k plus $100k other revenue per customer.
Start-up cost $1.5B per ship.
additional_questions: What factors would you consider in estimating the market size for this service?
How much of the market do you think Uranus can penetrate?
Does Uranus Co. have the operational capacity to meet this 20% target? If it runs at capacity, will Uranus Co. be able to breakeven within two years?
solution: Candidate should estimate market size focusing on millionaires eligible and willing to travel, calculate ticket demand, and estimate market share penetration (20%). Operational capacity supports this penetration. Breakeven analysis shows Uranus Co. can break even in less than two years given contribution margin and fixed costs. Recommendation is to enter the luxury space travel market given market size and competitive advantages.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to perform market sizing with relevant filters, estimate market share with justification, conduct breakeven analysis, and synthesize findings into a clear recommendation. Structured thinking and quantitative rigor are critical.

title: Stance at a Distance
description: Our firm has just wrapped up a project with The Stance School, which offers K-12 education in Philadelphia, to help them with developing two major strategic initiatives. The first initiative, which is the priority, is an e-learning platform for their students to facilitate remote learning, in response to a global pandemic that resulted in students having to take classes from home for the last few months of the school year. The other initiative is a professional development tool to help the teachers with developing and effectively providing a remote curriculum to the students. The team that worked on the strategic planning determined that the cost would be $1M. You are now tasked with determining how these initiatives will be funded within the next year and whether or not the school should move forward with this project.
additional_info: Timeframe: currently 2020 H1; initiatives launch start of 2020-2021 school year.
School is private in a major US metropolitan city, with 1000 students primarily middle-upper middle class.
~10% of students on scholarship.
No ongoing costs for the program beyond initial $1M.
Endowment is restricted and not available for funding initiatives.
Pro forma income statement and historical tuition data provided.
Roles, salaries, headcount, and benchmarks against competitor schools provided.
Goal headcount data provided.
solution: Candidate should analyze school’s revenue and expenses, identify internal funding opportunities especially in staff salaries, and quantify potential cost savings. Candidate should assess risks and challenges of cost-cutting and implementation feasibility. Recommendation should consider whether the school can fund the $1M initiative internally and the impact on operations.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to analyze financial statements, identify cost-saving levers, maintain structured thinking, and consider qualitative risks. Creativity and practical judgment are important.

title: Stance at a Distance
description: The Stance School is facing declining enrollment and high tuition compared to competitors. How can the school cut costs without impacting academic performance?
additional_info: Stance has the highest tuition and highest rate of increase (30k, 4%).
Enrollment has declined as tuition has gone up; students may be leaving for competitor schools.
Grants are not allocated to school expenses.
Salary and headcount data for various positions compared to competitor schools.
Benchmark ratios of students to headcount for various positions.
solution: Focus on reducing headcount in non-educator roles where Stance is over-hired relative to competitors, such as custodians, guidance counselors, IT technicians, librarians, and teacher aides, while considering the risks of eliminating these roles and their impact on school performance.
evaluation: Poor candidates pick roles based on savings without rationale; good candidates identify over-hiring in non-educator roles; great candidates also consider risks and ask for data to understand minimum staffing needs.

title: Grad-U-Date
description: Our client is an online dating startup named Grad-U-Date, specializing in matching graduate students. They have finished building their app and want your help deciding on a monetization strategy. What is Grad-U-Date’s best method of monetization? How profitable will the company be?
additional_info: Gender ratio of users is 50/50.
MBA, law, and medical students are considered professional students.
The app is a sunk cost and will operate only in the US.
There are 4 major competitors and hundreds of small competitors.
No other online dating services focus only on graduate students.
Only one revenue model can be used.
Fixed costs are $10 million/year; variable cost is $1 per member per year.
Market size is approximately 6 million graduate students.
Revenue models include monthly fee, premium account, and advertising.
Historical data shows a decline in users paying monthly fees starting in 2013.
additional_questions: Does the historical market data change your recommendation for which revenue model?
Why do you think the percentage of users paying monthly fees dropped quickly starting in 2013?
Given your recommended revenue model, what other strategies can Grad-U-Date use to increase profit?
solution: Recommend the premium account revenue model, expected to bring $20.6 million in annual profit. Monthly fee model is declining and limits user base. Strategies to increase profit include reducing costs (outsourcing servers, limiting support) and increasing revenue (profile consulting, micro-transactions, expanding market).
evaluation: Strong candidates brainstorm multiple monetization models, perform accurate profit calculations, and address follow-up questions with structured reasoning and creative strategies.

title: GGC Health
description: GGC Health operates eight Ambulatory Surgical Centers (ASCs) with flat revenues of $400 million/year. The CEO wants to increase revenues by 15%. How should GGC Health achieve this revenue growth?
additional_info: ASCs provide same-day surgical care and are alternatives to hospital outpatient procedures.
Physicians dictate where surgeries are performed.
Target revenue increase is $60 million (15% of $400 million).
Revenue = number of procedures * reimbursement per procedure.
Three potential expansion cities: Charlotte (30 doctors), Indianapolis (25 doctors), Nashville (16 doctors).
Physician specialty breakdowns and reimbursement rates provided.
Orthopedic: 15 patients/doctor/month, $5K reimbursement per patient.
Neurology: 10 patients/doctor/month, $25K reimbursement per patient.
Gastroenterology: 12 patients/doctor/month, $15K reimbursement per patient.
Opportunity to add 2 urology doctors per existing ASC site (8 sites), each bringing 8 patients/month at $8K reimbursement.
additional_questions: Which city should GGC Health enter for expansion?
Calculate the revenue potential for each city.
How can GGC Health increase revenues at existing ASC locations?
Calculate additional revenue from adding urology doctors.
solution: Recommend opening a new ASC in Indianapolis ($49.8M revenue) and adding urology surgeries at existing ASCs ($12M revenue) to meet the $60M revenue target. Nashville is eliminated early due to fewer doctors. Additional revenue growth ideas include increasing patients per doctor, adding new procedures, and prioritizing high reimbursement surgeries.
evaluation: Good candidates quickly eliminate weak options, perform detailed revenue calculations, brainstorm multiple revenue growth strategies, and integrate quantitative and qualitative insights.

title: Nook Co.
description: Your client Fun Ventures, a PE firm, is considering acquiring Nook Co., a hospitality group developing premium private island vacation destinations. Nook Co. proposed an initial offer of $1.5 billion. Should Fun Ventures proceed with the acquisition?
additional_info: Nook Co. owns islands, constructs resorts, operates activities and transportation.
Operates 10 islands in East Asia, 5 more under construction.
Fun Ventures targets a 15% ROI.
World population is 8 billion; top 0.05% have income $100K+.
Target customers visit once every 2 years; 2 guests per room; $10,000 per night room rate; average stay 5 nights.
Nook Co. market share is 28% of $1B market = $280M revenue.
Costs include labor, food, transport, fixed costs; total costs $196M; profit $84M; profit margin 30%.
Discount rate 5%; NPV = $1.68B; ROI = 12%, slightly below target.
Potential to cut $5M administrative costs to exceed 15% ROI.
Risks include unsustainable growth, customer churn, generic service, financial manipulation, global warming, regulatory issues.
additional_questions: What is the potential market size for premium island vacations?
What is Nook Co.'s profit in 2019?
Calculate the ROI on the acquisition.
What other risks should be considered?
solution: Nook Co. has a $1B market with 28% share, generating $280M revenue and $84M profit. ROI is 12%, below the 15% target, but cost reductions could improve ROI. Risks include internal and external factors. Recommendation depends on risk tolerance and potential for operational improvements.
evaluation: Strong candidates calculate market size, profit, and ROI accurately, identify risks thoughtfully, and provide a clear recommendation with supporting evidence.

title: Chococo
description: Chococo, a premium chocolate company based in Peru, wants to expand into the US market. What should Chococo consider before launching this expansion?
additional_info: Chococo currently operates only in Peru and sells chocolate in stores.
Chococo made $100M revenue last year.
Premium chocolate industry in US has grown >10% annually.
US population 320M, 50/50 gender split.
30% of men and 60% of women purchase chocolate; 20% of men and 33% of women purchase premium chocolate.
Average bars purchased per year: men 5, women 10; price per bar $10.
Chococo is comfortable moving forward if it can reach $100M revenue by year 2.
Risks include competition, saturated market, brand awareness, FDA approval, production capacity, relationships, financial issues.
Potential entry channels: online, retail stores, coffee shops, branded stores, partnerships, subscription services.
additional_questions: What is the size of the premium chocolate industry in the US?
What are some risks associated with entering the US market?
Through what channels should Chococo enter the US market?
solution: The US premium chocolate market is approximately $4 billion. Chococo would need to capture about 3% market share to reach $100M revenue, which is feasible. Risks include competition and regulatory hurdles. Recommended entry channels include online sales and retail stores, prioritized by ease of implementation, financial return, and consumer reach.
evaluation: Good candidates estimate market size accurately, identify and prioritize risks with mitigation strategies, and propose a ranked list of entry channels with rationale.

title: Royal Cinema
description: Royal Cinemas, a NYC theater chain, has declining ticket sales. They are considering converting their theaters into luxury cinemas. Should they proceed?
additional_info: Currently 10 locations in NYC, 10 screens per theater, 150 seats per screen.
Ticket price and concessions currently $20 total.
Luxury conversion reduces seats to 65 per screen but increases ticket and concession price to $50 total.
Showings per week: 20 peak, 40 off-peak.
Capacity utilization: standard 80% peak, 20% off-peak; luxury 90% peak, 30% off-peak.
Conversion/renovation cost $100M; projected luxury weekly revenue $9.75M; variable costs 50% of revenue.
Payback period estimated at ~5 months.
Competition: fewer than 5 major players in NYC luxury cinema market.
Luxury cinema market is growing.
Potential differentiation strategies include premium concessions, environment enhancements, added content, partnerships.
additional_questions: How does the financial exhibit influence your opinion about entering the luxury cinema market?
How long will it take to recoup conversion costs?
How might Royal Cinemas differentiate itself in the luxury cinema space?
solution: Royal Cinemas should proceed with conversion due to higher revenue potential and market growth. Payback period is short (~5 months). Differentiation can be achieved through premium concessions, enhanced environment, unique content, and partnerships.
evaluation: Good candidates perform accurate revenue and payback calculations, assess market attractiveness, and provide structured, creative differentiation ideas.

title: Adventure Capital
description: Idaho Johnson, an archeologist, is considering launching an expedition to recover a valuable 6,000-year-old crown in Egypt. Should he proceed given costs, risks, and potential returns?
additional_info: Idaho must self-finance the dig but has a buyer lined up pending recovery.
Dig estimated to take 12 months.
Idaho wants an expected ROI of 85%.
Chance of success is 20%.
Costs include one-time (map, travel, permits, equipment), recurring (labor, lodging, food), and opportunity costs (teaching salary).
Total estimated cost is $500K.
Two buyers: private collector offers $4M one-time; museum offers percentage of ticket sales (900K attendance/year, 50% buy tickets at $10 each, % owed to Idaho unknown).
additional_questions: What costs are required to embark on the expedition?
Calculate the total cost of the expedition.
How would you calculate the value of the museum’s offer and which offer is better?
solution: Total expedition cost is $500K. Expected value of private collector offer is $4M. Museum offer depends on percentage of ticket sales owed; calculation involves estimating annual revenue from tickets and discounting. Candidate should weigh costs, success probability, and ROI target to recommend proceeding or not.
evaluation: Good candidates identify and bucket costs, perform cost calculations, analyze offers quantitatively, and incorporate risk and ROI considerations into decision.

title: Steel Co.
description: Your client is Steel Co., an international steel manufacturer operating in 100+ countries. The company wants to identify cost saving opportunities, focusing specifically on transportation costs across four transport methods: rail, truck, barge, and vessel. How can Steel Co. optimize its transportation to reduce costs?
additional_info: Steel is only manufactured as coils.
Customers are predominantly large B2B manufacturers with both short and long-term contracts.
Utilization refers to the % of vehicle capacity used for steel coils; remaining space is empty.
Deliveries per week, volume per delivery, utilization %, cost per ton, and frequency differ by transport method.
Total cost saving potential of $7.3M through better optimization of capacity.
Vessels currently operate at 100% utilization; changing KPIs to 95% could reduce leftover inventory at the pier.
Better inventory management could save an additional $540K by reducing truck deliveries caused by vessel capacity constraints.
Truck drivers may be unionized; reducing trucks may require contract renegotiation.
solution: Drive cost savings of approximately $7.84M, with $7.3M savings through better capacity utilization and $540K from improved inventory management for vessels. Recommendations include modifying vessel labor KPIs to ensure 95% utilization instead of 100%, gradually reducing the number of trucks used to improve utilization, and renegotiating cost per ton if possible.
evaluation: Candidate should incorporate steel industry specifics, focus on cost savings primarily through transportation optimization, evaluate all four transport methods, and creatively propose inventory management improvements. Strong candidates correlate exhibits and demonstrate operational knowledge.

title: All Night Long
description: Your client, CNB Movies, a national movie theater company, is considering offering 24-hour opening weekend screenings for Marvel and DC Comics movies to capitalize on record high demand. Should CNB Movies implement this 24-hour experience?
additional_info: Opening weekend runs from Thursday night to Sunday 11:59 PM.
CNB wants to test one theater first; if successful, roll out to all 400 theaters in the US.
Ticket prices are $15 for adults and $10 for children/seniors.
No competitors currently offer this experience.
CNB can finance this on its own.
Expected seat utilization varies by time and demographic; each theater has 300 seats.
Average concession revenue is $10 per person.
Staff costs will increase 50%, concession costs 50%, concession maintenance 25%, projector maintenance 10% under the new model.
Current daily costs per theater are provided.
Incremental profits for one theater are projected at $10,800 for the weekend.
additional_questions: What are some additional factors that our client should consider before making their final decision?
solution: CNB Movies should implement the 24-hour experience. The expected incremental profits are $10,800 for one theater over the weekend, representing a 22% profit margin. This model also provides a competitive advantage for high-grossing movies.
evaluation: Candidate should structure revenue and cost analysis including ticket sales, concessions, staff, maintenance, and utilities. Strong candidates consider financial risks, competitive response, premium pricing potential, and alternative project options. Creativity in brainstorming additional factors is valued.

title: Is Teleconferencing a Good Call?
description: Your client is a national wealth management firm considering whether to roll out a cutting-edge virtual conferencing software across all locations to reduce Advisor travel costs. How should the client proceed?
additional_info: The pilot was implemented 6 months ago in the NYC office.
Advisors currently travel extensively to satellite offices in the Midwest and southern US.
Virtual conferencing includes instant messaging, video chat, screen sharing, conference calling, and call forwarding.
Goal is to reduce costs; revenue growth is out of scope.
Travel costs per city and number of advisors are provided.
Teleconferencing is expected to reduce travel expenses by 10%.
Two implementation options: Outside Vendor and Build In-House, with similar payback periods.
Qualitative factors include client experience impact, compliance, cybersecurity, training, and advisor relationships.
additional_questions: What would you include in your data request to address this problem?
How much is spent on travel per year? What insights can you draw?
What is the annual cost of each teleconferencing solution? How long to break-even? How much would each save annually?
What other factors should we consider before making a decision?
solution: Both teleconferencing options have similar payback periods (~2-3 months). The choice depends on qualitative factors: Outside Vendor requires less internal manpower but has higher subscription costs; Build In-House offers customization but requires more maintenance. The client should conduct employee and customer studies, assess cybersecurity risks, and consider impacts on advisor-client relationships before deciding.
evaluation: Candidate should creatively request relevant data, perform cost-benefit analysis, calculate payback periods, and brainstorm qualitative factors. Strong candidates defend a recommendation considering both financial and non-financial aspects.

title: Apple of My Eye
description: Bob, a master brewer with a successful Belgian Witbier brewery, is considering expanding into cider production to meet increasing customer demand for gluten-free alternatives. Should Bob renovate his brewery to produce cider?
additional_info: Currently, Bob sells bottled beer direct to consumers only.
Renovation would reduce beer production from 30 to 24 cases/day and add 12 cases/day of cider production.
Selling price: $5.00 per beer bottle, $6.00 per cider bottle.
30 bottles per case; production runs Monday-Friday, 20 days per month.
Bob needs to capture at least 0.5% of the NYC take-home cider market to break even.
15.5 million bottles of cider expected to be sold in NYC next year.
additional_questions: What will be the change in Bob’s annual revenue if he begins to produce cider?
Besides potential revenue, what other factors should Bob consider before expanding into the cider market?
Will Bob be able to produce enough cider to capture the 0.5% market share?
solution: Bob should invest in cider production. He will increase annual revenues by approximately $302,400 and can produce enough cider to capture the 0.5% NYC market share needed to break even. However, further market research and competitive analysis are recommended to validate demand and pricing.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate revenue changes accurately, consider costs, market size, cannibalization, and go-to-market strategy. Strong candidates question assumptions and consider operational capabilities and market dynamics.

title: Jimmy’s Dilemma
description: Jimmy Smith, an MBA2, has two job offers from competing firms: Firm A (second-tier) and Firm B (top-tier). He wants to maximize financial value over 3 years. Which offer should Jimmy accept?
additional_info: Jimmy only cares about money and plans to work 3 years at either firm.
Firm A offers lower starting salary but includes tuition reimbursement at end of year 3.
Firm B offers higher starting salary and signing bonus but no tuition reimbursement.
Annual bonuses are expected to remain constant.
Exit opportunities after 3 years differ, with Firm B offering higher expected starting salaries.
Discount rate for NPV calculations is 14%.
Exit opportunity salary distributions and probabilities are provided.
additional_questions: What other things should Jimmy consider before making a decision?
What do you make of the exit opportunities data Jimmy collected?
solution: Although Firm A has a higher 3-year NPV ($440k vs $430k), the higher expected value of exit opportunities at Firm B (NPV of $220k vs $185k) makes Firm B the better choice overall. Jimmy should choose Firm B.
evaluation: Candidate should perform DCF/NPV calculations, consider exit opportunities, and brainstorm financial and non-financial factors such as firm culture, location, and career growth. Strong candidates integrate quantitative and qualitative analysis.

title: Apartment Co.
description: Apartment Co., a residential real estate company managing 10 buildings in NYC, has invested $5M in upgrades but has not seen expected profitability increases. How can Apartment Co. achieve higher long-term profitability?
additional_info: Revenue source is tenant rent only; 50 units per building.
5 buildings in Manhattan, 3 in Brooklyn, 2 in Queens.
Company contracts 30 maintenance staff; no recent portfolio additions.
Tenants mostly young working professionals.
Management decided in 2017 to pay utilities as part of rent to stay competitive.
Two main utility cost reduction investment options: energy efficient lighting and solar panels.
Energy efficient lighting: $1.5M cost, $750K annual savings, 2-year payback, 10-year bulb lifespan.
Solar panels: $3.2M cost, $960K annual savings, ~3-year payback, 20-year lifespan.
Financial exhibits include revenues, costs, electricity rates, utility spend, salaries, and resident income levels.
additional_questions: What are some ways Apartment Co. can reduce their utilities expense?
Evaluate the two investment options: energy efficient lighting and solar panels. Which should Apartment Co. pursue?
solution: Apartment Co. should consider installing solar panels for greater long-term savings and tax benefits, energy efficient lighting for quicker payback, or both if financially feasible. Doing nothing is also an option if risks outweigh benefits. Recommendations should be supported by data and consider risks such as volatile energy prices, upfront costs, and tenant behavior.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze profitability drivers, focus on utilities cost reduction, perform investment payback calculations, and creatively brainstorm solutions. Strong candidates assess risks and next steps comprehensively.

title: Great Burger
description: Great Burger (GB), the fourth largest fast food chain, is considering acquiring Heavenly Donuts (HD), a growing doughnut producer with US and international presence. Should GB acquire HD?
additional_info: HD operates under a franchising model different from GB's; HD franchises areas requiring franchisees to open a set number of stores.
Areas to explore include HD's standalone value (market growth, sales growth, competition, profitability, capital needs), management team and cultural fit, and synergies/strategic fit.

Potential synergies include cost savings in corporate SG&A, food cost reductions, leveraging GB's property and equipment cost advantages, cross-selling products, international expansion, better site selection, and faster HD store expansion.
GB aims to double HD’s US market share in 5 years and expand HD stores by 2.5 times.
US doughnut consumption per capita is $10/year today, projected to grow to $20/year in 5 years; US population assumed 300M.
additional_questions: What areas would you want to explore to determine whether GB should acquire HD?
What potential synergies can you think of between GB and HD?
What sales per store will HD require in 5 years to achieve the growth goals?
solution: Candidate should evaluate HD’s standalone value, management and cultural fit, and potential synergies. To meet growth goals, HD must increase sales/store significantly, leveraging GB’s strengths in capital access, international presence, and site selection. The acquisition should be assessed on strategic fit and financial impact.
evaluation: Candidate should structure the analysis across standalone value, synergies, and strategic fit, perform quantitative calculations on market size and sales targets, and consider franchising model differences. Strong candidates balance qualitative and quantitative insights.

title: Great Burger: Incremental Profit per Store
description: What would be the incremental profit per store if we think we are going to sell 50 thousand doughnuts per store at a price of $2 per doughnut at a 60% margin with a cannibalization rate of 10% of GB’s sales?
additional_info: Incremental Profit = contribution from HD sales less contribution lost due to cannibalized GB sales
Calculation: 50K units x $2/unit x 60% margin – 300K units x 10% cannibalization x $3/unit x 50% margin = $60K – 45K = $5K incremental profit/store
Cannibalization Rate means the percentage of GB’s sales that are lost due to the introduction of HD doughnuts
solution: Incremental profit per store is $5,000 based on the given sales, price, margin, and cannibalization rate.
evaluation: Candidate should correctly calculate incremental profit considering both incremental sales and cannibalization effect.

title: Great Burger: Acquisition Recommendation Synthesis
description: You run into the CEO of GB in the hall. He asks you to summarize McKinsey’s perspective so far on whether GB should acquire HD. Pretend I am the CEO - What would you say?
additional_info: Early findings suggest acquiring HD would create significant value for GB.
Incremental profit per GB store from selling HD doughnuts is estimated at $15K.
Potential revenue and cost synergies exist but need further quantification.
Recommendation on acquisition price and integration plan will follow after full analysis.
solution: Recommend acquisition as it could add significant incremental profit and synergies; further analysis needed for price and integration.
evaluation: Candidate should synthesize quantitative and qualitative findings into a clear, concise recommendation.

title: Drinks Gone Flat
description: The beverage department of a regional supermarket has seen a drop in revenue over the last year and has hired your firm to determine the cause of the decline and recommend ways to reverse the trend.
additional_info: Client is a leading regional grocer within the southeastern U.S.
Competitive landscape has not changed in the last year.
Revenue decline is specifically within the beverage segment.
Revenue and sales numbers segmented by beverage product category are available on request (Exhibit 1).
Further segmented soda revenue by brand is available on request (Exhibit 2).
additional_questions: What is causing the decline in beverage revenue?
Which beverage categories are most affected?
What are the revenue trends by soda brand?
What recommendations do you have to reverse the decline in beverage revenue?
solution: Decline is driven by soda revenue, specifically Brand A's volume and revenue decline, offset partially by Value Brand volume increase but at lower prices. Recommendations include adjusting Value Brand pricing, improving differentiation between brands, exploring bundling, marketing, and shelf placement strategies.
evaluation: Candidate should identify root cause of revenue decline, segment data effectively, and provide structured, creative recommendations.

title: Toto Foundation
description: Our client, the Toto Foundation, a non-profit arm of a drone manufacturing company, is exploring aid projects in Africa. Determine where and how to deploy their drone technology to have the maximum impact.
additional_info: Parent company manufactures and sells drones with commercial experience in the US.
Foundation grant: $4 million upfront, $1 million per year thereafter.
Project goals: maximize impact on human lives within budget, safety is primary concern, no additional funding, projects only in Africa.
Potential projects include blood & plasma delivery, crop monitoring, vaccine & medicinal delivery, dry food delivery, poacher monitoring, insulin delivery.
Upfront costs and expertise vary by project.
Country selection factors include population, density, vaccination rates, poverty, danger to non-profits, road quality, ease of doing business.
Annual costs and impact vary by country.
additional_questions: Which project should the foundation pursue?
What factors should be considered when choosing target countries?
Which countries should be selected for launch?
Calculate annual costs and impact for target countries.
What is your recommendation based on cost and impact analysis?
solution: Recommend pursuing vaccine & general medicinal delivery due to high need, impact, and manageable upfront cost. Select Senegal and Guinea as target countries based on need and safety. Senegal is preferred due to lower variable costs and higher impact (more vials delivered per year). Launch vaccination delivery service in Senegal with pilot program and regulatory approvals as next steps.
evaluation: Candidate should use elimination based on cost and expertise, consider non-profit priorities, analyze country data for impact and risk, perform cost-impact math, and provide a clear recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: Cups
description: Your client, Proctor & Gamble, has developed a hypoallergenic and eco-friendly silicone material and is considering launching menstrual cups. Should they sell the silicone menstrual cups?
additional_info: P&G will manufacture and sell the product if at least 10% of customers willing to purchase menstrual cups are willing to pay for it and cannibalization does not negatively impact sales.
Competitors include Diva Cup, Softcup, Blossom, Saalt, and Lena.
Product has a 5-year lifespan before recycling.
R&D investment costs $12M with a 10% compound discount rate over 5 years.
Market sizing data: US population 330M, female 165M, menstruation ages 10-50, 10% willingness to use initially, comparable product price $40.
Survey data shows 40% undecided; P&G believes 25% of undecided can be converted.
Cannibalization of existing products and complementary product sales must be considered.
Survey response data and pricing willingness available.
Marketing should leverage sustainability trends.
additional_questions: What is the market size for menstrual cups in the US?
How does survey data affect market size assumptions?
How should P&G price the menstrual cup to offset cannibalization?
Is the product launch feasible given pricing and survey data?
What marketing strategies would you recommend to promote the menstrual cup and other products?
solution: Market size estimated at $33M to $66M first year depending on capture and willingness to use. Adjusted market size with survey data increases potential revenue. Breakeven price per cup calculated between $25-$30 to offset cannibalization and R&D costs. Survey data supports launching the product. Marketing should emphasize sustainability, ease of use, lifetime savings, and use 4Ps framework for both cup and non-cup products.
evaluation: Candidate should perform accurate market sizing, incorporate survey insights, calculate pricing to offset cannibalization, assess launch feasibility, and propose structured marketing recommendations aligned with sustainability trends.

title: Chococo
description: Chococo is a premium chocolate company based in Lima, Peru. The company has been operating profitably in its first few years of business and is now looking to expand into the United States. What should Chococo consider before launching this expansion?
additional_info: Time frame: Chococo is looking to enter the US immediately.
Operations: Currently, Chococo only operates in Peru and only sells chocolate in stores.
Pricing: Chococo sells their bars for the same price as competitors.
Financials: Chococo made $100M in revenue this past year.
Market growth: Premium chocolate industry has seen strong growth (>10%) for each of the past few years.
US Total Population = 320M, split 50/50 male/female.
30% of males purchase chocolate, 60% of females purchase chocolate.
20% of males purchase premium chocolate, 33% of females purchase premium chocolate.
Average number of bars purchased per year: 5 for males, 10 for females.
Average price per chocolate bar: $10.
Premium chocolate market size estimated around $4B.
Chococo is comfortable moving forward if they reach $100M revenue by year 2 (~3% market share).
additional_questions: What is the size of the premium chocolate industry in the United States?
What are some risks associated with entering the US market?
Through what channels should Chococo enter the US market?
solution: The candidate should estimate the US premium chocolate market size (~$4B) using population and purchasing assumptions. They should assess if capturing ~3% market share to reach $100M revenue by year 2 is feasible. Risks include competition, saturated market, brand awareness challenges, FDA approval, production capacity, relationships, financial risks, and currency issues. Channels to enter include online (own website, Amazon), retail stores, coffee shops, branded stores, partnerships, and subscription services. Candidate should prioritize channels based on ease of implementation, financial return, and consumer reach, and provide a recommendation possibly with a timeline.
evaluation: Candidate is evaluated on ability to structure market sizing with clear assumptions, identify and prioritize risks with mitigation strategies, and generate a comprehensive, prioritized list of entry channels with rationale and recommendation.

title: The Pricing Games
description: Your client is Next Level Gaming (NLG), a start-up in the E-sports and computer gaming industry based in Los Angeles, California. NLG is planning to launch its first game – an online, multiplayer role playing game that is unlike any existing franchise. Being a new player in the industry, NLG’s CEO, Bobby Beck, has asked for your help in deciding its business model. The company is considering 3 alternatives: a subscription model where players pay a monthly fee; a retail model where players pay full price at initial purchase, and a free-to-play model where the game is free to play but charges players for in-game merchandise. How would you advise NLG to proceed?
additional_info: Time frame: NLG is looking to launch immediately.
Competition: The online gaming industry is dominated by 3 major players with 35%, 25%, and 20% market share respectively; many smaller companies exist.
Target Market: Majority of players are aged 21 to 40.
Geography: Launch planned in the US only.
Platform: Compatible with Apple and Windows computers.
R&D Cost: $10 million spent on game development (sunk cost).
Market size estimate: ~10 million players in the US.
Profitability calculations for each model are based on player acquisition rates, revenue per player, fixed and variable costs.
Subscription model: 10% of 10M players pay $10/month.
Retail model: 12% of 10M players pay $60 upfront.
Free-to-play model: 50% of 10M players play, 30% of those pay $100 for in-game merchandise.
Profit margins: Subscription and free-to-play models yield ~$20M profit, retail ~$9M.
additional_questions: What factors would you consider to estimate the number of players NLG can target with its first game? How large is this player base in the US?
What is NLG’s expected first year profitability with each of the 3 pricing models?
What non-financial factors would you consider in the subscription vs. free-to-play business models?
solution: Candidate should estimate the addressable market size (~10M players) using demographic and gaming participation assumptions. They should calculate first year profitability for each business model using provided data, noting subscription and free-to-play models yield similar profits (~$20M) and retail less (~$9M). Qualitative factors include competitive response, player base stickiness, growth potential via new platforms or geographies, merchandising opportunities, customer acquisition and retention costs, and infrastructure costs. Candidate should recommend the optimal business model balancing financial and qualitative factors.
evaluation: Candidate is evaluated on ability to structure market sizing with clear assumptions, perform accurate profitability calculations, and brainstorm qualitative factors in an organized, MECE manner leading to a justified recommendation.

title: Stance at a Distance
description: The Stance School is facing funding challenges and needs to determine how to meet a $1M funding goal. Analyze the school's expenses and enrollment data to identify cost-cutting opportunities and recommend how to close the funding gap.
additional_info: The school cannot raise tuition without impacting enrollment.
Stance is not eligible for additional grants.
All expenses are a proportion of Tuition & Fees.
Salary expenses are out of line with competitor schools and are the highest line item.
Scholarships are the second largest expense; reducing scholarships will reduce grant funding significantly.
Number of students: 1000
Funding goal: $1M
Benchmark ratios of students to headcount for various positions are provided.
Eliminating 5 specific roles would save $910K annually, short of the $1M goal by $90K.
Potential risks and costs of eliminating roles include severance, pension, union negotiations, negative PR, enrollment decline, and mental health impacts.
Alternative ways to close the funding gap include lobbying for appropriations, licensing platforms, offering paid remote tutoring, soliciting donations, and targeting high net worth alumni.
additional_questions: What are the potential risks and associated costs when eliminating these roles?
What are some ways the school could close the funding gap to reach the $1M goal?
solution: The best approach is to eliminate the 5 roles to save $910K and find alternative funding sources to cover the remaining $90K. The candidate should recognize that cost-cutting is faster than revenue increases given enrollment sensitivity to tuition. Risks include negative PR, enrollment decline, and potential negative impacts on school performance. Next steps include ensuring no enrollment impact, contract reviews, and exploring additional revenue sources.
evaluation: Poor candidates compare expenses in absolute terms without insight. Good candidates focus on cost-cutting, especially salaries, and calculate savings. Great candidates ask for detailed data (e.g., salaries by function, headcount growth), consider risks creatively, and brainstorm multiple funding alternatives.

title: Drinks Gone Flat
description: A regional supermarket's beverage department has seen a drop in revenue over the last year. Determine the cause of the decline and recommend ways to reverse the trend.
additional_info: Client is a leading regional grocer in the southeastern U.S.
Competitive landscape has not changed in the last year.
Revenue decline is specifically within the beverage segment.
Revenue decline is only from sodas; waters and other beverages have stable or increasing revenue.
Within sodas, Brand A's revenue and volume have declined, while the Value Brand's volume increased but price decreased.
Brand A and Brand B prices remained at $1; Value Brand price dropped from $0.60 to $0.40.
additional_questions: What are your recommendations to reverse the decline in beverage revenue?
solution: Adjust the price of the Value Brand to stop revenue decline and improve differentiation between Brand A and the Value Brand. Consider bundling, marketing, and shelf placement strategies. Risks include alienating value brand customers and suppliers of other brands.
evaluation: Poor candidates fail to segment revenue decline or focus on costs. Good candidates identify sodas as the problem and segment by brand. Great candidates calculate price changes, identify Value Brand as the cause, and provide structured, creative recommendations using the 4P’s framework.

title: Apple of My Eye
description: Bob, a master brewer, is considering expanding his brewery to produce cider due to increasing customer demand for gluten-free alternatives. Analyze the options and advise whether he should renovate his brewery to produce cider.
additional_info: Current beer production: 30 cases/day; after renovation: 24 cases beer + 12 cases cider per day.
Selling price: Beer $5.00/bottle, Cider $6.00/bottle.
30 bottles per case; production runs Monday-Friday, 20 days per month.
Bob wants to capture at least 0.5% of the NYC take-home cider market to break even.
15.5 million bottles of cider expected to be sold in NYC next year.
additional_questions: What are your thoughts on the two renovation options?
What will be the change in Bob’s annual revenue if he begins to produce cider?
Besides potential revenue, what other factors should Bob consider before expanding into cider?
Will Bob be able to produce enough cider to capture 0.5% of the NYC market?
solution: Bob should invest in cider production as it increases annual revenue by approximately $302,400 and he can produce enough to meet the 0.5% market share break-even point. However, he should consider market demand, costs (labor, equipment, marketing), cannibalization of beer sales, competition, and distribution channels before proceeding.
evaluation: Poor candidates focus only on revenue without considering costs or market demand. Good candidates calculate revenue impact and consider operational factors. Great candidates integrate market sizing, breakeven analysis, and strategic considerations such as cannibalization and competitive landscape.

title: Tres Burritos
description: A national burrito chain with 100 locations faces a widespread flu virus in NYC. They need help deciding how to respond to the virus in NYC and understand the implications for profitability and brand.
additional_info: There are 10 locations in NYC.
The virus is projected to last 3 months.
All sales are grab-and-go takeout; no delivery currently.
Options include staying open, closing, modifying hours/services, offering delivery or curbside pickup, partial closures, or closing some stores.
Revenue will drop by 10%, COGS will increase by 10%, launching delivery costs $20,000 one-time.
Monthly revenue per restaurant is $100,000; expenses include COGS $10,000, wages $35,000, SG&A $5,000, fixed overhead $20,000.
additional_questions: What should they consider in responding to the virus?
How would you evaluate the options for responding to the virus?
What will be the virus's impact on profitability per location if only delivery is allowed?
What are some possible ways to improve EBITDA if they keep restaurants open and offer delivery?
solution: Offering delivery results in a better profitability outcome than closing stores, with positive EBITDA over 3 months despite an initial loss in month 1. The client should offer delivery, explore partnerships with third-party delivery services to reduce costs, consider raising prices, reduce staff or menu options, and possibly sell groceries or partner with other restaurants to drive traffic.
evaluation: Poor candidates consider only open vs. close. Good candidates identify multiple options and evaluate profitability. Great candidates calculate month-by-month EBITDA, consider external factors, and brainstorm creative solutions to improve profitability.

title: Men’s Extra Comfortable Essentials
description: A US-based basic apparel manufacturer aims to grow revenues 4x by 2020 and achieve a 10.5% profit margin. Assess whether these targets are realistic and how to achieve them.
additional_info: 2016 revenues: $60M; CEO promises $240M by 2020 with 10.5% margin.
Product lines: Socks (40% revenue, 15% margin), Tanks (25%, -5%), Tees (15%, 0%), Underwear (20%, 10%).
Market shares and growth rates vary by product line.
Firm purchases uncut fabrics and processes in-house.
Firm sells only men's products through traditional channels.
Gross margins expected to remain constant through 2020.
additional_questions: What will be the client’s 2020 revenue by product type?
What is the gross margin for the firm in 2020?
How can the firm reach the targets promised to shareholders?
