What are some ways to increase market share of the Underwear product line?
solution: The client can exceed the revenue target ($362.5M vs $240M) but will not meet the margin target (7.1% vs 10.5%) unless it cuts unprofitable product lines. Dropping Tanks and Tees increases margin to 10.54% while maintaining revenue above target. To grow underwear market share, the firm should consider luxury lines, women's products, marketing, new distribution channels, e-commerce, and operational improvements.
evaluation: Poor candidates fail to integrate market growth and margin data. Good candidates calculate revenues and margins accurately. Great candidates identify unprofitable lines to cut, brainstorm growth strategies for underwear, and consider operational and marketing levers.

title: Adventure Capital
description: Idaho Johnson, an archeologist, is considering launching a self-financed expedition to recover a valuable 6,000-year-old crown in Egypt. Determine whether he should proceed based on costs, expected ROI, and risks.
additional_info: Expedition duration: 12 months.
Chance of success: 20%.
Required ROI: 85%.
Costs include one-time (map, equipment, legal, travel) and recurring (labor, lodging, food), plus opportunity cost (teaching salary).
Two buyers: private collector offers $4M one-time; museum offers 10% of exhibit ticket sales revenue.
Museum attendance: 900,000/year; 50% buy tickets at $10; 10% owed to explorer; discount rate 15%, growth rate 5%.
Total expedition cost: $500K.
Expected revenue from museum deal PV: $4.5M.
Expected revenue from success weighted by chance: $900K.
Profit margin on investment: 80%, slightly below required 85%.
additional_questions: What costs are required for the expedition?
Calculate the total cost of the expedition.
How to value the museum's offer and which offer is better?
What is the expected ROI given success probability?
solution: The expected ROI is 80%, slightly below the required 85%, but close enough to consider going forward. Additional artifacts or synergies could improve returns. Risks include low success probability, personal financial risk, and uncertainties in museum revenue. Idaho's passion for adventure and potential fame also support proceeding.
evaluation: Poor candidates overlook probability and opportunity cost. Good candidates calculate costs and expected values. Great candidates incorporate discounting, growth rates, risk factors, and qualitative considerations in their recommendation.

title: All Night Long
description: CNB Movies, a national movie theater chain, is considering offering 24-hour opening weekend screenings for Marvel and DC movies. Should they implement this experience?
additional_info: Opening weekend runs Thursday night to Sunday 11:59 PM.
Test one theater first; if successful, roll out to 400 theaters.
Ticket prices: $15 adults, $10 children/seniors.
No competitors currently offer this experience.
Expected seat utilization varies by time and customer type.
Average concession revenue is $10 per person.
Daily costs include staff, concessions, maintenance, and projector maintenance.
Incremental profits calculated as total revenues minus total costs.
solution: The 24-hour model yields an incremental profit of $10,800 per theater over the weekend. The candidate should consider increased staffing, maintenance costs, and concession sales. The recommendation is to pilot the 24-hour experience given positive profitability and no current competition.
evaluation: Poor candidates miss key revenue or cost components. Good candidates calculate revenues and costs accurately. Great candidates consider operational risks, competitive response, premium pricing, and alternative options.

title: All Night Long
description: What are some additional factors that our client should consider before making their final decision?
additional_info: Potential to cannibalize our current customer base
Upfront marketing costs
Long-term marketing benefits (word of mouth, additional market share)
Whether CNB should roll out to all their theaters or a subset
Customer/demographic analysis by region
What movies should we offer this for – do further market analysis on blockbusters
How will competition react to this model? If CNB does not do this, do they lose customers to competition?
solution: CNB Movies should implement this 24-hour experience. The expected incremental profits are $10,800 (22% profit margin) for one theater. Offering this experience gives CNB a competitive advantage for high-grossing movies. Risks include demand and cost assumptions needing further analysis, projections varying by theater and geography, and movies possibly being a flop. Next steps include developing marketing strategy, sensitivity analysis on demand and costs, and looking for optimal roll-out strategy across the US and criteria on which movies and markets to select.
evaluation: Excellent candidates drive the case themselves, are creative in brainstorming, consider a wide range of internal, external, short-term and long-term factors.

title: Steel Co.
description: Our client is a steel manufacturing company in the Netherlands. They are considering investing in an advanced analytics tool that will help them better predict inventory schedule. They are also looking at immediate methods to save costs and would like your help with the following: A) Analyze the four transport methods (Railways, Trucks, Vessels, Barges) for steel coils delivery and suggest any opportunities for cost saving. B) Suggest any alternative techniques to save cost in the immediate future.
additional_info: Operates internationally in 100+ countries
Customers are predominantly large B2B manufacturers with short and long-term contracts
Steel is only manufactured as coils
Objective is to identify cost saving with no particular target
Utilization refers to % of vehicle capacity used for steel coils
Truck usage is high due to vessels being loaded at 100% capacity, causing coils to be left behind at pier
Cost saving potential of $7.3M through better capacity optimization and $540K from better inventory management for vessels
solution: Drive cost savings of ~$7.84M, with $7.3M savings through better capacity utilization and $540K from better inventory management for vessels. Continue to work on the advanced analytics tool to help inventory management. Modify vessel labor KPIs to ensure 95% utilization instead of 100%, gradually reduce number of trucks used, and renegotiate cost/ton if possible.
evaluation: Good candidates quickly understand options, drive math to cost savings, correlate exhibits, show operations knowledge, and propose creative inventory and capacity utilization solutions.

title: GGC Health
description: Our client, GGC Health, operates eight Ambulatory Surgical Centers (ASC) on the east coast. Revenues have been flat at $400 million/year. The CEO wants to increase revenues by 15%. How can GGC Health increase revenues?
additional_info: ASCs provide same-day surgical care and are alternatives to hospital outpatient procedures
Physicians dictate where surgeries are performed
Pricing increases are not possible
Target revenue increase is $60M (15% of $400M)
Revenue = number of procedures * reimbursement per procedure
Three potential expansion cities: Charlotte (30 doctors), Indianapolis (25 doctors), Nashville (16 doctors)
Physician specialty breakdowns and reimbursement rates provided
Orthopedic: 15 patients/doctor/month, $5K reimbursement
Neurology: 10 patients/doctor/month, $25K reimbursement
Gastroenterology: 12 patients/doctor/month, $15K reimbursement
Urology can be added with 2 doctors per ASC, 8 patients/doctor/month, $8K reimbursement
additional_questions: Which city should GGC Health enter for expansion?
Calculate revenue potential by city based on doctors and specialties.
How can GGC Health increase revenues at current ASC locations?
Calculate additional revenue from adding urology doctors to current ASCs.
solution: Open a new ASC in Indianapolis ($49.8M revenue) and increase revenue at current ASCs by adding urology surgeries ($12M). Together, these meet the $60M revenue target. Other revenue growth opportunities include increasing patients per doctor, adding new procedures, prioritizing high reimbursement surgeries, and improving physician outreach.
evaluation: Excellent candidates quickly eliminate less attractive options, calculate revenues accurately, brainstorm extensively on revenue growth at existing sites, and integrate quantitative and qualitative insights.

title: Gassy Convenience
description: Our client is a large U.S. retail chain owning convenience stores at gas stations in California. They want to pilot just-walk-out (JWO) technology in one existing store for 3 years and want assurance the pilot will be profitable. How should the client evaluate this opportunity?
additional_info: Competitor: Amazon is industry leader; client would be first mover among convenience stores
Product mix includes tobacco, hot and packaged foods, beverages, medicine, toiletries
Client owns all gas station locations (no franchises)
Client has engineering team but no prior digital projects
JWO stores use advanced tech to allow customers to shop without checkout lines
Regions: Northern, Central, Southern California with different market sizes and revenues
Annual profit from goods sold estimated at $2.16M, total store profit $2.5M including labor savings and gas sales
Technology options: In-house development (initial $4.5M, annual $500K) vs Partner (initial $3M, annual $750K)
Discount rate: 10%
additional_questions: What region would be ideal for the pilot?
Estimate annual profit from goods sold for the JWO store.
Which technology option should the client choose based on costs and NPV?
What are possible risks for this pilot?
solution: Pilot in Southern California is recommended due to highest revenue potential. The pilot is expected to be profitable with NPV of $1.0M (in-house) or $1.8M (partner) over 3 years. Risks include customer learning curve, privacy concerns, internal resistance, security risks, job displacement, immature technology, EV adoption reducing gas stations, and regulatory changes.
evaluation: Excellent candidates develop a clear framework covering financials, implementation, and market trends; perform mental math; assess risks comprehensively; and provide a confident recommendation.

title: Nook Co.
description: Your client Fun Ventures, a PE firm, is considering acquiring Nook Co., a hospitality group developing premium private island vacation destinations. Nook Co. proposed an initial offer of $1.5B. Should Fun Ventures proceed with the acquisition?
additional_info: Nook Co. owns and operates 10 islands in East Asia, with 5 more under construction
Serves international customers
Fun Ventures targets 15% ROI
Market size: $1B revenue/year for premium island vacations
Nook Co. has 28% market share
Room rate: $10,000/night, average stay 5 nights
Costs include labor, food, transport, fixed costs like maintenance, insurance, marketing, and administrative
Nook Co. profit margin is 30%, but lower than competitors due to high administrative costs
Discount rate: 5%
ROI calculated at 12%, slightly below target
Potential to cut administrative costs by $5M to exceed 15% ROI
additional_questions: What is the potential market size for premium island vacations?
Calculate Nook Co.'s profit in 2019.
Calculate ROI on the acquisition.
What other potential risks should be considered?
solution: The acquisition yields a 12% ROI, slightly below the 15% target. Cutting administrative costs by $5M could raise ROI above 15%. Risks include unsustainable growth, customer churn, lack of competitive advantage, financial manipulation, environmental risks, regulatory issues, and shifts in consumer preferences. Recommendation can be yes or no based on risk tolerance and potential for operational improvements.
evaluation: Excellent candidates calculate market size and profitability accurately, analyze ROI critically, identify risks creatively, and provide a confident, evidence-based recommendation.

title: Apartment Co.
description: Apartment Co. owns and operates 10 residential buildings in NYC boroughs. Despite $5M investment in amenities and upgrades, profitability has not increased as expected. Half the buildings have laundry facilities; the rest do not. How can Apartment Co. achieve higher long-term profitability?
additional_info: Revenue source is tenant rent; other sources negligible
50 units per building; 5 buildings in Manhattan, 3 in Brooklyn, 2 in Queens
Buildings fully owned, no loans
Tenant demographic: young working professionals
Management pays utilities as part of rent since 2017
Utilities include electricity, gas, water
Two main energy reduction investment options: energy efficient lighting and solar panels
Lighting: $1.5M cost, $750K annual savings, 2 year payback, 10 year bulb lifespan
Solar panels: $3.2M cost, $960K annual savings, ~3 year payback, 20 year lifespan
Electricity rates and utility costs have increased over recent years
additional_questions: What are some ways to reduce utilities expense?
Evaluate investment options: energy efficient lighting vs solar panels.
Which investment should Apartment Co. pursue?
solution: Apartment Co. should consider installing solar panels for greater annual savings and tax benefits, install energy efficient lighting for quicker payback, or both if financially feasible. Risks include volatile energy prices, high upfront costs, tenant behavior, and opportunity costs. Next steps include researching providers, securing subcontractors, and piloting installations.
evaluation: Excellent candidates focus on utilities as main cost driver, perform investment payback calculations, creatively brainstorm risks and benefits, and make data-supported recommendations.

title: Sternofi
description: Hamilton & Hamilton (H&H), a multinational pharmaceutical company, is considering acquiring Sternofi, a biotech company with a promising pancreatic cancer drug candidate in phase 2 trials called Deanraghutumab. Should H&H proceed with the acquisition?
additional_info: Drug treats pancreatic cancer; other pipeline drugs are early stage and uncertain
Focus on US market only
65,000 people diagnosed with pancreatic cancer annually in US
Pancreatic cancer market size is $1.5B/year in US
Deanraghutumab has 12 years of patent protection remaining
H&H wants to break-even within 8 years
H&H has few oncology products and wants to expand in this area
additional_questions: What is the market size and potential patient population?
How efficacious is the drug compared to current standard of care?
What are Sternofi’s potential profits and ROI?
What synergies and risks should be considered?
Should H&H acquire Sternofi?
solution: Candidate should analyze market size, drug efficacy, pricing, and expected profits over patent life. Consider synergies in sales, development, and manufacturing. Evaluate risks including FDA approval, patent protection, competitor response, and reimbursement. Decision to acquire should be based on logical financial and strategic rationale; either yes or no is acceptable if well supported.
evaluation: Excellent candidates structure the problem clearly, perform quantitative market sizing and valuation, consider strategic fit and synergies, identify key risks, and provide a confident recommendation.

title: Cups
description: Calculate the US market size for such a product.
additional_info: Population of U.S.: 330 Million
Female Population: 165 Million
Assume even distribution of population from 0-80
Assume menstruation starts at 10 and ends at 50
Assume a 10% willingness to use such a product
Comparable product price: $40
Market capture: aggressive 10% in first year, 5%
solution: Market size estimated using top-down approach: 82.5M menstruating females, 10% willing to use = 8.25M potential users. At $40 price, total market $330M. With 10% market capture first year, revenue $33M; conservative 5% capture yields $16.5M.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure a top-down market sizing, use given assumptions, and perform calculations accurately.

title: Cups - Survey Analysis
description: Proctor and Gamble conducted a survey sent to all US women aged 18-50 to determine their willingness to switch from their current menstrual products to a menstrual cup. Based on this information, what adjustments would you make to your market size?
additional_info: Survey was sent to approximately 64M women
Total survey respondents: 3.2M
P&G believes this survey accurately represents the population’s interest in this product
P&G believes they could switch a quarter of the undecided 40% to use the product with 10% market capture in Year 1
solution: Adjust willingness to use from 10% to 20% (adding ¼ of 40% undecided). This doubles the addressable market to 16.5M users, increasing potential revenue to $660M. With 10% market capture, first year revenue is $66M; conservative 5% capture yields $33M.
evaluation: Candidate should incorporate survey data to refine market sizing and demonstrate understanding of customer willingness impact.

title: Cups - Pricing and Cannibalization
description: Given the information in Exhibit B and Exhibit C, how should P&G price the menstrual cup in order to offset cannibalization of current menstrual care product sales?
additional_info: Use 10% market capture to calculate cannibalized annual sales of existing care products
Addressable market is 16.5M, with 10% market capture = 1.65M customers
Annual sales per customer for current products is $90
Gross up sales for 5-year cup lifespan
PV of cannibalized revenue at 10% discount rate is $675M
R&D costs are $12M
Estimated complementary product revenue is $637.5M PV
solution: Calculate breakeven unit price as (Cannibalized Revenue + R&D Costs - Estimated Complementary Revenue) / Captured User Market = ($675M + $12M - $637.5M) / 1.65M = approx. $30 per cup. This price offsets cannibalization and covers costs.
evaluation: Candidate should perform multi-step financial calculations, understand cannibalization impact, and derive a breakeven price per unit.

title: Cups - Product Launch Feasibility
description: Given the information in Exhibit D and the break-even unit sales price calculated in Question 3, should P&G launch the menstrual cup product?
additional_info: All respondents for a certain price category will purchase at any price in that category
At least 10% of customers interested in menstrual cup are willing to pay the break-even unit sales price
P&G estimates they can convert 25% of respondents in the 'Neither Likely or Unlikely' category to purchasers
solution: Sum of respondents willing to pay break-even price exceeds the minimum required customers (298K > 162K), indicating the product launch is feasible.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze survey data and price sensitivity to assess launch feasibility.

title: Cups - Marketing Brainstorm
description: P&G has noticed consumers increased interest in sustainability. What suggestions would you have for the marketing team to promote the menstrual cup? How can P&G lean into this consumer interest in their other menstrual products?
additional_info: Product: ease of use, new material
Pricing: according to customer willingness to pay and previous calculations
Place: pharmacies, direct-to-consumer channels
Promotion: ease of use, sustainability factors, lifetime savings vs. tampons & pads, influencer marketing
For non-cup products: biodegradable materials, less packaging, transparency on ingredients, organic products
Sustainability-focused ad campaigns for non-cup products
solution: Marketing should emphasize sustainability, ease of use, and cost savings for the cup. For other products, shift to biodegradable materials and sustainability messaging to align with consumer trends.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate creativity and understanding of marketing mix (4Ps) with sustainability focus.

title: Fungicide
description: Funguy Corporation is a small producer and wholesaler of agricultural fungicides. After a yearly financial review, they find one of their proprietary products, Formula X, is likely to be operating at a loss. They ask you to quantify the exact loss and find options on how to return Formula X to profitability.
additional_info: Competitors: Large international competitors
Market Growth: Mature, growing at inflation rate
Demand for Formula X is growing
Product Benefits: Very effective, patented with 10 years left
Client owns production plant and distribution centers
Contracts with suppliers and buyers are several years long
Costs: Fixed (infrastructure, CapEx, insurance), Variable (labor, COGS, misc)
Operations: Batches per day, production process details
Legal, patent, compliance, competition, geography, cannibalization considerations
additional_questions: Quantify our current profitability loss
Brainstorm ways to increase profitability
What is the effect of running three batches on operating profit?
What is the effect of recycling the reject pile on long term operating profit?
solution: Current loss is $5,000 per batch or $10,000 per day. Running three batches per day improves marginal costs and breaks even. Recycling reject pile before granulator can increase net profit by $19,200 per batch. Recommendations include running three batches and introducing recycling of rejected pellets.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a profitability framework, perform quantitative analysis on operations, identify cost drivers, and propose operational improvements with understanding of constraints.

title: Hybrid Work Model
description: Galloway Entertainment, a small television production company, is considering shifting to a hybrid work model to consolidate real estate and generate operational efficiencies. Evaluate whether the CEO should proceed with this move.
additional_info: Current lease is up in 3 years; breaking lease costs 50% of remaining lease value
Current office space utilization and costs provided in Exhibit 1
New office lease details provided in Exhibit 2
Server room will be moved offsite at $5 per square foot per month
Moving costs: $250,000 one-time
Lease breakage costs: $250,000
New technology costs: $500,000 implementation + $250,000 annual license
Hybrid work trends and filmmaking hubs data in Exhibit 3
CEO expects cash-neutral or better over 2 years
additional_questions: How does shifting to a hybrid work model impact the company’s current operational footprint, and how does space utilization change? What does this tell you?
The real estate team found a new space and need to move on the opportunity quickly due to multiple competing bids. Under the new location’s lease, how much can they save on an annual basis?
What costs and benefits do you think there are for Galloway Entertainment to make this move?
We found some data on filmmaking hubs and hybrid / remote work trends. What does this tell you?
solution: Shifting to hybrid reduces space needs and rent costs but incurs significant one-time moving, lease breakage, and technology costs. Over 2 years, net savings are slightly negative. Qualitative benefits include access to new talent and competitiveness in hiring. Recommendation is cautious; CEO’s belief in near-term savings is misguided but strategic benefits exist.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze quantitative savings and costs, incorporate qualitative factors, challenge assumptions, and provide a balanced recommendation.

title: Toto Foundation
description: The Toto Foundation, a non-profit arm of a drone manufacturing company, is exploring aid projects in Africa. Determine where and how to deploy their technology to maximize impact within budget constraints.
additional_info: Grant: $4 million upfront, $1 million per year thereafter
Focus on maximizing lives impacted, safety, and budget constraints
Projects considered: blood & plasma delivery, crop monitoring, vaccine & general medicinal delivery, dry food delivery, poacher monitoring, insulin delivery
Expertise, need, impact scores, and upfront costs provided
Country options: Rwanda, Tanzania, Senegal, Guinea, Central African Republic
Country data includes population, density, size, vaccination rates, poverty, road quality, danger to nonprofits, ease of doing business
Annual cost and impact data for Senegal and Guinea provided
additional_questions: Based on your analysis, determine which project is the most attractive for the foundation to pursue.
What factors should the client consider when choosing a country? Which countries should they select?
Calculate annual costs and impact for both target countries.
solution: Vaccine & general medicinal delivery is the most attractive project due to high need, impact, and manageable upfront cost. Senegal and Guinea are best country options due to low danger and need. Senegal offers better scalability and impact for the same annual cost. Recommendation is to launch vaccination delivery in Senegal.
evaluation: Candidate should balance non-profit priorities, analyze quantitative and qualitative data, and make a clear recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: WiFi in the Sky
description: A domestic airline carrier is considering entering the in-flight connectivity (IFC) market to stay competitive. What key factors should they consider in their go-to-market strategy for IFC?
additional_info: IFC is typically installed and managed by outside vendors with 10+ year contracts
Different vendors use different technologies with varying quality
Total bandwidth is shared among passengers; pricing can affect usage and service quality
Airline flies domestic and select international routes with 90 planes and ~30K flights per year
In-flight entertainment is separate but can be integrated with IFC
Competitor IFC business models and pricing provided
Capex and margin data provided for break-even analysis
additional_questions: What are your thoughts on the two competitor IFC business models?
Calculate what percentage of passengers need to purchase a session for the airline to break even in 2 years.
What do you think are the key drivers of IFC take rate?
solution: Competitor 1’s model offers less operational burden but less pricing control; Competitor 2’s model offers more control but higher costs. Break-even take rate is approximately 12.9%, higher than current market rates (5-10%). Key drivers of take rate include flight length, price, service quality, passenger demographics, and entertainment options. Recommendation should consider pricing strategy, vendor choice, and customer experience.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze business models critically, perform break-even math, identify market drivers, and provide strategic recommendations.

title: Take Your Pills
description: Our client is experiencing a growth slowdown. Diagnose the cause and recommend whether they should launch a patient adherence campaign.
additional_info: The cause of growth slowdown is patient adherence and persistence.
The campaign could improve adherence leading to healthier patients and potentially increase patient acquisition.
The campaign has a high upfront cost and benefits are uncertain.
No data on causes of low patient adherence or persistence is available.
Alternative solutions could include pursuing additional indications or newer drugs.
A rollout plan for the campaign needs to be established.
solution: Candidate should identify patient adherence and persistence as the cause of slowdown. Recommendation depends on cost-benefit analysis. Campaign may be justified if benefits outweigh costs. Risks include uncertainty and high investment. Alternative uses of funds should be considered.
evaluation: Candidate stays focused on diagnosing cause and recommending solution. Maintains structured approach to math and analysis. Considers risks and alternative solutions.

title: Great Burger
description: Our client, Great Burger (GB), is considering acquiring Heavenly Donuts (HD). Should GB acquire HD?
additional_info: GB is a large fast food chain operating mostly via franchising.
HD is a doughnut chain with a franchising model based on regions.
GB and HD have different franchising structures.
GB is the 4th largest fast food chain worldwide.
GB competes with McDonald's, Wendy's, Burger King, KFC, etc.
GB owns 15% of stores; 85% are franchisee-owned.
HD operates in US and internationally.
GB has experience with mergers and integration.
Potential synergies include cost savings in SG&A, purchasing, property costs, and revenue synergies from cross-selling and international expansion.
US doughnut consumption per capita is $10/year, projected to double in 5 years.
GB plans to expand HD stores by 2.5x and double HD's US market share in 5 years.
Cannibalization of GB sales by HD doughnuts is estimated at 10%.
Incremental profit per GB store from selling HD doughnuts is approximately $15K.
additional_questions: What areas would you want to explore to determine whether GB should acquire HD?
What potential synergies can you think of between GB and HD?
What sales/store will HD require in 5 years to achieve the growth goals?
How would you assess the profitability impact of selling doughnuts in GB stores?
What would be the incremental profit per store given sales, price, margin, and cannibalization?
Summarize McKinsey’s perspective on whether GB should acquire HD.
solution: Acquiring HD could create significant value through cost and revenue synergies. Doubling HD's market share and expanding stores is feasible given market growth. Selling HD doughnuts in GB stores adds incremental profit. Integration challenges exist due to different franchising models and cultures. Recommendation is to acquire HD with a detailed integration plan.
evaluation: Candidate explores acquisition rationale, synergy identification, quantitative analysis of sales and profitability, and synthesizes findings into a clear recommendation. Structured thinking and math accuracy are key.

title: Uranus Co.
description: Uranus Co. is considering entering the luxury space travel market with a 2-week cruise orbiting Earth. Should they enter and can they be profitable?
additional_info: Uranus Co. is an aerospace company headquartered in Bangkok.
Competitors include SpaceX and MoonShine.
Market is global; target customers are millionaires.
Market sizing factors include global population, % millionaires, health and age requirements, willingness to travel to space, and ticket purchase frequency.
Projected market size is about 6.9 million tickets.
Uranus aims to capture 20% market share gradually.
Operational capacity: 150 ships, 2 trips/month, 400 passengers/trip, total 1.44M tickets/year.
Start-up cost: $1.5B per ship, total $225B upfront.
Annual fixed costs approx. $10B including fuel, maintenance, insurance, payroll, marketing.
Contribution margin per customer is $100k.
Breakeven expected in less than 2 years.
additional_questions: What factors would you consider in estimating the market size for this service?
Estimate the total market size of space travel tickets.
How much market share can Uranus capture?
Does Uranus have the operational capacity to meet the 20% market share target?
Will Uranus break even within two years at this capacity?
solution: Market size is large enough with a wealthy customer base. Uranus has operational capacity to meet 20% market share. Financials show breakeven within 2 years is achievable. Uranus should enter the market leveraging competitive advantages.
evaluation: Candidate builds a structured framework for market sizing, competitive analysis, operational capacity, and financial breakeven. Performs quantitative analysis accurately and synthesizes recommendation.

title: Green Dreamz
description: Green Dreamz, a cannabis company in Colorado, faces market saturation and slowed growth. How can they find new opportunities for profitable growth?
additional_info: Cannabis industry is heavily regulated with restrictions on sales, advertising, and banking.
Green Dreamz operates cultivation and 12 dispensaries with mixed medical and recreational offerings.
Medical and recreational cannabis have different sales volumes, prices, and regulations.
Market shows slowing growth and price declines due to saturation and competition.
Profitability can be calculated from sales, visits, prices, and costs.
Growth options include organic growth (new products, loyalty programs), inorganic growth (acquisitions), geographic expansion, and new customer segments.
Considering acquisition of Treetops, a smaller competitor with infused products division.
Treetops has higher profit margin and complementary product offerings.
Valuation of Treetops based on revenue, profit margin, discount rate, and growth rate suggests a bid price around $30 million.
additional_questions: What can you infer about the cannabis market in Colorado from the sales and price data?
Calculate Green Dreamz’s profitability based on provided data.
What are some ways Green Dreamz can grow its overall revenue?
How would you evaluate the acquisition opportunity of Treetops?
What price should Green Dreamz bid for Treetops?
solution: Market is mature with slowing growth and price pressure. Green Dreamz is profitable but growth is limited. Growth strategies include product innovation, marketing, geographic expansion, and acquisitions. Acquisition of Treetops offers revenue and cost synergies but carries risks. Bid price should reflect NPV of future profits.
evaluation: Candidate interprets complex market data, performs profitability calculations, generates creative growth ideas, evaluates M&A opportunity with structured framework, and performs valuation math.

title: Jimmy’s Dilemma
description: Jimmy Smith has two job offers from competing firms. Which offer should he accept to maximize financial value?
additional_info: Jimmy intends to work 3 years at either firm.
Firm A is second tier but improving; Firm B is top tier.
Compensation includes salary, signing bonus, annual bonus, and tuition reimbursement.
Firm A offers tuition reimbursement at end of year 3; Firm B does not.
Exit opportunities after 3 years differ, with Firm B offering higher expected salaries.
Discount rate for NPV calculations is 14%.
Exit opportunity salary distributions are provided with probabilities.
additional_questions: What are the components of compensation at each firm?
Calculate the NPV of each offer over 3 years.
What other factors should Jimmy consider beyond immediate compensation?
Evaluate the exit opportunities after 3 years and their impact on decision.
What is your recommendation?
solution: Firm A has higher 3-year NPV, but Firm B offers better exit opportunities with higher expected salaries. When discounted, exit opportunities make Firm B the better choice overall. Jimmy should accept Firm B’s offer.
evaluation: Candidate identifies all compensation components, performs DCF/NPV calculations accurately, brainstorms relevant financial and non-financial factors, and integrates exit opportunity analysis into final recommendation.

title: Mord Motor Co: Market Sizing
description: Mord Motors sees a lot of potential in the Electric Vehicle space. The CEO has asked that you calculate the size of the retail EV Market in the US?
additional_info: Competitors: International mid-level automotive company with $25B market cap, competes across all vehicle segments in a fragmented industry.
Market Growth: Internal Combustion Engine Market is stagnating; EV Market is growing at 10% CAGR with few major players.
Product Mix: Mord Motors has a couple promising EV models in development.
Client Characteristics: Owns entire supply chain, headquartered in Detroit, sells cars through own dealerships.
Bottom-Up Approach: # of households, % buying new car this year, % preferring EVs over ICE, # cars x price per EV.
Great Market Sizing: Consider luxury vs economic EV preferences and price points.
Example Answer: 100M households, 20% buy new car/year, 20% buy EVs, 75% economic, 25% luxury, total market size approx. $220B.
solution: Calculate total households, estimate % buying new cars, estimate % choosing EVs, segment by luxury vs economy preferences, multiply by price points to get total market size. Mord Motors should then estimate their potential market share and EV development readiness.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate structured approach to market sizing, use bottom-up logic, segment market by consumer preferences, and reason about pricing and volume assumptions.

title: Mord Motor Co: EV Market Entry Decision
description: Mord Motors has two promising EV models in development but is strapped for cash and worried about risks. What factors would you consider before entering the Electric Vehicle space?
additional_info: Financials: Loans, liquidity, fixed vs variable costs, revenue projections, resale value.
Market: Competitive landscape, product differentiation, value proposition (e.g., autonomous capabilities).
Consumer Preference: ESG considerations, price sensitivity, geography (charging station accessibility).
External Factors: Regulatory approvals, safety standards compliance.
EV market in US broke records in 2021 with 607,600 light EV sales, 83% growth since 2018.
solution: Consider financial constraints including fixed and variable costs, market competition and differentiation, consumer preferences and adoption factors, and external regulatory and safety hurdles before entering EV market.
evaluation: Candidate should cover multiple dimensions including financial, market, consumer, and external factors, and demonstrate structured brainstorming.

title: Mord Motor Co: EV Model Selection Math Question
description: Mord Motor has decided to enter the EV market but only has capital to move forward with one of its two existing models. Given cost and sales data, which model should Mord Motors produce?
additional_info: Raw Materials and Labour & Assembly costs are unit-based; other costs are yearly.
R&D is a one-time sunk cost already incurred for both models.
Mord Motor wants to break even as early as possible.
Model X: Higher price ($160K), higher fixed costs, shorter breakeven when including R&D.
Model Y: Lower price ($50K), higher sales volume, longer breakeven when including R&D.
solution: Calculate unit profit for each model, add fixed costs to find yearly profit, factor in R&D costs to determine breakeven duration. Model X has shorter breakeven including R&D and improves brand equity but is riskier; Model Y is safer with proven sales but longer breakeven.
evaluation: Candidate should perform breakeven analysis, distinguish fixed vs variable costs, consider sunk costs, and weigh qualitative risks and benefits.

title: Mord Motor Co: Recommendation on EV Model
description: Lenry Mord asks for your assessment of the company's future outlook and which EV model to invest in.
additional_info: Model X: New profitable segment, diversifies stagnant business, shorter breakeven, improves brand equity.
Model Y: Less risky, proven sales, higher upside for predicted sales, diversifies stagnant business.
Risks: Highly competitive market, Mord Motors may lack EV production expertise.
Next Steps: Consumer preference surveys, pressure test sales forecasts, optimize production costs.
solution: Recommend investing in Model X for brand and profitability benefits or Model Y for safer market entry. Highlight risks and suggest immediate and long-term next steps.
evaluation: Candidate should synthesize quantitative and qualitative insights, articulate risks, and propose actionable next steps.

title: Curling and Careers
description: Your client is Curling & Careers (C&C), a NYC non-profit providing disadvantaged high school students with leadership training and career development through curling. The program is growing quickly and unable to meet demand. How can they increase their impact?
additional_info: Local chapters operate mostly independently; national org provides funding and marketing.
Currently 1,000 students aged 14-18 from NYC, families under income criteria.
Program includes curling, leadership workshops, mentoring; students transported to curling centers.
Main costs: facility rental, equipment, transportation, staff salaries, administrative overhead.
Goal: Serve more students and meet demand.
additional_questions: Based on Exhibit 1, where can C&C focus efforts to serve more students?
What are some ideas to increase mentorship capacity?
If removing weekly report to HQ, how many more students can be served per week?
solution: Focus on increasing 1:1 mentoring capacity, which has highest unmet demand and impact. Ideas include hiring more mentors, scheduling changes, external partnerships. Removing administrative burden (weekly report) can free up mentor time to serve an additional 420 students weekly.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze capacity vs demand, identify bottlenecks, brainstorm creative solutions, perform math to quantify impact, and contextualize within non-profit priorities.

title: Center Stage
description: A producing team has a 10-week hold on a major Broadway theater next summer and must decide which show to mount among three options: Beauty and the Beast (revival), To Kill a Mockingbird (play), and Steve Live (one-man show). They want to maximize profit margins and decide ticket pricing.
additional_info: 41 active Broadway theaters: 22 musicals, 18 plays, 1 one-person show.
Revenue growth mainly from ticket price increases; typical ticket price $150.
Theater maintenance fee $1,000/show; 8 shows/week.
Costs and revenues detailed in Exhibit 1.
Goal: Achieve 20%+ profit margin.
Non-ticket revenues include concessions and merchandise.
additional_questions: How should the team determine which show to mount?
What are potential costs and revenue sources?
Which show has highest projected profit margin per performance?
How should tickets be priced to maximize revenue?
If willingness to pay $200 increases from 40% to 50%, what is max marketing spend per performance to maintain 25% profit margin?
How should the team spend additional marketing money to increase willingness to pay?
solution: Use financial and market frameworks to evaluate shows. Calculate profit margins per performance: Beauty and the Beast highest at 25%. Price tickets at $200 to maximize revenue with marketing to increase willingness to pay. Spend up to $5,625 per performance on marketing. Marketing ideas include production enhancements, media marketing, and perks.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate quick mental math, structured brainstorming, risk assessment, and ability to connect qualitative and quantitative insights.

title: Pharmageddon
description: Your client Hoffmann, a Swiss pharmaceutical holding company with 26% stake in Technologene, faces a competitor acquiring 20% of Technologene shares. Hoffmann wants to determine if they can take control (51%) of Technologene and how to proceed.
additional_info: Goal: Acquire at least 51% of Technologene shares to take control.
Hoffmann can raise $24B capital for acquisition.
No regulatory constraints on share purchase.
Technologene revenues $9B, costs $4.5B, WACC 10%, growth 5%.
1B shares outstanding.
Shareholder groups: Insiders ($110/share), Passive Investors ($95/share), Competitor ($90/share).
additional_questions: Is it feasible for Hoffmann to take control of Technologene?
How much should Hoffmann offer per share?
Which shareholder group(s) should Hoffmann buy shares from?
Based on Exhibit 2, how can Hoffmann acquire Technologene within budget?
Would you raise additional capital to buy from Insiders or buy from mix of investors? How to mitigate risks?
solution: Valuation: NPV = $90B; Hoffmann needs 25% more shares costing $22.5B, feasible within $24B budget. Offer $90/share. Buying all from Insiders ($110/share) exceeds budget. Best approach is mix: majority from Passive Investors and some from Competitor or Insiders to stay within budget. Mitigate talent loss risk with retention incentives if buying from Insiders is not feasible.
evaluation: Candidate should perform valuation, understand control thresholds, evaluate acquisition strategies, consider shareholder motivations, and propose financial and non-financial risk mitigations.

title: GoNet Internet Service Provider
description: Our client, GoNet, is a US-based Internet Service Provider (ISP) considering entering the European market. They want to determine the potential profitability of entering Europe given market fragmentation and their current revenue streams.
additional_info: Competitive dynamics: Highly fragmented industry, no info about market leaders or trends.
European industry overview: GoNet plans to capture 10 million subscribers.
Subscription fee: $20/month.
Average subscriber online spending: $1,800/year.
GoNet commission: 3% of online spending.
Fixed costs: $1 billion annually.
Variable costs: $110 per subscriber annually.
additional_questions: How could we reduce the fixed costs of investment?
Would there be any reason to continue with the investment even if it looks like it will lose money?
Are there any other risks/benefits that GoNet should consider?
How would you sum up the situation and what is your recommendation?
solution: Initial calculations show profitability with $20/month subscription, but competition charging $10/month reduces revenues and profitability. To maintain profits at $840M, unrealistic increases in online spending per subscriber are required. Breakeven requires more subscribers than market size. Recommendations include exploring cost reductions, differentiating product, or using the investment as a loss leader. The market is attractive but price elasticity and competition reduce profitability.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate's quantitative skills in calculating revenues, costs, and profits; ability to interpret market elasticity; creativity in suggesting cost reductions or strategic options; structured thinking; and ability to synthesize and recommend.

title: Maine Apples
description: Our client, Danut, a Korean conglomerate, has acquired a biotech firm with a chemical that controls produce ripening, especially apples. The chemical allows earlier harvest and improves quality. Danut wants to know if they should commercialize this chemical.
additional_info: Client is only concerned about a test market in Maine.
No competitive products currently on the market.
Local industry growing at GDP rate.
Product benefits: reduced costs through earlier harvesting, improved apple yields, sweeter apples yield more juice.
Maine has 200 orchards, average annual orchard revenue is $30K/acre, average orchard size is 100 acres, one harvest per year.
Cost to maintain crops: $1.5K per night for 100 acres; chemical allows harvest 10 days earlier.
Product improves yield by 10% and juice yield by 5%.
Revenue split: 25% whole apples, 75% juice sales.
Product cost: $100K per 200-acre farm.
solution: The market size is approximately $600M. Cost savings and revenue improvements total $2,025 per acre per year. Pricing the product at $1,000 per acre yields a 50% margin for Danut and a 75% margin for farmers, making commercialization attractive. Qualitative considerations include differentiation, environmental risks, operational capacity, patent issues, test market representativeness, and strategic fit.
evaluation: Assess candidate's ability to size the market, quantify cost savings and revenue improvements, apply value-based pricing, and consider qualitative risks and strategic fit.

title: Orrington Office Supplies (OOS)
description: Our client, OOS, a leading office products manufacturer with $275M sales in 1991, has weakening earnings despite strong brands and acquisitions. They have 5 autonomous divisions sharing manufacturing and marketing. Three plants run at 50% capacity. Analysts see OOS as an acquisition target. How would you improve its profitability?
additional_info: U.S. office supplies market grew at 5% CAGR historically but declined 5% in 1990 and 1991.
Superstore channel is critical and gaining share.
OOS has a broader product line (12.5K SKUs) than competitors (4-5K SKUs).
Distribution: 75% wholesalers, 15% superstores, 10% end customers.
Most profitable product is a high-end branded stapler.
Staples, Inc. is OOS’s largest customer.
Shared costs are 45% of total, allocated by % of sales.
Three plants: Chihuahua, Michigan, New Jersey with different fixed and variable costs.
Current profits: $25M/year.
additional_questions: How would consolidating to Chihuahua change revenues?
How would this change production costs?
How would this change pre-tax profits?
solution: Closing 500 SKUs and consolidating production to Chihuahua plant reduces costs by $68M and revenues by $11M, increasing profits from $25M to $82M. Qualitative issues include implementation timeframe, union relations, distribution and warehousing changes, purchasing centralization, and culture/morale management.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate's ability to analyze profitability trends, interpret fixed vs variable costs, calculate impact of SKU reduction and plant consolidation, and consider operational and cultural implications.

title: Syzygy Supercomputers
description: Syzygy Supercomputers, a large international computers and communications company with $20B annual revenues, has seen a steady decline in profits. The CEO asks how to get back on track.
additional_info: Syzygy is fully integrated and international.
Decline in profitability despite strong competitive position.
Company has been overinvesting in R&D relative to returns.
solution: The main issue is excessive R&D spending that is not generating sufficient returns. The company should realign R&D investments with market needs and profitability goals, optimize product portfolio, and improve operational efficiency.
evaluation: Assess candidate's ability to identify root causes of profitability decline, analyze competitive and operational data, and recommend strategic and operational improvements.

title: Winter Olympics Broadcasting Revenue and NPV
description: Calculate the revenue from broadcasting the Winter Olympics. Factoring in costs, is this a good investment? Find the NPV.
additional_info: Revenues: $400K/ad for prime time (M-F 7-11 PM, all weekend) and $200K/ad for non-prime time
10 minutes/hour of advertisements
Costs: $428,000,000 of total costs
Opportunity cost: $1M/hr
WACC: 12%
solution: Total revenues are $928M. After subtracting $428M total costs and $146M opportunity cost, net cash flow is $346M. Discounting for 6 years at 12% WACC halves the value to $177M NPV. The bid should be around $177M to $200M considering intangibles like new viewers and prestige.
evaluation: Candidate should correctly calculate revenues, costs, opportunity costs, and apply discounting to find NPV. They should also discuss intangible factors influencing bid value beyond pure NPV.

title: Rotisserie Ranch: Pre-Seasoned Chicken Market Introduction
description: Rotisserie Ranch is considering selling pre-flavored rotisserie chickens to grocery chains. Should they begin selling this new product?
additional_info: Rotisserie Ranch currently sells chickens to grocery chains for fresh roasting.
Market research shows consumers are buying more flavored rotisserie chickens.
Client has patented sterile packaging keeping chicken fresh for 30 days, no freezing needed.
Competition is only private label brands; competitors freeze chickens and cannot pre-season.
Four new flavors to be introduced: Barbecue, lemon herb, tandori, teriyaki.
Grocers are interested but want proof the product will sell well.
Test market data available comparing sales and margins in test and control stores.
additional_questions: Do you think grocery retailers would be interested in pre-seasoned chickens from Rotisserie Ranch?
How would you make sure the chickens will sell well?
Should the grocers carry our product based on the test market results?
solution: Client should launch the pre-seasoned BBQ chicken and test other flavors. Grocers benefit from labor cost reduction and economies of scale. Test market shows grocers can expect $100-$150 more gross profit per store. Cannibalization is not an issue as margins are similar. Client benefits from increased sales and price offsets cost increases.
evaluation: Candidate should structure analysis around customer value, revenue and cost implications, competition, and test market results. They should calculate margins and profits from exhibits and interpret demand elasticity. Creativity in discussing benefits and risks is valued.

title: Tarrant Fixtures: Improving Profitability
description: Our client, Tarrant Fixtures, is a manufacturing company producing retail display fixtures. Their ROI has fallen over the last three years. How can they get back on track?
additional_info: Product types: Custom displays (50% sales, made to order) and Standard displays (50% sales, built to stock).
Number of standardized SKUs increased from 5 to 12 in last 3 years.
Revenues grew 25% from $100M to $125M; costs stable at 80% of revenue.
No new CAPEX; PP&E stable.
Working capital increased from $80M to $130M, inventory increased 300% from $25M to $75M.
Market has grown 25%, client maintains 25% market share.
solution: ROI decline is due to $50M increase in working capital caused by inventory build-up from SKU proliferation, inaccurate demand forecasts, and obsolete inventory. Recommendations: write off obsolete inventory, improve demand forecasting, reduce standardized product lines back to top 5 SKUs.
evaluation: Candidate should use ROI formula, analyze profit and capital employed, identify inventory as cause, and propose practical solutions to reduce inventory and improve forecasting.

title: Vindaloo Corporation: Valuation of New Sugar Beet Seed
description: Vindaloo Corporation developed a new sugar beet seed that produces twice as much sugar per beet. They want to sell the company and wonder how much it is worth.
additional_info: New seeds produce 2 lbs sugar per 100 beets vs. 1 lb previously.
Elasticity of demand for sugar is 1.
Value chain costs: Farming 40%, Trucking 10%, Refining 30%, Distribution 20%.
Farmland not used for sugar can be repurposed to cabbage, which is 1/5 as profitable as sugar.
Trucking costs: 5% fixed, 95% variable proportional to weight.
Refining costs: all variable, 25% more expensive per beet for new seeds.
No cost savings in distribution.
Sugar market size: $2B worldwide, growing 2% annually.
Assume 100% market penetration and patent protection.
additional_questions: What are the cost savings/increase in profits from farming?
What are the cost savings from Trucking, Refining, and Distribution?
What are the total cost savings in the value chain?
How valuable is this product?
solution: Farming profits increase by 10% due to repurposing half the land to cabbage. Trucking costs decrease by 47.5%. Refining costs decrease by 37.5%. Distribution costs unchanged. Total cost savings weighted at 20%. Valued as a perpetuity with 10% discount and 2% growth, product worth $5B.
evaluation: Candidate should identify impact on farming profits, calculate weighted cost savings across value chain, and apply NPV formula correctly. They should also discuss risks and benefits qualitatively.

title: Zephyr Beverages: Divest or Retain Chilled Juice Business
description: Zephyr Beverages produces fruit juices in chilled, juice box, and frozen concentrate forms. The chilled segment is breaking even or losing money. Should Zephyr sell the chilled juice business?
additional_info: Zephyr's chilled juice sales: $120M; total juice division sales: $600M.
Chilled juice industry size: $5B worldwide.
Market shares: two largest players 40% and 25%, Zephyr 12%.
Market growing at GDP rate; brand name important; price sensitive market with coupons and promotions.
Zephyr uses more expensive flavor blends than competitors.
All products produced in one California plant; difficult to repurpose plant.
Divesting chilled juice affects synergies with other juice businesses.
solution: Three options: sell chilled juice business (loses synergies), sell entire juice division (low selling price), or keep chilled juice and reduce costs by reformulating ingredients. The last is most feasible given competitors' success and plant constraints.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze competitive dynamics, cost structure, product differentiation, and synergies. They should weigh qualitative and quantitative factors and propose well-reasoned recommendations.

title: A+ Airline Co.: Switch to Credit Card Only for In-Flight Sales
description: Gamma Airline switched from accepting cash and credit cards to credit cards only for in-flight food and beverage sales. Should A+ Airline Co. follow this strategy?
additional_info: Gamma is the only airline to have switched; others are evaluating.
A+ Airline is third largest US airline; similar to Gamma in all respects.
Card use: 99% of consumers have credit cards.
Items sold: food and alcoholic beverages only, US domestic flights.
Cash management overhead costs $35 per flight; credit card processing fees 2% for both methods.
Switching to card only results in losing 1/3 of cash-only customers.
Payment collection is 30 days faster with credit cards, improving cash flow.
Working capital benefits from reduced cash on hand and theft risk.
solution: Switching to credit card only results in $35 revenue loss per flight but $35 overhead savings, net neutral to bottom line. Additional benefits include faster payment collection, improved working capital, reduced theft, and potentially happier customers due to faster transactions. Recommendation is to switch.
evaluation: Candidate should structure analysis as before vs after, quantify revenue loss and cost savings, consider time value of money and working capital impacts, and discuss qualitative benefits.

title: Bell Computer Inc.: Declining Profit Margins Despite Revenue Growth
description: Bell Computer Inc., the second largest US PC manufacturer, has growing revenue and market share but declining net income. Why have profit margins declined and how can profitability be improved?
additional_info: Product segments: Desktop PCs, Laptops, Netbooks, Servers, Other.
From 2005 to 2010, units sold increased, but ASPs declined especially for laptops and netbooks.
Cost structure remained stable; no significant cost reductions.
Competitors have reduced costs through better part procurement, labor efficiency, and overhead control.
Data exhibits show sales units, prices, and costs for 2005, 2010, and 'Full Potential' scenario.
solution: Margins declined due to shift in product mix towards laptops and netbooks with lower ASPs and negative net income per unit. Bell did not reduce costs accordingly. To improve profitability, Bell should adopt competitors' cost reduction strategies in procurement, labor, and overhead to reach 'Full Potential' margins.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze profit equation, calculate net income per unit and total profits by product and year, identify causes of margin decline, and propose cost reduction strategies based on competitor benchmarks.

title: Vitality Insurance, Inc.
description: Our client, Vitality Insurance, is a leading provider of supplemental insurance products in the United States. Vitality agents partner with companies to offer their employees optional, supplemental insurance for such conditions as life, cancer, etc. Vitality has undergone fairly steady growth in the past two years, but they suspect that some of their costs may be out of whack. Is this indeed true, and, if so, what should they do about it?
additional_info: All line items except for sales costs growing at 10% per year.
Sales costs grow at 10% from 2008 to 2009, but at 45% from 2009 to 2010 (while premium growth remains at 10%).
Profit margins are declining significantly from 09-10, suggesting that the increase in sales costs is not paying off.

Vitality launched a contest called “Sweeps Week” that aimed to drive increased premium in weeks that were traditionally low volume for the company. Vitality paid an extra 10% commission on all premium booked in those two weeks.
Sales agents thought “Sweeps Week” was a great contest.
Vitality has over 10K field sales agents who are solely compensated on a % commission of total new premium.
Major costs: sales, G&A, and advertising.
Mature market with high agent turnover.
Agents have authority to book sales whenever they want by influencing the enrollment timing by up to one week.
Acquiring new customers costs considerably more than retaining or up-selling existing customers.
solution: Vitality overspent in 2010 on the “Sweeps Week” sales contest, hurting profitability. The contest influenced the sales force to push/pull sales into contest weeks to earn extra commission, which did not add much value beyond the main commission system. Recommendation is to eliminate “Sweeps Week” for 2011 and repurpose funds toward activities not covered by the main commission structure. The increased contests on new accounts may also decrease profitability due to higher acquisition costs.
evaluation: Candidate should recognize the abnormal increase in sales costs and link it to declining profit margins. They should identify the shift in contest mix and its impact on sales timing and profitability. Strong candidates will discuss the cost structure, sales incentives, and suggest actionable recommendations.

title: Chic Cosmetology University
description: Our client is a for-profit, specialty college named Chic Cosmetology University (CCU). CCU is currently the market leader for cosmetology education with campuses in ten major metropolitan areas in the US. CCU has capital to invest in a new campus and is considering Chicagoland as a location – should they do it? The client considers a successful launch as achieving positive operating profit for the new campus two years after opening.
additional_info: Average revenue per enrollment = $15K.
Total annual fixed cost per campus = $4.8M.
Total variable cost per student = $8K.
Gross profit per student = $7K.
Initial building costs to renovate site in Chicagoland = $4.5M amortized evenly over 3 years.
Fixed costs remain flat per year.
CCU and industry enrollments growing at 5% per year.
2-3 other large specialty colleges compete in the market; community colleges offer lower-priced alternatives.
Campuses with competitors present tend to have lower market share (10%) than those without (15%).
Projected 10% market share for Chicago campus (one competitor).
80% of enrolled students come directly from high school; 20% from adult market.
CCU identified 1,000 targeted high schools in Chicagoland with an average of 6 potential students per high school.
solution: CCU should NOT enter the Chicago market under the current cost structure. Even with a 10% market share assumption in year 1, the Chicago campus will enroll only 750 students, 150 fewer than the 900 required to break-even. The client should consider scholarships, other cities without CCU presence, adding a second campus in high market share cities, market research to increase potential students, and ways to reduce fixed costs.
evaluation: Candidate should perform break-even analysis, estimate market share and enrollment, and identify qualitative factors affecting success. Strong candidates will recognize that new campuses typically achieve lower market share initially and suggest strategic alternatives.

title: DigiBooks Inc.
description: Our client, DigiBooks, is a manufacturer and seller of electronic book readers. DigiBooks also distributes e-books for the e-reader through their website. The reader is only compatible with books sold through the DigiBooks site. DigiBooks is planning the launch of its e-book readers in a country where no e-book readers are currently sold. Only 1% of the population has ever used an e-book reader, though 50% is aware of the concept. The Chief Marketing Officer of DigiBooks has come to you to help determine: How should DigiBooks launch and market the e-reader product in this new country?
additional_info: E-book reader uses e-ink electronic paper display with 16 shades of grey, 12-hour battery life, easy readability.
DigiBooks has never sold a product outside of the US.
No competitors in the e-book or tablet space plan to enter this country.
Market segments: Super Readers (8M), Occasional Readers (20M), Rare Readers (10M).
Average price of an e-book is $10; e-books and e-reader have the same margin.
Retail channel: 50% of total potential market, 50% e-book GM, 30% e-reader GM, 40% penetration, $20M initial investment.
Internet channel: 50% of total potential market, 50% e-book GM, 60% e-reader GM, 10% penetration, $10M initial investment.
additional_questions: Through research, we found several segments. Using a 3-year projection, which segment should the e-book reader target?
DigiBooks is now considering how it should sell its e-book readers: through retail stores or through the internet. How would you go about evaluating this decision?

Based on revised market estimates, we decided to price the e-reader at $100 and target the Occasional Reader segment. Using this information and Exhibit 2, can you estimate the profit potential of each of these sales channels? Which should we choose? What segment would you recommend to your client?
solution: DigiBooks should launch the e-book reader targeting the Occasional Reader segment through the Retail Sales Channel. This approach yields an estimated $300M return on a $20M initial investment. The retail channel encourages product trial and joint marketing but has lower margins, while the internet channel has higher margins but lower penetration. The Occasional Reader segment offers the highest revenue potential.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a marketing framework covering segmentation, targeting, positioning, product, price, promotion, and distribution. They should perform quantitative analysis to identify the best segment and sales channel. Strong candidates will consider nuances such as time value of money and probability of purchase.

title: After School Programming
description: Our client offers after school programming focused on supporting at-risk youth through high school, enabling them to enter and succeed in college. The client is trying to identify the best approach to meet its growth target. The client’s goals for expansion are to most efficiently serve students at 7 new sites, while raising their national profile. We have been hired to help them vet potential sites to maximize their social and financial impact.
additional_info: 'At-risk' youth are those who, due to behavior or grades, are at risk of dropping out of high school or have already done so.
The client operates local centers attached to high schools with full time staff.
The client offers tutoring and test prep support, as well as connecting youth to internships and career opportunities.
All centers are in Massachusetts or southern New Hampshire.
The client operates 8 sites with 2,500 youth served.
School districts and state agencies reimburse the client for activities.
The client has a high national profile and has declined offers to expand to Florida and California.
Central office costs are allocated uniformly per site; central costs do not vary by expansion method.
Data on at-risk youth and school districts is provided for analysis.
additional_questions: What are the client’s options for locating and for opening new sites, and what are some considerations the client should consider in selecting among these options?
Let’s look at the financial considerations, particularly at the effect of additional sites on central costs.
From a mission perspective, which geographic areas show the most promise for mission fulfillment?
What do you think are the pros and cons of additional factors in each geographic area? How might this influence the client’s choice of target geographies?
Please summarize your findings and provide a recommendation to the client.
solution: The client should focus on expanding within existing states and possibly neighboring states rather than new distant states. Financial analysis shows benefits from scale, and mission impact is highest in existing and neighboring geographies with high densities of at-risk youth. Expansion outside New England is not recommended due to higher risks and lower mission fit.
evaluation: Candidate should identify organizational options (partnerships, licensing, wholly-owned), analyze financial impact of expansion on central costs, evaluate mission fit using data, and weigh qualitative factors. Strong candidates will integrate financial and mission considerations into a coherent recommendation.

title: Dark Sky Co.
description: Our client, Dark Sky, is a small manufacturer of unmanned data collection aircraft. The Assessor aircraft is profitable but sales have stagnated. The client wishes to grow. What are some steps Dark Sky could take to achieve growth?
additional_info: The military started purchasing the Assessor in 2002 at $100,000 per unit; it is Dark Sky’s only source of revenue.
Dark Sky has developed prototypes for new aircraft: SeaBird (navy, $220k), SandBird (army, $210k), JointBird (army/navy compromise, $180k).
Military agreed to a 10% price increase for the Assessor; forecast sales remain flat at 50 units annually.
Introduction of a new aircraft will cannibalize Assessor sales at different rates depending on the new product.
Dark Sky has additional capacity and can strengthen workforce divisions as needed.
There are about 20 competitors; Dark Sky is considered an acquisition target.
Contracts are Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee with the U.S. Military.
additional_questions: How many units were sold in 2010? What was the growth rate from 2002 to 2006?
What are some steps to achieve growth?
To maximize short-term growth, which aircraft should Dark Sky produce?
solution: To maximize short-term growth, Dark Sky should introduce the JointBird aircraft. This yields the highest total revenue ($16.75M) despite heavy cannibalization of Assessor sales. However, the client should consider profitability, long-term forecasts, competitive response, and manufacturing capacity.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze current sales and growth, identify organic and inorganic growth options, perform revenue calculations for product scenarios, and recommend the best growth strategy. Strong candidates will consider strategic implications beyond short-term revenue.

title: Salty Sole Shoe Co.
description: Your client is a large retail-focused private equity firm that owns Salty Sole, a leading designer of junior women’s footwear, primarily targeting the 14 – 22 year old age group. Salty Sole was purchased last year by the private equity firm expecting to realize substantial profits upon sale in 2012 by increasing the company’s EBITDA. The situation, however, is that due to a current recession, annual profit has only grown modestly post the acquisition and is not on track to generate the double-digit returns that the private equity firm originally anticipated. How can the company increase profitability and achieve the private equity firm’s return on investment objectives?
additional_info: Client is the market leader in junior women’s footwear in the U.S. only.
Apparel industry is characterized by cyclicality due to economy and consumer preferences.
Client designs and distributes footwear to discount retailers (think Kohl’s) and is considered mid-priced.
Client outsources all manufacturing on a fixed-contract basis (manufacturing costs with outsourced providers fall under Fixed Costs).
Client follows a “me-too” strategy and follows fashion rather than inventing it then offering lower prices than name brands.
Client competes on the basis of trendy fashion and value pricing.
Fixed costs cannot be adjusted based on the company’s research.
Variable costs are currently at the lowest possible rates based on market research.
Pricing has remained constant at an average of $25/unit; it would not be prudent to adjust pricing based on industry research.
To achieve a 20% return on investment, EBITDA needs to be approximately $50 million, requiring volume to more than double.
The client is already the market leader with greater than 35% share and the market size is not projected to increase.
solution: The interviewee should identify that volume is the key lever to increase profitability since cost structure and price are fixed. Volume must more than double to achieve the desired growth, which is challenging given the recession and stagnant market size. Recommended strategies include pursuing new products, entering new geographies or distribution channels, increasing marketing to steal share, acquiring growth brands, and adjusting product mix to higher-margin products. Risks include capacity constraints and cannibalization. Fixed and variable costs are at minimal levels, and pricing adjustments are not advisable.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to break down profitability into price, volume, and cost components; identify volume as the key driver; perform quantitative calculations to determine required volume; consider market share and market growth; propose realistic growth strategies; and discuss risks and trade-offs.

title: Plastic World
description: Our client is a private equity firm interested in PlasticWorld, a plastic packaging manufacturer. PlasticWorld’s owners are requesting $25M. The offer is final. Should our client buy?
additional_info: PlasticWorld makes plastic packaging for beverages, cosmetics, household and automotive chemicals.
Products are top quality with 350 sets of molds, different materials, finish, colors, and constant innovation.
Overall product mix has not changed in recent years.
Sales force is highly skilled, compensated on market share, and holds market share.
Two years ago, PlasticWorld invested in equipment for product innovation.
Customers exhibit strong loyalty but face increasing pressures to innovate.
PlasticWorld has experienced a steady drop in revenues while sales volume has grown, indicating price reductions.
Profit margin is negative (-6%), while competitors are profitable.
Sales force incentives based on market share encourage price reductions.
Simplifying the product line by eliminating some molds could reduce costs.
Valuation sensitivity shows company worth $25M if profit margin improves to 0%, and $40M at industry average profit margin.
solution: The client should accept the $25M offer and focus on boosting profitability by changing sales force compensation from market share to company earnings, simplifying the product line to reduce costs, and examining industry best practices for further improvements. The main issue is pricing driven by sales force incentives, and addressing this will improve margins and company value.
evaluation: Candidate should identify pricing as the cause of revenue decline, link sales force incentives to price drops, analyze profitability relative to industry peers, understand valuation sensitivity to profit margin, and propose actionable improvements including incentive restructuring and product line simplification.

title: Zoo Co.
description: Our client is a zoo that is thinking about acquiring a famous zebra from an African preserve. It’s a huge investment, but they believe the new zebra would be a great contribution to their animal community. You have been engaged to help decide whether this is a good idea. What would you consider when trying to help your client make this decision?
additional_info: 300,000 people visit the zoo yearly.
Admission price is $15.
Another zoo that acquired a similar zebra had an 8% increase in attendance.
Acquisition cost: $235K; New facilities: $850K; Transportation: $110K; Annual maintenance: $90K.
Discount rate is 20%.
Insurance company offers a contract to guarantee revenue if attendance increase is less than or equal to 5%, charging 0.1% of total annual revenues as premium.
Market research shows probabilities and revenue associated with attendance increases of 3%, 5%, 7%, and 9%.
additional_questions: What is the breakeven attendance increase required?
Do you think the insurance company is providing a good deal to the zoo?
solution: The acquisition is unlikely to be a good idea given the financial analysis. The breakeven attendance increase is approximately 7.3%, higher than the expected 8% increase from the comparable zoo but with uncertainty. The insurance contract is too expensive relative to the expected benefit and does not create value. Negotiations with the insurer to improve terms could be considered.
evaluation: Candidate should perform NPV calculations, conduct breakeven and sensitivity analyses, evaluate risk mitigation options such as insurance, interpret market research data, and synthesize findings into a clear recommendation.

title: Money Bank Call Center
description: Our client is a large financial services firm with multiple locations around the world. Part of their service offering includes a 24 hour helpline. The client has their call centers in New York and Paris. The client has recently acquired a small firm (Firm B) in order to expand its reach in a particular geography. Firm B provides a subset of the services and has its call center located in the Philippines. The client has asked us to evaluate its strategy going forward for handling customer calls. In particular they want us to look into the call center operations.
additional_info: Client provides full range of financial services for individuals and small organizations.
Acquired firm was started 5 years ago and is still run by the original founders.
New employees are college graduates with basic knowledge of financial services and products.
Fluency in English and several European languages required.
Very difficult to lay off employees in Paris location; significant costs will be incurred.
Philippines government encourages investment with significant tax advantages.

Call center performance data: New York - 600,000 calls/year, $50K employee cost/year, $450K overhead, 75 employees; Paris - 400,000 calls/year, $50K employee cost/year, $600K overhead, 60 employees; Philippines - 300,000 calls/year, $20K employee cost/year, $300K overhead, 75 employees.
Philippines can process 20 calls/employee/day at $6 per call; New York processes 40 calls/employee/day at $7 per call.
CEO requires solution to be at least cost neutral by year 2.
One-time cost of $5M to close NY and Paris centers and expand Philippines facility.
solution: The case is open-ended. The interviewee should evaluate cost-effectiveness and efficiency of the three locations, consider qualitative factors such as regulatory environment, employee skills, and customer satisfaction, and assess risks of outsourcing. A good recommendation would justify whether to outsource to the Philippines, maintain multiple centers, or consolidate, considering cost savings, risks, and implementation challenges.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a structured framework covering cost, capacity, quality, and regulatory issues; analyze quantitative data on costs and productivity; consider qualitative factors; identify risks and mitigation strategies; and provide a balanced recommendation with next steps.

title: Thompson Healthcare
description: Our client is Thompson Healthcare, a health insurance firm located in the Midwest. Customers pay Thompson a fixed monthly premium per person covered under the plan. In exchange, Thompson pays for all health services that each member requires. In recent years, Thompson’s financial and competitive position has begun to erode, and the CEO has retained our firm to help them determine what is causing the problem and how to fix it.
