additional_info: Thompson Healthcare is a mutual insurance company; profits are returned to members as lower premiums.
Market share is steady despite presence of major national competitor United Healthcare (UHC) with 30% market share.
UHC typically earns a 5% profit margin.
National average medical cost inflation is 10% over past five years; Thompson’s medical cost inflation is 12%.
UHC’s medical cost inflation is 10%.
Medical costs = number of claims * cost per claim.
Potential reasons for higher medical costs: low deductibles, higher procedure payments, older or sicker insured population.
Administrative costs are also higher, driven by high cost of sales through independent agents and General Agencies.
Commission paid is 10% of premiums, totaling $150 million annually.
Potential approaches to reduce sales costs include reducing commission percentage, capping commissions, or changing commission structure to flat fee.
General Agencies perform training, application processing, and performance management costing $30 million annually.
If total commission is set at $20 per member per month, maximum payment to General Agencies is $5 per member per month.
solution: Thompson Healthcare should take initiatives to reduce both medical and administrative costs. Recommendations include enhancing marketing to attract younger, healthier customers; conducting benchmarking to reduce payments for medical services; and changing commission structure to a flat fee per member per month to reduce expenses while maintaining agent incentives.
evaluation: Candidate should structure the problem into medical and administrative costs, analyze causes of cost increases, quantify commission expenses, propose cost reduction strategies, consider risks of agent behavior changes, and synthesize recommendations clearly.

title: Hot Air Balloons
description: Your client is TakeFlight, a company that sells hot air balloon rides out West. The business has expanded to offer two rides, twice per day, rather than one ride, twice per day, but has not experienced the economies of scale expected. You have been hired to target strategies to grow TakeFlight’s profit margins to support future growth.
additional_info: Purchasing a hot air balloon ride is all-inclusive: watching the balloon inflate, up to 15 passengers ride for approximately 1 hour, and a car ride back from the landing zone to the starting point.
The entire process takes 3 hours.
Doubling sales has not reduced margins at all.
The balloon ride costs the same for customers, even if only 2 people ride.
TakeFlight owns three balloons, allowing one back-up.
They have 2 inflation devices because they launch two balloons simultaneously.
The balloon lands wherever the wind takes it, and several chase vehicles pick up passengers.
Previously, two 9-passenger vans were used for one flight; now four vans per flight are used.
Gas/fuel prices and salaries have not risen recently.
Margins are currently $500 and 10%.
School bus maintenance costs $15 per use; vans cost $20 per use.
Gas costs per use: vans $60 total, bus $40 total.
Labor cost per driver is $75; four drivers currently employed for vans.
additional_questions: What are the factors you would investigate to determine opportunities for margin expansion?
TakeFlight has determined its pricing is optimal, so it cannot improve margins by growing revenues. Can you determine opportunities for reducing costs?
How would you go about cutting costs for the return trip after landing the balloon? What cost savings would TakeFlight experience?

TakeFlight management decides that the best option is to invest in a school bus. Could you calculate TakeFlight’s new margin after implementing the school bus? If the school bus costs $50,000, how many flight days does it take for the school bus to pay itself off?
solution: The root cause of declining profitability is that costs have doubled with the doubling of flights, without economies of scale. Pricing is optimal, so focus is on cost cutting. The main cost savings come from consolidating return vehicles from four vans to one school bus, reducing labor, gas, and maintenance costs. This increases margins from 10% to 15%, adding $250 margin per flight. The school bus pays for itself in 100 flight days. Selling off vans could finance the bus. Future growth options include adding post-flight refreshments, unbundling packages, and regional expansion.
evaluation: Candidate should systematically analyze pricing and cost factors, distinguish fixed vs variable costs, creatively propose cost-saving ideas, perform quantitative margin and payback calculations, and synthesize recommendations clearly.

title: Autoparts
description: Your client is CarCo, a family-owned mechanics and auto parts company in the Midwest. Historically generating $25 million in profits annually, profits have recently dropped to $10 million. The mechanic does not know what went wrong. You have been hired to assess the root cause of decreased profits and suggest strategies for restoring profits to previous levels.
additional_info: The company’s cost structure has held constant at 50%.
There are no relevant external market factors.
Total quantity of products/services sold has remained constant at 1 million.
Prices of offerings have not changed.
Product prices: Regular Tire $10, Oil Change $30, Engine Overhaul $200.
Margins are 50% across all product/service lines.
Proposed new product: Star Tires at $25 with 60% profit margin.
additional_questions: What are the factors you would investigate to determine why profits have declined?
What are the revenue streams you would expect this mechanic to have?
The mechanic sells a mix of products and services. Despite constant total quantity sold and prices, profits have declined. Can you explain what has happened and how to increase profits?
Your client wants to add Star Tires to its product line. What are the risks of selling Star Tires?
solution: The decline in profits is due to a shift in sales mix: customers are buying proportionally more low-margin products (tires, oil changes) and fewer high-margin engine overhauls. Since prices and total quantity sold are constant, this mix shift reduces profits. Strategies include increasing the customer base, expanding product offerings, adjusting pricing on engine overhauls, and marketing campaigns. Adding Star Tires could increase profits but risks include cannibalization, customer acceptance, brand impact, permanence of the product, and execution challenges.
evaluation: Candidate should identify revenue mix shift as root cause, demonstrate quantitative reasoning on profit impact, consider pricing and product strategy, and evaluate risks of new product introduction thoughtfully.

title: Health Insurance
description: Your client is FitCo, a health insurance company that has grown rapidly in the past five years, expanding into new markets with government subsidies. However, profitability has dropped sharply in the last three years. The CEO wants you to determine the root cause of declining profitability and suggest strategies for restoring profits.
additional_info: Average revenue per person insured has held constant at $5,000 over five years.
Average healthcare costs per person insured have risen from $3,500 to $4,500.
Number of insured has doubled from 1 million to 2 million.
Half of customers are now in new markets where costs are $5,500 per person.
For every 20% price increase in new markets, FitCo loses 20% of customers.
Current margin in new markets is negative $500 per person.
Price increase scenarios and their impact on customers, revenue, and profits are provided.
additional_questions: What are the factors you would investigate to determine why profits have declined?
Given revenues have held constant but costs have risen, what is the difference in profitability? How much must costs be lowered to restore prior profits?
What strategies would you pursue to grow margins? What would help determine whether to stay in new markets?
How high should FitCo price in new markets given the trade-off between price increases and customer loss? What other factors besides profits should be considered?
solution: Profit decline is driven by rising healthcare costs in new markets where FitCo is operating at a loss. To restore profits, FitCo must either reduce costs or raise prices. Raising prices by 40% in new markets yields similar total profits as before but loses 40% of customers. Strategies include segmenting markets to identify unprofitable segments, evaluating government subsidies, and balancing price increases with customer retention and brand considerations.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost drivers quantitatively, segment markets, evaluate pricing elasticity, consider qualitative factors like regulation and brand, and recommend balanced strategies.

title: Gold Mine
description: Your client is MyBank, a major financial institution managing a portfolio with annual returns of 10%. The Peruvian government is selling an untapped gold mine for $250 million. The bank wants to analyze whether this is a worthy investment.
additional_info: Mine development costs $50 million and takes one year.
Mine produces 150,000 ounces of gold annually.
Fixed costs are $25 million per year.
Variable cost per ounce is $200.
Market price of gold fluctuates between $1,200 and $1,800; use $1,200 for calculations.
There is a 50% chance the mine will be seized by the government.
Option 1: Increase fixed costs by $25 million to pay high wages, reducing seizure chance to 30%.
Option 2: Sell 40% stake to local investors for $100 million, reducing seizure chance to 10%.
Peruvian presidents serve 5-year terms; analysis over 5 years.
additional_questions: What factors would you consider in determining the worthiness of the investment?
Calculate expected profits given costs, production, and gold price.
What are the risks of this investment?
Evaluate options to reduce seizure risk and their expected values.
solution: Financially, the mine is profitable with expected annual profits of $125 million after development. The investment pays back in about 3 years and exceeds the 10% portfolio return over 5 years. Risks include political/regulatory risk of seizure, brand risk, gold price volatility, natural disasters, labor availability, and infrastructure. Option 2 (selling 40% stake) has the highest expected value ($437.5 million) despite dilution, followed by option 1 ($350 million), both better than the base case ($312.5 million). Qualitative factors like long-term control and government relations must be considered.
evaluation: Candidate should perform financial valuation, incorporate risk and probability, evaluate strategic options quantitatively and qualitatively, and communicate trade-offs clearly.

title: Data Storage
description: Your client is DataCo, a data storage company selling products and services primarily to businesses. DataCo has been an innovation leader but has recently seen declining profitability. You have been hired to assess the root cause and suggest strategies to restore profits.
additional_info: DataCo’s products include high-capacity storage devices and data servers.
Services include data back-up tools and cloud storage.
Data services market is growing faster than data products market.
R&D spending is split equally between products and services, not reflecting market trends.
Margins in data products have shrunk; SG&A costs in data products are up.
SG&A costs in data services have tripled due to poor cloud functionality and increased customer service.
Management offers $35 million investment in services with two options: (1) add cloud functionality, (2) expand reach to product-only customers.
Option 1 expected sales growth: 7.5%, SG&A cut by half, profit increase $55 million.
Option 2 expected sales growth: 10%, SG&A cut $50 million, profit increase $35 million.
additional_questions: What factors would you investigate to determine why profits have declined?
Is the cost increase avoidable? How should R&D spending be adjusted?
How would you approach deciding between investing in cloud functionality or expanding market reach?
Given many product buyers are technologically behind and unwilling to pay more, how should DataCo proceed?
solution: Profit decline is due to over-investment in declining product segment and under-investment in growing services segment. R&D spending should be reallocated to favor services. Investing $35 million in cloud functionality (option 1) yields higher profit increase than expanding market reach (option 2). Qualitative factors include customer retention, competitor dominance, and marketing integration. DataCo should focus on improving cloud functionality to remain competitive.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost trends, interpret market data, perform quantitative investment analysis, consider qualitative strategic factors, and recommend resource reallocation.

title: Bun on the Run
description: Our client, Bun on the Run, operates a fast food restaurant chain. Their fast food outlets are located on toll roads and in airports. The industry’s average profit margin is 3 to 5%. However, our client currently is making only 1.5%. How can you help them increase their margins to 3%?
additional_info: The airport outlets are franchises
Airports constitute most of their business
The low profit margins are due to their airport business
The client operates in 80% of airports, present in all large airports
They service 90% of all airport passengers
There is only one other competitor, who has the remaining 10% market share – their competitor’s margins are on par with the industry
Menu: Standard fast food menu; competitor is no different
Variable costs: 70% (COGS 35%, Labor 35%)
Fixed costs: 30% (Airport fee 10%, Infrastructure 5%, Rent 15%)
COGS core ingredients (bread, meat, etc): 70% of COGS, already optimized
COGS non-core ingredients (paper towels, ketchup, etc): 30% of COGS, contracted through SYSCO and costs are competitive
Labor: Most employees salaried and full time, earning minimum wage $6/hour; benefits start after 6 months; turnover 200%
New employee costs an additional $18/hour including training (4 weeks) and background check
High turnover due to employees trained then leaving for McDonalds for higher wages
solution: The root cause of low margins is high labor costs due to high turnover. The client should reduce turnover/increase retention rate to reduce costs and increase profit margins. Suggestions include making benefits begin earlier, raising wages to parity with McDonald's, training employees in multiple functional areas, qualitative improvements like flexible hours, bonuses for sales targets, and referral bonuses.
evaluation: Candidate identifies labor turnover as root cause, suggests multiple retention strategies, weighs pros and cons of each, and links retention improvements to margin increase.

title: Switching Costs
description: This company makes only one product – mechanical switches sold to companies operating heavy and complicated machines. The CEO believes there is an opportunity to improve profitability even further. How should this company increase profitability?
additional_info: Switches can only be used in specific industrial machinery
Switch failure can cause serious damage to customer equipment, so customers replace switches frequently
Customer equipment costs about $10MM per machine; each company operates several machines
Price is $12/switch; costs are $5/switch
One competitor with 60% market share sells switches at $15/switch
No real difference between client and competitor switches; products undifferentiated
Market share stable for years; excellent customer relationships
No new customers expected in foreseeable future
additional_questions: How do you expect the competition to react?
How high are you willing to risk going?
What would happen if a low-cost competitor came in at $6?
solution: Customer acquisition is expensive and unlikely; customer relationships create barriers to entry. Since price does not dictate market share and switches are marginal cost to customers, the company should increase prices to improve profitability, potentially up to or above competitor price depending on price elasticity.
evaluation: Candidate understands market dynamics, switching costs, customer relationship barriers, and supplier power; recommends price increase with rationale; considers competitive response and risk.

title: Fine China
description: Our client, FC Limited (Fine China Limited), manufactures sets of fine china sold only in sets at $1,500 per set. They sell 10,000 sets per year meeting demand exactly. A key competitor recently entered the industry with at least 50% higher margins. FC wants to match or exceed these margins. How should they do this?
additional_info: FC sells 10,000 sets per year; demand stable
Raw material costs (porcelain clay) are in-line or less than competitors
Manufacturing is efficient and slightly lower cost than competitors
Distribution: 6 warehouses (2 NC, 2 Ohio, 2 Southern California); china made in NC then shipped to warehouses
China sold only as sets through upscale retailers (Neiman Marcus, Saks Fifth Avenue)
Retailers do not hold inventory except one display set; orders shipped next day or delivered personally
Delivery requires 'white glove service' to avoid damage
Competitor located in Tennessee (Memphis), has one warehouse, uses FedEx for one-day delivery
Pricing is in-line or slightly higher than competitors; revenue not an issue
Current total costs $12 million; cost breakdown: Fixed Overhead 40%, Raw materials 20%, Labor 20%, Shipping 10%, Inventory 10%
Switching to overnight shipping (FedEx/UPS) would cut fixed overhead in half, labor down 25%, inventory down half, but shipping costs up 25%
solution: The current distribution system is inefficient and costly. By cutting out middle distribution warehouses and switching to overnight shipping, FC can reduce fixed overhead, labor, and inventory costs, more than offsetting increased shipping costs, resulting in margin improvement from 20% to about 42%.
evaluation: Candidate identifies cost drivers, quantifies impact of distribution changes, calculates new margin, and recommends pursuing overnight shipping and warehouse consolidation.

title: The End of the Road
description: GM sells vehicles to fleet customers and then manages resale of used fleet vehicles. GM piloted an online auction site allowing customers nationwide to buy fleet cars directly, increasing average sale price from $500 to $2,500. Should GM roll out this pilot more broadly?
additional_info: GM continues to sell other fleet vehicles through existing channels
Pilot cherry-picks best 30-50% of cars for online program
Auctioneer picks up car and refurbishes it
Vehicle transported to dealer local to customer
Customer test drives at local dealer and completes purchase
Auctioneer realizes same refurbishment margin but loses margin on sale to dealer
Dealer sells new and used GM cars, realizes same service margin but loses margin on sale to customer
Fleet sales comprise 10% of GM sales
Resale profits under old system: $25MM on 50,000 vehicles ($500 profit per vehicle)
GM costs under new system: $600 auctioneer refurbishment, $100 transportation, $400-$700 dealer transfer/warranty, $200 administration/online infrastructure
solution: New system yields $900-$1200 profit per vehicle (incremental $400-$700 plus original $500), more profitable for GM. However, auctioneers and dealers lose margin, risking channel disruption. Recommend a measured rollout monitoring channel effects or consider cancelling pilot to protect channel.
evaluation: Candidate calculates unit economics, understands channel margin shifts, considers strategic risks to channel partners, and recommends balanced rollout or cancellation.

title: Circuit Boards
description: CircuitNow refurbishes circuits for golf course irrigation devices but is struggling to replace circuits as fast as before. Golf courses rely on same-day or next-day service. Determine the root cause of slow delivery and suggest strategies to speed up service.
additional_info: Inventory problem: 50% of the time, not enough refurbished circuits to meet demand
Refurbishment process is slow; golf courses upgrading irrigation systems
Options to increase inventory: make circuits in-house (not possible), purchase/source circuits from suppliers or golf courses
Market value of a circuit is $5,000; CircuitNow margin per replacement is $500 * 8 = $4,000, so sourcing from suppliers unprofitable
Scrap value of irrigation machine is $36,000; $3,000 per circuit from golf courses is profitable
1 in 8 circuits dysfunctional rate could be lower if sourcing from early adopters
Risks: standard adopters may buy from early adopters at markdown (unlikely)
Opportunities: bundle buying scrap irrigation machines with refurbishment; higher scrap value could increase early adopters
additional_questions: What are the factors you would investigate to determine why replacement takes longer than before?
What are the options for CircuitNow to have more inventory on hand?
solution: Root cause is inventory shortage due to slow refurbishment. Best strategy is to source circuits from early adopters buying scrap irrigation machines, which is profitable and can reduce dysfunctional circuit rate. Candidate should consider risks and opportunities of this approach.
evaluation: Candidate identifies inventory as key issue, evaluates sourcing options with financial reasoning, considers risks and opportunities, and recommends sourcing from early adopters.

title: Zootopia
description: The National Zoo in Washington, D.C. is considering acquiring a panda bear. Should they do it?
additional_info: Zoo attendance is down slightly; management wants higher visitor traffic
Zoo is part of Smithsonian Institute and funded by federal government; profitability not a concern
Two or more pandas that can procreate is a longer-term solution and aligns with endangered species theme
Zoo can handle climate, exhibit space, training, feeding, and survival needs for pandas
Pandas would come from China via government negotiation, likely successful
Pandas available are 25-30 years old; pandas live 14-20 years in wild, up to 35 in captivity
Baby pandas will go back to China
Marketing ideas include newspaper ads, direct mail, email/text marketing, social media, live video feed, updates on panda's journey, blogs
additional_questions: What would you like to investigate?
What are ideas on marketing to increase foot traffic?
solution: Zoo should acquire multiple pandas to increase attendance and awareness. Marketing efforts should be robust and creative to maximize visitor traffic. The zoo has capability to care for pandas. The short lifespan of pandas should be considered but does not preclude acquisition.
evaluation: Candidate clarifies client goals, investigates feasibility, considers lifespan and sourcing, proposes marketing strategies, and balances attendance and awareness objectives.

title: Pharmaceutical Pricing
description: A pharmaceutical firm has developed an AIDS drug and has a rollout plan for developed countries. They want to develop a pricing strategy for the developing world, which includes very poor to near-developed nations. How should they price the drug for the developing world?
additional_info: Four broad regions to consider: China, Africa, Eastern Europe, Middle East
Key pricing factors: GDP per capita (willingness to pay), percent of population infected with HIV/AIDS
U.S.: high income, low AIDS rate
Eastern Europe: lower income than U.S., more AIDS incidence
China: lower income than Eastern Europe, similar AIDS incidence
Middle East: low income, low AIDS incidence due to religion
Africa: very poor, highest AIDS incidence
No countries with both high income and high AIDS incidence
China market sizing: population 1B, 1% AIDS, 20% reachable, 20% market share, $50/month price, market size ~$240MM
Africa: income below cost of manufacturing; options include charity, legal enforcement, controlled distribution, color-coded pills
Eastern Europe: risk of arbitrage to Western Europe; price set to prevent arbitrage
Middle East: AIDS not serious; can walk away
solution: Price drug differently by region based on income and AIDS prevalence. In Africa, charity or subsidized distribution is needed with controls to prevent smuggling. In Eastern Europe, price to prevent arbitrage. Middle East can be deprioritized. China represents a sizable paying market.
evaluation: Candidate segments market by region, uses relevant factors for pricing, considers social and regulatory issues, and proposes differentiated pricing and distribution strategies.

title: Megabank Underpenetration
description: Megabank issues credit cards and wants to grow its Hispanic market share in the U.S., where penetration is low compared to competitors. What are the possible reasons for underpenetration and how should the bank move forward?
additional_info: New cards sold via cross-sell to existing customers, direct mail campaigns, and private label partnerships
Hispanic population in US: 40 million; 3/8ths too young for credit cards
Average customer lifetime value $180; Hispanic customers worth 10% more
Current penetration 10%; goal 30% over 5 years
Cross-selling to branch customers is below industry norms (Megabank 5,000/month vs 15,000-20,000 average)
Direct mail response rate for Hispanics historically 3%, expected to decline by 1/3 each mailing; conversion rate 45%
Marketing/advertising, product definition, pricing, channel partners, internal sales incentives are areas to investigate
additional_questions: What do you think might be going wrong?
Calculate the number of additional members Megabank wants to add
How much would Megabank be willing to spend on that additional market share?
Why is cross-selling to branch customers below norms?
How many new customers should the bank expect after the third mailing?
solution: Underpenetration may be due to misaligned marketing, sales incentives, or channel issues. Direct mail alone will not meet growth goals (~450,000 new customers vs needed 1 million). Bank should explore sales force training, compensation, and targeted marketing to increase penetration.
evaluation: Candidate identifies potential root causes, performs market sizing and financial calculations, analyzes marketing effectiveness, and recommends multi-pronged approach to increase penetration.

title: Everlasting Light Bulb
description: You have invented the world’s first everlasting light bulb with a rock-solid patent. The current light bulb industry is a global monopoly. How much is your patent worth?
additional_info: Patent is eternal
Domestic light bulb only; no commercial applications
7B people in world; 4B have electricity
Average household size 8 people
Conventional bulbs priced at $2, last 2 years; everlasting bulbs priced at $5
Assume everyone switches to everlasting bulb in year one
Profit margin 20% on both bulb types
Discount rate 5%
solution: Market size: 500M households (4B/8), 5 rooms per household, 2 bulbs per room = 5B bulbs. Industry worth $5B annually in profit ($10B sales / 2 years * 20% margin). Everlasting bulb is a one-time purchase worth $5B profit. Patent value is about $4.5B considering lack of sales infrastructure and monopoly buyer dynamics.
evaluation: Candidate performs market sizing, understands one-time vs recurring sales, applies discounting, and reasons through monopoly and infrastructure effects on patent valuation.

title: The Wealthy Barber’s Banker
description: Your client is one of the major Canadian banks. In recent years, they have found that growth in their current businesses has not been as fast as they would like and recent regulatory changes have begun squeezing their margins. As such, they would like to move into the wealth management business as a way of generating additional profits and have hired us to figure out how to do so.
additional_info: Do the other major Canadian banks have a wealth management offering? Yes, all competitors have all our services plus wealth management.
How concentrated is the market? The other major banks hold an 80% market share, with independent advisors holding 20%.
What is wealth management? A client meets with an advisor to discuss their investment needs, then the advisor invests in specific securities on the clients behalf.
How do you make money? Typically fees are a percentage of assets under management (AUM).
What does our bank currently do to make money? Retail banking, corporate banking, payments, and insurance.
What type of clients do banks typically serve? All types, but some cater to certain needs of either high or low net-worth clients.
Each FTE costs $100,000 per year (inclusive of salary).
solution: The bank should move into wealth management focusing on high and super-high net-worth clients, as retail clients are not profitable. Serving HNW and SHNW clients will add $1.5M in additional profit. The bank should personalize services and consider creating a package to serve all client needs at once. Risks include adapting to the fast-moving digital banking space. Next steps include leveraging existing bank clients through promotions and special offers.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate's ability to identify profitability drivers, segment customers, perform quantitative analysis on profitability per segment, creatively design service offerings, and synthesize a clear recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: New Flight Plan
description: Your client is an airline. Competition is fierce among the major airlines, with ticket prices being driven steadily downward. After a year of weaker than average growth, they have hired our firm to devise a set of strategies for growth. Assume management’s goal is profitable revenue growth, rather than cost cutting.
additional_info: There are over ten competitors in the market, each holding a small share – no dominant player in the market. Some flight routes are controlled by only one or two competitors.
The client has not specified a growth target but is more interested in finding ways to increase profit, not any specific number.
The client charges for the price of a seat, including first, business, and economy class as well as the time to fly – all inclusive in one ticket price.
The client broadly serves two types of customers: Vacationers and Business Travelers.
Quality of service is highly important to business travellers; vacation travellers care most about price and are highly price sensitive.
Airplanes are almost fully booked during the winter season and relatively empty during the summer.
Unbundling baggage cost from ticket price is likely to increase sales.
Price elasticity data shows revenue maximized at $700 per seat with 70% occupancy.
additional_questions: What matters most to the client’s customers?
What do you notice about the company’s cyclicality? What strategies could you use to leverage this fact?
What do you notice about pricing? What would you change about ticket pricing to increase demand?
What do you notice about price elasticity? How can you use this information to find the optimal pricing outcome?
Given all the information, what is your conclusion and recommendation?
solution: The client should lower the listed price through unbundling baggage and other services to capture the price-sensitive vacation market. Invest in increasing service level for first-class seats to differentiate for business travelers. Grow demand in summer months through marketing, promotions, and sales. Key risks include maintaining enough seat utilization to support pricing and competitive threats undercutting price. Next steps include increasing summer marketing and piloting ticket bundling changes.
evaluation: Assess candidate’s ability to segment customers, analyze qualitative and quantitative data, identify pricing strategies, consider seasonality effects, and synthesize a clear, actionable recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: Alcoholic Farmer
description: Your client is a farmer who has recently inherited 20 acres of land from a distant relative. Since he has little business knowledge, he has hired you to determine how to get the most profit out of the land.
additional_info: The land location does not impact the case and is extremely arable.
The farmer wants to farm the land, not use it for any alternative such as leasing or selling.
The farmer is a simple person and simply wants to earn a profit from the inherited land.
Annual demand, price per ton, production per acre, and cost per acre data are provided for five crops: potatoes, barley, corn, agave, and sugarcane.
Each crop’s profitability per acre and maximum acres based on demand are calculated.
The farmer has 20 acres to allocate.
solution: The farmer should produce agave, barley, and sugarcane to distill into tequila, whiskey, and rum. Allocate 4 acres to agave, 8 acres to barley, and 8 acres to sugarcane for maximum annual profits of $136,000. Consider fixed costs that might affect crop choice.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate’s ability to ask clarifying questions, apply profitability frameworks, perform quantitative analysis including capacity constraints, and synthesize a clear recommendation with considerations.

title: New Club in the Hub
description: An investor has purchased a building in Downtown Kingston and is considering using the space for a new nightclub. They come to you and ask for help assessing the viability of a new nightclub in this market. What do you tell your new client?
additional_info: Key nightlife incumbents include Ale House (two-floor nightclub focusing on conference social business), Stages (dance club with occasional big-name musicians), The Spot (low-cost nightclub with cheap drinks), and Undies (student nightclub on-campus with no cover and cheap drinks).
Queen’s University has 15,000 undergraduate students (8,000 female, 7,000 male).
No club currently charges more than $5 for cover or $10 for a drink on regular nights; special event ticket prices can reach $40.
solution: Candidate should analyze the competitive landscape, customer segmentation, and willingness to pay. They should assess market size, pricing strategy, and differentiation opportunities to determine viability. A structured framework considering competitors, customers, pricing, and operational factors is recommended.
evaluation: Assess candidate’s ability to identify key market players, segment customers, analyze pricing and willingness to pay, and develop a structured approach to assess market viability.

title: New Club in the Hub
description: A new nightclub is opening with 6000 square feet of space (1/3 unusable). Maximum capacity is 1 person per 10 square feet of usable space. The club reaches capacity at midnight, and every half-hour after midnight, 100 people leave and are replaced by 100 new people until closing at 2 AM. Each person purchases two shot-equivalents on average. Bottles cost $10 and contain 25 shots. Cover charge is $5 and drinks cost $5 per shot-equivalent. Calculate the nightclub’s nightly profits. Then evaluate two alternative pricing strategies: (1) free cover before midnight with one extra shot purchased on average, and (2) discounted cover charge of $2 for girls, with 300 females and 500 males attending, where girls purchase one extra shot on average. Which pricing strategy yields the most revenue?
additional_info: Total floor space is 6000 sq ft, with 1/3 unusable.
Maximum capacity is 1 person per 10 sq ft of usable space.
Club reaches capacity at midnight (400 people).
Every half-hour after midnight, 100 people leave and are replaced by 100 new people until 2 AM.
Average person purchases 2 shot-equivalents per night.
Bottles cost $10 and contain 25 shots.
Cover charge is $5 per person and drinks cost $5 per shot-equivalent.
Pricing strategy 1: free cover before midnight, with one extra shot purchased on average.
Pricing strategy 2: discounted cover charge of $2 for girls; 300 females and 500 males attend; girls purchase one extra shot on average.
solution: Base case profit is $11,360 per night. Pricing strategy 1 reduces profits to $11,200 due to increased costs from extra shots. Pricing strategy 2 increases profits to $11,840 by charging girls $2 cover and gaining revenue from extra shots. Therefore, pricing strategy 2 yields the most revenue.
evaluation: Candidate should correctly calculate capacity, throughput, revenues, costs, and profits. They should analyze the impact of pricing strategies on revenues and costs, and recommend the most profitable strategy with clear reasoning.

title: Too Dollar Store
description: Your client owns a chain of dollar stores in the US. Despite revenue growth, profit growth has lagged. Investigate the profitability issue focusing on costs. The top 5 SKUs make up 80% of revenues and profits. Product B’s variable cost exceeds its price. Calculate total profit and evaluate two pricing strategies: (1) move away from $1 pricing to varied prices reflecting costs, with expected changes in unit sales, and (2) cut least profitable products (B and E) and assess impact on sales of other products. Recommend the best strategy.
additional_info: Overall retail sales growth in US is 0.5%, discount stores 4%, client 5.5% CAGR.
Client is smallest of four major dollar store chains.
Two chains use all $1 pricing; two use $1-$5 pricing.
COGS ~80% of sales on average, varies by product.
Top 5 SKUs make up 80% of revenues and profits.
Product A-E unit sales, prices, and costs provided in Exhibit 1.
Pricing strategy 1: raise prices of A, B, E to $1.50; lower D to $0.50; unit sales expected to change accordingly.
Pricing strategy 2: eliminate products B and E; sales of A, C, D expected to increase.
solution: Current total profit is $20.5M. Pricing strategy 1 increases profit to $44.9M by aligning prices with costs and adjusting sales volumes. Pricing strategy 2 increases profit to $36.4M by cutting unprofitable products and shifting sales. The best strategy is pricing strategy 1, moving to cost-based pricing, which more than doubles profits.
evaluation: Candidate should identify unprofitable products, calculate total profits, analyze impact of pricing and product elimination strategies on unit sales and profits, and recommend the best approach with supporting calculations.

title: Unprofitable Potash
description: Your client, Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, has record sales volumes but profits have slipped recently. Investigate the cause and propose a fix. Sales volumes are at an all-time high, costs have not increased, but profits declined. Analyze product mix changes and margins. Consider eliminating the freight discount on offshore potash by moving distribution in-house and calculate the maximum investment justified.
additional_info: Client mines potash, phosphate, nitrogen, and other fertilizers.
Industry is fragmented and commoditized.
Company switched to cheaper offshore potash, which grew 50% from 2012 to 2013.
Customers pay their own freight; offshore potash is discounted due to higher freight costs.
Prices and costs of individual products unchanged.
Gross margins: Potash $250/tonne, Nitrogen $150/tonne, Phosphate $100/tonne.
Exhibit 1: Sales mix changes from 2012 to 2013.
Exhibit 2: Sales volumes for North American and offshore potash.
Freight discount on offshore potash is $80/tonne; moving distribution in-house costs $50 variable per tonne plus fixed depreciation.
solution: Profit decline is due to shift from high-margin potash to lower-margin phosphate, reducing average gross margin by 10%. Offshore potash sales cannibalize North American potash due to freight discount. Eliminating the $80/tonne freight discount by moving distribution in-house at $50 variable cost per tonne justifies up to $810 million investment (depreciated over 10 years) to maintain profitability.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze product mix and margin changes, calculate impact on profits, understand freight discount effects, and perform investment justification calculations.

title: The Value of Loyalty
description: Southwest Air CEO wants to institute a loyalty program to boost deluxe seat sales. Evaluate the proposal by calculating incremental revenues and costs from the program. Business travelers earn 1 point per mile flown in deluxe seats; 10,000 points redeemable for a free deluxe flight. Deluxe seats cost $500 plus $2/mile; regular seats cost $300 plus $1/mile. After program, business travelers take deluxe seats 75% of the time; recreational travelers 50%. Calculate profitability and suggest improvements.
additional_info: Business travelers fly 10,000 miles/year, average flight 250 miles, currently 50% deluxe seats.
Recreational travelers fly 4,000 miles/year, average flight 1,000 miles, currently 25% deluxe seats.
Points earned equal miles flown in deluxe seats.
Points redeemable at 10,000 points per free deluxe flight.
After program, business travelers increase deluxe seat usage to 75%, recreational to 50%.
Exhibit 1: Customer segmentation with miles flown, flight distance, and deluxe seat percentage.
additional_questions: Is there anything missing in the analysis?
What ways could the program be improved?
solution: The program increases profits by $3,750 per business traveler and $700 per recreational traveler annually. Costs include the value of free flights redeemed. Improvements include offering lower-cost rewards redeemable at fewer points to reduce costs and increase profitability.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate revenues before and after program, costs of free flights, incremental profits, identify assumptions and missing factors, and creatively suggest program improvements.

title: Bird Blows Up
description: Bird Construction’s profits have fallen despite recovering industrial demand and rising revenues. Investigate the cause focusing on revenue and cost segments. The Road Work segment revenues rose, but profits fell. Analyze cost breakdowns, asphalt mixtures, and pricing. Propose ways to reduce costs and improve profitability.
additional_info: Road Work segment revenues increased; maintenance work declined; new road work increased.
Bidding department evaluated on number of bids won, separate from finance.
New roads take 3x time and materials per km compared to maintenance.
New roads use different asphalt mixture with higher raw material costs.
Exhibit 1: Cost breakdown by segment.
Exhibit 2: Asphalt mixtures for maintenance and new roads.
Exhibit 3: Average price chart for services.
Exhibit 4: Construction volumes by year.
Exhibit 5: Roadwork cost data.
additional_questions: Propose ways to reduce costs and improve profitability.
solution: Profit decline driven by high raw material costs in new roads, which cost more than revenue per km. Recommendations include incentivizing profitable bids, adjusting asphalt mixtures to reduce costs, and raising minimum bid prices to ensure profitability. Risks include quality degradation and raw material sourcing issues.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost trends, identify cost drivers, compare margins, and creatively propose cost reduction strategies with consideration of risks.

title: Big Yellow Buses
description: Big Yellow runs a school bus fleet with $200M annual revenue. CEO promises $300M revenue next year. Core business growth projected at 5%. Investigate other growth sources. Consider organic growth by offering senior shuttle bus service during downtime at no extra bus cost, and inorganic growth via acquisitions in the coach bus market. Evaluate acquisition targets under a $50M capital constraint and recommend growth strategy.
additional_info: Clients are district school boards and private schools; contracts won by competitive bidding.
Cannot improve bidding strategy beyond 5% growth.
Company can invest up to $50M.
School buses operate 7AM-10AM and 2PM-5PM weekdays; school year is 40 weeks.
Charge $70/hour for school bus service; senior shuttle bus could fill downtime at $35/hour with no extra buses.
Exhibit 1: Charter school bus industry competitive landscape.
Exhibit 2: Acquisition target financials and projections.
solution: Core business growth yields $10M revenue. Senior shuttle bus service adds $70M organically. Remaining $20M growth requires acquisition. Deluxe Bus exceeds capital limit and is excluded. Either Coach4You or FirstCoach acquisition meets growth needs; FirstCoach preferred for higher projected growth. Recommend combined organic and inorganic growth.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate bus fleet size, revenue potential from new services, evaluate acquisition targets under capital constraints, and recommend balanced growth strategy with risks.

title: Who Needs Flight Simulators?
description: CAE Inc., a Canadian flight simulator manufacturer, faces slowing growth, especially in military segment. CEO wants to grow revenues profitably. Explore growth opportunities: selling more to current customers, new products, and new markets. Four new segments interested: medical, architectural, geological, electrical. Client can enter two segments at most. Evaluate projected revenues and break-even for new training services. Recommend growth strategy.
additional_info: Civil flight simulators growing at 3% CAGR; CAE growing at 5%.
Military simulators declining at -10% CAGR; CAE declining at 8%.
Market research indicates no further growth rate increase possible.
New segments interested: medical, architectural, geological, electrical.
Costs of serving segments are equal; revenues only focus.
Training service: $500 monthly fee, $100 labor/customer/month, $500,000 machines depreciated over 10 years, $100,000 marketing, $90,000 admin.
Exhibit 1: Projected annual revenues for new segments.
additional_questions: What are possible growth opportunities for CAE?
Select best growth opportunity and provide rationale.
solution: Recommend pursuing new products with aviation training service (break-even at 50 customers) and entering architectural and geological segments for $1.775M additional annual revenue. Risks include internal expertise and meeting break-even customer numbers.
evaluation: Candidate should identify growth avenues, analyze revenue projections, perform break-even calculations, and recommend optimal segments with rationale and risk considerations.

title: Keeping the Company Intact
description: Intact Financial, a leading Canadian insurer, faces slow growth in insurance premiums and low investment returns. Tasked with growing gross premiums earned by the insurance division. Focus on growing premiums by selling more insurance to current segments or entering new segments. Specialty insurance is a new segment. Evaluate pricing and distribution strategies for specialty insurance and recommend approach.
additional_info: Client operates in all insurance areas except specialty.
Exhibit 1: Proposed pricing strategies for specialty insurance.
Exhibit 2: Proposed distribution strategies for specialty insurance.
BrokerLink and Brokers yield same net income but differ in control and expertise.
solution: Entering specialty insurance can grow premiums. BrokerLink and Brokers distribution strategies yield similar net income, but BrokerLink requires broker expertise to sell, while brokers outsource control. Candidate should recommend based on control vs. expertise trade-off.
evaluation: Candidate should explore growth options, analyze pricing and distribution trade-offs, and recommend a strategy considering control, expertise, and market entry risks.

title: Credit Disunion
description: Compare client performance to similar credit unions in revenue generation and profit in both banking and payments divisions. Identify issues and recommend solutions to improve profitability.
additional_info: Banking Analysis: Revenue per account is $2000, profit margin is 5% which is lower than competitors implying higher costs. Major costs include funding costs (cost of money loaned out).
Exhibit 1: Deposits should be 100% of assets, but client branches have mismatched deposits causing some to fund loans with expensive short-term debt while others have unused deposits.
Payment Analysis: Revenue per transaction is $1, below competitors at $1.50. Profit margin is 10%, in line with competitors.
Exhibit 2: Richest customers disproportionately use more expensive services; client customer profile is 0.25% richest 2%, 9.75% next 18%, 90% poorest 80%.

Solution hints: Even out deposits by borrowing deposits at a rate between unused deposit earnings and short-term debt interest; attract wealthier clients possibly by partnering with credit unions in other regions.
solution: Two main issues: high funding costs in banking and low revenue per transaction in payments. Recommendations: allow branches to lend more easily to each other to reduce unused deposits and reliance on short-term debt; attract wealthy customers by partnering with credit unions in other regions and creating unique products.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate's ability to identify cost drivers and revenue issues, use exhibits to generate insights, propose practical solutions addressing both banking and payments divisions, and consider risks and implementation challenges.

title: Calling the TTC
description: Rogers Wireless wants to build a wireless network offering full coverage in the TTC subway. Evaluate the proposal including market sizing, breakeven analysis, qualitative feasibility, and operational considerations.
additional_info: Toronto population is 3 million, growing at 5% CAGR.
Rogers has 40% market share with 8 million Canadian subscribers; Telus and Bell have about 25% each.
Customers are price sensitive but value coverage and service highly.
Rogers will fund investment internally.
TTC subway is 40 km long; equipment costs $1 million per km.
One-time fee of $20 million and annual fee of $1 million to TTC.
Payback period is 4 years.
Installation only during scheduled TTC track repairs.
Daily subway riders estimated at 1 million rides, representing 400,000 unique riders.
Rogers has 160,000 subway riders (40% market share).
Breakeven price per user is about $8.33 per month initially.
Partnering with another carrier halves costs and breakeven price.
Spreading cost across all Rogers subscribers reduces price impact.
Cost savings from reduced customer churn can lower breakeven price.
additional_questions: What qualitative factors affect customer acceptance of the price increase?
How can Rogers reduce the breakeven price or improve feasibility?
What operational challenges might Rogers face and how can they be mitigated?
solution: There is a substantial market of 160,000 Rogers subscribers using the subway daily. Although partnering reduces costs, Rogers should pursue the project alone to maximize customer loyalty benefits. Start installation in high-traffic areas during TTC track repairs. Risks include project delays due to TTC unreliability.
evaluation: Assess candidate's ability to perform logical market sizing, breakeven calculations, consider qualitative factors, propose cost reduction strategies, and identify operational risks and mitigation plans.

title: Diamonds are Forever
description: Forever Diamond, a diamond company with mining operations in Africa, wants to enter the Canadian diamond market in both mining and retail sectors. Determine how they should enter the market, including which mines and retail acquisitions to pursue.
additional_info: Mining sector goal: maximize absolute probability of finding diamonds with a $250 million budget (no benefit spending less).
Retail sector goals: maximize cash on hand at end of year 1 and achieve best ROI by year 5 with a $300 million budget (unspent money counts as cash on hand).
Available mines: Yellowknife ($200M, 50% chance), Whitehorse ($210M, 55%), Watson Lake ($220M, 60%).
Equipment: Excavators ($30M, +10% probability), Seismic sensors ($50M, +25%).
Retail acquisition targets: People's Diamonds ($250M, $225M Y1 revenue, 20% growth), Ben Moss ($275M, $275M Y1 revenue, 20% growth), W.K. Chan ($300M, $290M Y1 revenue, 10% growth).
Branding challenge: Ben Moss is a low-end brand; Forever Diamond is high-end.
additional_questions: Which mine and technology combination is best?
Which retailer should the client acquire?
How can Forever Diamond align Ben Moss with its high-end brand?
solution: Mining: Purchase Yellowknife mine with seismic sensors for a 75% chance of finding diamonds. Retail: Acquire Ben Moss for highest cash on hand and ROI. To address branding, rebrand Ben Moss stores to Forever Diamond, improve customer experience, launch marketing campaigns, and establish community partnerships.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate's quantitative analysis of probabilities and ROI, ability to balance budget constraints, and creativity in addressing branding challenges.

title: Professional Football Team Profitability
description: Your client owns a professional football team that has just won the Super Bowl. Despite the Super Bowl victory, the team finished the season with a financial loss for the 5th season in a row. What could be the cause of the team’s profitability problems? What would you recommend to reverse this past season’s financial performance?
solution: Analyze revenue streams such as ticket sales, merchandise, sponsorships, and broadcasting rights. Investigate cost drivers including player salaries, stadium costs, and operational expenses. Identify areas where revenues can be increased or costs reduced. Recommendations may include improving marketing to boost attendance, renegotiating sponsorship deals, optimizing player contracts, or enhancing merchandise sales.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to break down profitability into revenues and costs, identify key drivers, ask relevant clarifying questions, and propose actionable recommendations. Structured thinking and creativity in identifying revenue and cost levers are important.

title: Towing Company Sale Decision
description: You are the owner of a small, but relatively successful, towing company with a handful locations throughout the Southeastern U.S. Two recent MBA graduates have approached you about purchasing the company. Should you accept the offer to sell your company?
solution: Evaluate the offer price relative to the company’s valuation based on earnings, assets, and growth potential. Consider future prospects of the business, competitive landscape, and personal goals. Analyze financials, market position, and potential risks. Decision depends on whether the offer reflects fair value and aligns with owner’s objectives.
evaluation: Candidate should assess valuation methods, consider strategic and personal factors, and demonstrate structured approach to decision-making. Ability to weigh pros and cons and ask clarifying questions is key.

title: Security Software Company Acquisition
description: Your client is an industry-leading security software company that is considering acquiring a data management software company. Should the client go through with the acquisition?
solution: Analyze financials of the target company, potential synergies in product lines, market expansion opportunities, and cultural fit. Consider risks such as integration challenges and market reaction. Evaluate whether acquisition will create value and align with client’s strategic goals.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to evaluate M&A financials, synergies, risks, and strategic fit. Structured approach to decision-making and relevant clarifying questions are important.

title: Fitness Chain Sub-Brand for Small Towns
description: Your client is a national chain of fitness centers with locations in 35 major cities. The company is thinking of creating a sub-brand of gyms for the small-town market. Should they do it? What issues come into play when making this decision? Under what circumstances could the venture be successful?
solution: Assess market attractiveness of small-town gyms including size, demographics, and competition. Evaluate fit with company’s brand and operational capabilities. Consider pricing, marketing, and distribution strategies. Success depends on market demand, cost structure, and ability to differentiate the sub-brand.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market entry considerations, strategic fit, and operational challenges. Ability to identify key success factors and risks is critical.

title: Publishing Company New Sports Magazine
description: A publishing company that has traditionally created news and entertainment magazines is thinking of introducing a new sports-focused magazine. Should they do it?
solution: Evaluate market demand for sports magazines, competitive landscape, and potential readership. Analyze costs of launching and marketing the new magazine versus expected revenues and profitability. Consider brand fit and potential cannibalization of existing products.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to assess market opportunity, financial viability, and strategic fit. Structured approach and relevant clarifying questions are important.

title: Non-Profit Fundraising Problem
description: You have taken over as the Executive Director of a non-profit that has had trouble raising enough money to fund its programs. What could the problem be? What can be done to resolve the issue?
solution: Analyze fundraising strategies, donor base, marketing and outreach efforts, and program appeal. Identify gaps in donor engagement or communication. Recommend improvements in fundraising campaigns, donor relations, and possibly program adjustments to increase appeal.
evaluation: Candidate should identify key fundraising drivers, diagnose problems, and propose actionable solutions. Ability to think creatively and structure analysis is important.

title: City School District Academic Performance
description: You have just accepted a job as the Superintendent of city school district where more than 90% of the students have scored below proficient in English and Math for the past 15 years. How will you solve this long-standing academic performance problem? What resources would you need to implement your plan?
solution: Assess root causes including curriculum quality, teacher effectiveness, student socio-economic factors, and resource allocation. Develop a comprehensive improvement plan involving curriculum reform, teacher training, community engagement, and resource investment. Identify necessary funding, personnel, and partnerships.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to analyze complex social and operational issues, develop structured solutions, and consider resource requirements. Creativity and practical thinking are valued.

title: Beverage Company Brand Damage Control
description: Your client is a beverage company that was excited to see its sponsored cyclist win the Tour de France. That is, until authorities announced that the cyclist had tested positive for performance-enhancing drugs. What sort of “damage control” does the company have to do in light of this event? Will there be any negative effects on the company’s brand as a result of this discovery?
solution: Evaluate brand impact and public perception. Develop a crisis communication plan including public statements, distancing from the athlete, and reinforcing company values. Consider long-term brand rebuilding strategies. Negative effects likely include loss of consumer trust and sales decline.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate understanding of brand management, crisis response, and communication strategies. Ability to anticipate consequences and propose mitigation plans is key.

title: Enterprise Software Product Underperformance
description: Your client is an enterprise software company that has spent over $100M developing a new product that isn’t selling well. What could the problem be and what can they do to resolve the situation?
solution: Analyze product-market fit, pricing, sales strategy, competition, and customer feedback. Identify issues such as poor marketing, misaligned features, or pricing problems. Recommend adjustments in product features, marketing campaigns, sales incentives, or pricing strategies to improve sales.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to diagnose product underperformance, analyze market and internal factors, and propose actionable solutions. Structured thinking and relevant clarifying questions are important.

title: Franchisee Decision for Fast-Food Chain
description: A friend of yours is considering becoming a franchisee of a national fast-food chain. What issues should he be thinking about in making his decision? And, do you think he should open the franchise?
solution: Evaluate franchise costs, fees, expected revenues, market demand, competition, and operational challenges. Consider personal fit and risk tolerance. Decision depends on financial viability and alignment with personal goals.
evaluation: Candidate should identify key factors in franchise evaluation, including financial, operational, and strategic considerations. Ability to structure decision-making and ask clarifying questions is important.

title: Jams & Jelly Company
description: Our client is a jam & jelly manufacturer in the New England area. They have experienced a drop in profitability over the past few years. Why has profitability fallen?
additional_info: Profitability decreased 2 years ago.
Firm is a single store operation. Niche player so no major competitors.
Product is a premium jam / jelly product line.
Small operation, with no new capital, labor, operations investment expenditures in the past few years.
Revenues have increased.
Volume has gone up.
Prices have stayed the same.
Product mix has stayed the same.
No changes in direct material, labor, overhead. But transportation costs have risen.
Transportation change was instituted a little over 2 years ago (26 months ago).
Shipping was done to allow for sales in New York and Boston.
Another local firm does the distribution. They are charging us at the rate of transportation, no profit for them. We pass these costs on to our customers.
Bad products are returned with a full refund.
Jellies and jams go bad with low temperatures.
solution: The drop in profitability is due to increased transportation costs caused by a change in distribution method implemented about 26 months ago. The new distribution method involves freezing the products during shipping, which spoils the jams and jellies, leading to returns and losses. The recommendation is to stop this transportation method and find an alternative that does not spoil the product.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate's ability to structure a profitability problem, analyze revenue and cost components, identify key changes (transportation method), and logically conclude the root cause. Look for use of frameworks like profitability analysis and 4Ps. Assess communication and hypothesis testing.

title: Airline Call Center
description: Our client, Airline A, is one of two airline companies (duopoly) in Australia. Both companies are identical in every aspect and have a 50%, 50% market share. For year X, Airline projected revenue of 200MM. An audit reveals that their revenue has dropped to 180MM, a 20MM reduction. What happened and how can we rectify the situation?
additional_info: No new competition or regulatory changes.
No major changes in customer behavior or segments.
No new substitutes.
Same prices / promotions by both airlines.
Same in-flight services by both as last year.
No new organizational changes or new investments.
We sell tickets through agents (20%) and through call centers (80%).
Client decided to sell holiday packages in addition to tickets.
Call time increased to 3 minutes. Each call center has 20 operators. Old time was 2 minutes.
additional_questions: What happened and how can we rectify the situation?
What are the external factors affecting revenue?
What internal factors could have changed?
How does the call center operation affect revenue?
How can we analyze call center capacity and its impact on lost revenue?
What recommendations would you make to solve the problem?
solution: The revenue drop is caused by increased call times at the call centers (from 2 to 3 minutes) without increasing the number of operators, leading to capacity constraints. Customers experience long wait times and abandon calls, resulting in lost sales. The solution is to increase call center capacity (more operators or reduce call time) to handle demand and recover lost revenue.
evaluation: Assess candidate's ability to structure the problem into external/internal factors, prioritize issues, use quantitative analysis to evaluate call center capacity, interpret data (calls per hour), and develop actionable recommendations. Look for hypothesis-driven approach and quantitative rigor.

title: Country Music Cable Network Magazine Launch
description: Your client is considering launching a magazine targeted at country music cable network subscribers. Assess the potential profitability and strategic implications of entering this new market.
additional_info: There are 25 million households subscribing to country music cable networks; 60% subscribe to our client.
80% of our client’s cable network subscribers would subscribe to the magazine; 20% of other cable network subscribers would subscribe.
Subscription price is $4 per magazine, published monthly.
Advertisement revenue is about 50% of subscription revenue.
Competitor has a 12% margin in the magazine business; assume client’s margin is 10%.
Client’s current profit is about $300 million.
solution: The magazine business would generate approximately $1,008 million in revenue and about $100 million in profit, which is roughly one-third of the client’s current profit. The economics look promising, but qualitative factors such as competitor response, customer feedback, and client resources must be considered before proceeding.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate’s ability to estimate market size, perform profitability analysis, and discuss strategic considerations including competitive response and resource capabilities.

title: Eugene’s Famous Software Suite Profitability Analysis
description: Your client, Eugene’s Famous Software, is considering launching an integrated Suite of four software products at $1000, raising prices on two dominant products to motivate Suite purchases. Should the client proceed with the Suite strategy?
additional_info: Current prices and proposed price increases are detailed in Exhibit 2.
Concept test results on customer preferences are in Exhibit 1.
Variable costs per product are in Exhibit 2.
Sales volumes and market share data are in Exhibit 3.
Additional fixed costs and synergies are assumed to be a wash in the short term.
Assume all customers buy full versions; no upgrades or software lifecycles considered.
additional_questions: Will the proposed Suite strategy increase company profitability?
What are some additional long-term factors the company should consider in deciding on the Suite strategy?
solution: The Suite strategy is slightly more profitable than the status quo but not a clear winner due to cannibalization and price increases. Long-term considerations include leveraging market leadership, capturing growth in newer categories, and customer overlap to drive adoption of newer products.
evaluation: Assess candidate’s ability to analyze profitability including cannibalization, price effects, and to think strategically about long-term market opportunities and competitive dynamics.

title: Kenny’s Kapital Management Operations Optimization
description: Kenny’s Kapital hedge fund wants to improve its fundamental analysis throughput. Currently, only 100 of 250 shortlisted companies are analyzed annually. How should the fund optimize staffing to increase throughput without adding partners?
additional_info: Fundamental analysis involves Research (50%), Analysis (30%), and Strategy Formulation (20%).
Research: 20% must be done by partners, 60% by junior analysts and up, 20% by assistants and up.
Analysis: 30% must be done by partners, 70% by junior analysts and up.
Strategy Formulation: 60% must be done by partners, 40% by junior analysts and up.
Current staff: 3 partners, 4 junior analysts, 2 assistants.
Partners capped at 3; can hire junior analysts or assistants.
All staff equally productive at appropriate tasks.
additional_questions: What level and how many staff should be hired to increase throughput?
If 2 junior analysts are hired, how many companies can be analyzed annually?
What other ways might the fund optimize its procedure?
solution: Optimal staff ratio is 3 partners : 6 junior analysts : 1 assistant. Hiring 2 junior analysts increases throughput by about 29%, raising annual analyzed companies to 129. Further optimization includes prioritizing companies by expected return to analysis time ratio.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate’s ability to analyze operational bottlenecks, apply capacity planning, and consider prioritization for resource allocation.

title: Brinda’s Hair Styles Business Assessment and Improvement
description: Kate, founder of Hair Co., a premium hair products company, is considering selling her business. Assess Hair Co. from an investor’s perspective and recommend ways to improve the business.
additional_info: Hair Co. has four product lines: shampoos, conditioners, color products, styling products.
Sales primarily through high-end salons.
Financials show revenue growth but declining profitability from 2003 to 2004.
Styling products have lower margins and are impacting overall gross margin.
Sales & Marketing expenses growing faster than revenue.
R&D spending growth is low.
Salon market is saturating; new channels may be needed.
Math problem: Calculate required drug store price to achieve 10% growth given volume and price assumptions.
solution: Declining profitability is due to lower margins in styling products and increased sales & marketing costs. Recommendations include addressing the styling line (improve or drop), expanding into new sales channels (e.g., drug stores), and increasing R&D investment. Pricing strategy in new channels must consider cannibalization and costs.
evaluation: Assess candidate’s ability to analyze financial trends, identify root causes of profitability decline, and propose strategic growth and improvement initiatives.

title: Webb’s Bridge Building Project Valuation
description: The Buenos Aires transportation authority is considering building a new toll bridge to alleviate congestion. Should the bridge be built, and if so, what toll should be charged?
additional_info: Initial investment: $10 million; maintenance: $3 million/year; variable cost: $0.50 per crossing.
No taxes or government subsidies assumed.
Toll charged in both directions, must be whole dollar amounts.
Current bridge has 190,000 crossings per week; peak times 6AM-6PM weekdays have 20,000 crossings.
Willingness to pay among peak motorists: 40% unwilling, 30% up to $1, 20% up to $2, 5% up to $3, 5% up to $5.
Non-peak motorists’ willingness to pay varies daily; data provided.
Bridge must generate $4 million/year net revenue to break even over 10 years.
Toll revenue formula provided.
Spatial and usage data provided but not essential for valuation.
solution: The bridge project can be profitable. The profit-maximizing toll is $2 per crossing, generating approximately $5.4 million/year in net revenue, exceeding the $4 million/year break-even requirement.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate’s ability to gather and interpret data, perform valuation calculations, understand fixed vs. variable costs, and identify profit-maximizing pricing.

title: Hauffman’s Paper Capacity Expansion Decision
description: Hauffman’s Paper, the #2 US paper manufacturer, is considering increasing capacity by 50 tons/year to become #1. Should they proceed?
additional_info: Industry demand is flat; commodity market with no differentiation; price is key.
Competitors have full utilization except #6, who is at 40%.
Market clearing price is $60/ton; costs vary by competitor.
Increasing capacity may require lowering price, triggering competitive reactions.
Capital investment assumed costless for simplicity.
Potential to produce gift wrapping paper with excess capacity.
Considerations include new plant vs. existing facility, labor, economies of scale.
solution: Increasing capacity without cost may not yield incremental business due to flat demand. Lowering price to capture competitor #6’s share may trigger price wars and reduce margins. Alternative uses for excess capacity or market expansion should be considered.
evaluation: Assess candidate’s understanding of competitive dynamics in commodity markets, pricing impact, capacity utilization, and strategic trade-offs.

title: Lola Lo Zoo Dinosaur Investment and Exhibit Design
description: Lola Lo Zoo is considering purchasing a unique dinosaur exhibit. Should they invest in the dinosaur, and how should the exhibit be designed?
additional_info: Dinosaur is size of an elephant, can be transported from Africa, cannot reproduce, non-violent but requires trained handlers.
Zoo has sufficient land for exhibit without reconfiguration.
Revenues include tickets ($2M), hotel accommodations ($1M), licensing ($0.5M), food ($0.5M), merchandise ($0.25M), research ($0.25M).
Costs include exhibit construction ($15M fixed), staff ($1M variable), food & merchandise ($0.25M), operations ($0.25M).
Expected lifespan 50 years; current age 20 years.
Discount rate 10%.
NPV formula: NPV = Fixed Cost – [(revenues – costs) / discount rate].
Exhibit area size and traffic routing must be designed to optimize visitor experience and flow.
Example: round exhibit with 100-yard diameter; circumference = π * diameter.
Traffic flow should prevent congestion and ensure equal viewing opportunities.
additional_questions: How to determine if purchasing the dinosaur is a good project?
How to determine the purchase price?
How to design the exhibit area (size, traffic routing, shape)?
solution: Investment decision should be based on NPV considering revenues, costs, lifespan, and discount rate. Exhibit area should be large enough (e.g., football field size) to allow good viewing and visitor flow. Traffic should be routed to prevent congestion, possibly using controlled paths or multi-level viewing.
evaluation: Evaluate candidate’s ability to apply NPV analysis, identify relevant revenue and cost streams, and creatively design visitor experience and operational flow.

title: Starchaser
description: The CEO of Starchaser would like a strategy to reduce the overall costs associated with the construction of the Eagle-S rocket.
additional_info: Starchaser is a private company focused on developing a sustainable solution for achieving travel to space and different planets.
Their current rocket, dubbed the Eagle-S, has been making scheduled flights to the International Space Station delivering goods to its crew.
Due to the high-cost nature of space travel, the company has been plagued with large replacement costs to rebuild the rockets each time a new mission is scheduled.
The cost structure is mainly composed of Transportation & Logistics and this is where the issue lies.
Factories in Gulfport and Jackson have significantly higher transportation costs due to their locations.
Starchaser currently handles all their own logistics.
Third-party logistics providers are cheaper than in-house logistics.
Supplier profiles show different options with varying pricing, reliability, capacity, and coverage.
solution: The main cost issue lies in logistics, particularly transportation costs from factories in Gulfport and Jackson. Starchaser should consider outsourcing logistics to third-party providers, which is cheaper than handling logistics in-house. Among potential suppliers, Towing Trailers offers the best price without significantly compromising other metrics.
evaluation: Candidate should identify logistics as the main cost driver, analyze transportation costs by location, consider in-house vs third-party logistics cost differences, and recommend outsourcing logistics with supplier selection based on price and service metrics.

title: Noble Metals
description: The CEO of Noble Metals would like you to come up with a strategy to improve their profitability from their current state given that the price of gold and silver has been falling in recent years.
additional_info: Noble Metals is a Colorado-based mining company specializing in buying old mines and clearing out useful resources left behind.
Noble Metals has multiple mines across the western United States procuring gold, silver, and copper.
The price of silver has fallen 20% over the past two years, gold almost 10%.
Utilities costs are the highest cost component and include water, waste, and electricity.
Potential investments include energy efficient equipment, waste management, and water recycling with varying initial investments, monthly costs, and savings.
Noble management is looking for a return on investment of no less than 40%.
Additional factors influencing decision include regulatory changes, capital availability, and the ability to move improvements between mines.
solution: The profitability issue is driven by rising utilities costs, especially water. The best investment is water recycling, which has the highest ROI (54%) and addresses the fastest rising cost. Candidate should calculate ROI for each option and consider other factors such as regulatory environment and capital constraints.
evaluation: Candidate should use a profitability framework, identify utilities as the cost driver, analyze ROI for investment options, and consider qualitative factors affecting the decision.

title: Fancy Fishery
description: The CEO of Fancy Fishery would like you to identify the root cause of declining profitability due to declining sales orders and recommend a course of action to overcome this issue.
additional_info: Fancy Fishery is a mid-sized commercial fishing and processing company focusing on wild-fish capture in the Western Atlantic Ocean.
They are known for excellent catches of Blackfin Tuna and sustainable fishing practices.
Over the past two years, sales orders from customers have declined, lowering profitability.
Cost structure has remained stable over the past 5 years.
Fancy Fishery is the low-cost provider, which should ideally increase demand.
Customer orders from restaurants are declining heavily.
Customer preferences show that Fancy Fishery is catering to the wrong characteristics (price, portion size) rather than taste.
High-tier restaurants prioritize taste more than price or portion size.
Fancy Fishery must adjust its customer base from high-tier to mid or low-tier restaurants.
solution: The root cause of declining profitability is a mismatch between Fancy Fishery’s product characteristics and the preferences of their main customers (high-tier restaurants). The company should shift focus to mid or low-tier restaurants whose preferences align better with Fancy Fishery’s offerings.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost trends, identify customer preference mismatch, and recommend customer base adjustment to improve sales and profitability.

title: Screen Company
description: Screen Co. would like to figure out how best to leverage their new patented screen technology (Screen X) and make the most money.
additional_info: Screen Co. is a new player in the screen manufacturer industry.
They developed a new type of screen called Screen X, patented last year.
Screen Co. sells each panel to manufacturers at $100.
Total US TV market is estimated at 40 million TVs in 2015.
