Patent options include royalty deals, yearly licensing fees, or outright sale of the patent.
TV Co. offered a royalty of $20 per TV sold to be the exclusive manufacturer.
Mega TV Ltd. offered a yearly fee of $1 billion for exclusive rights.
TV Screen Inc. offered to buy all rights to the patent for $3 billion.
TV manufacturer market shares vary with TV Co. at 40%, Mega TV Ltd. at 45%, Bold Television Inc. at 10%, and others at 5%.
Market trends show a decreasing market size and competition.
solution: Candidate should evaluate the financial implications of each patent option. The royalty deal offers increasing revenue with market growth, while outright sale provides immediate cash but no future upside. The yearly licensing fee is a middle ground. The decision depends on market outlook and risk tolerance; either option can be justified if well defended.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market size, manufacturer shares, patent deal options, and defend a recommendation based on financial and strategic considerations.

title: Water & Bottle
description: Water & Bottle is considering launching a proprietary water filtering add-on to its existing water bottles and seeks input on whether this is a good idea.
additional_info: Water & Bottle is a leading OEM of water bottles in the US with growth stagnating at 5% due to competition.
Competitors are entering the market with lower premiums, often as ODMs manufacturing abroad with lower costs.
Water & Bottle is considering moving into ODM manufacturing.
Water & Bottle has produced 350,000 bottles; secondary manufacturers have produced 250,000.
Water & Bottle expects to capture 25% of the total market in year one and 35% in year two, with an additional 10% overall sales increase in year two due to the add-on.
Development costs for the add-on are $4.5 million; variable cost per unit is $5; selling price is $20.
Maximum payback period is two years.
Break-even point is 300,000 units; expected sales are below this.
CEO wants ideas to make the add-on work despite payback concerns.
Potential strategies include raising price, lowering costs, delaying launch, and marketing incentives.
CEO wants ideas to slow competitor encroachment.
BAB Contract Co. is a potential contract manufacturer; Water & Bottle wants to evaluate this option.
additional_questions: What might these other manufacturers be doing differently that allows them to command lower premiums?
What might Water & Bottle consider when moving into original design manufacturing?
How large of a market is Water & Bottle looking at for the add-on?
How would you determine if manufacturing and producing the add-on is a good idea?
What might be some ways to make the add-on work despite payback period concerns?
What might Water & Bottle do to slow down competitors’ encroachment upon its sales?
What would be the process to determine whether to sign a contract with BAB Contract Co.?
Please give an overview of your findings to the CEO.
solution: The add-on market size is under 300,000 units, below the breakeven point for a two-year payback, so launching is not financially advisable. To make it work, Water & Bottle could raise prices, reduce costs, delay launch to capture a larger market, or increase sales via marketing. To slow competitors, partnerships or IP protection could be considered. Contract manufacturing evaluation should include cost, quality, capacity, and reputational impact. Overall recommendation is not to launch the add-on alone but consider partnerships.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market sizing, profitability, breakeven analysis, strategic options to improve feasibility, competitive response strategies, and contract manufacturing evaluation.

title: Waste Not
description: Waste Not’s growth has slowed and competitors are outpacing them. The CEO wants an analysis of the situation and a strategy to overcome it.
additional_info: Waste Not is a leading waste management company headquartered in Chicago with service coverage from the Mississippi River to the Atlantic Ocean.
Services include commercial and residential waste pickup, medical waste, portable toilets, temporary dumpsters, and construction waste.
Population growth has driven profitability growth historically.
Recently, growth has slowed and competitors are growing faster.
Key factors to consider include revenue drivers (waste contracts, pricing), cost drivers (equipment, labor, overhead), frequency of pickups, competitor strategies, and services offered.
Decline in tons of waste processed, especially paper and plastics, is linked to loss of profitability growth.
Growth in construction and demolition waste services is significant.
Market entry into recycling is being considered with options: start from scratch, joint venture, merger/acquisition.
Acquisition targets vary in recycling competency and profitability.
Firm J is recommended as the best acquisition target due to strong recycling competency and relatively low cost.
Risks include operational integration and contamination of waste streams.
additional_questions: What are the key factors and drivers to consider in developing a solution for Waste Not?
What can be concluded from the waste material mix about profitability loss?
What initiatives could boost profitability growth given the lag in paper and plastics processing?
What are methods of market entry into recycling and their pros and cons?
Based on potential acquisition targets, which firm is the best choice and why?
Please give an overview of your findings to the CEO.
solution: Waste Not’s profitability loss is driven by declining processing of paper and plastics. To boost growth, the company should focus on recyclable materials and construction/demolition waste. Acquisition is the preferred market entry strategy into recycling, with Firm J as the best target due to strong recycling capabilities and lower cost. Risks include integration challenges which can be mitigated by separate processing facilities and customer communication.
evaluation: Candidate should identify revenue and cost drivers, analyze waste material trends, propose strategic initiatives, evaluate market entry options, assess acquisition targets, and communicate findings clearly.

title: Excellent Health
description: Excellent Health is considering moving its 24/7 customer service call center from New York City to another location and wants to evaluate the factors involved in this decision.
additional_info: The call center is staffed with 100 FTEs located in New York City.
New York has expensive rental space, higher wages, and high operating costs.
Potential new location offers cheaper rent, lower wages, and lower operating expenses.
Transition costs include facility build-out, furnishing, severance pay, recruiting, training, and lease break costs.
Assume 100% turnover of staff with move.
Facility size per FTE is 300 sq ft in NYC and 250 sq ft in new location.
Facility rent is $20/sq ft/month in NYC and $10/sq ft/month in new location.
Operating expenses are $1.00/sq ft/month in NYC and $0.50/sq ft/month in new location.
Average salary is $50K in NYC and $40K in new location.
Lease break cost is $500,000 with no sublease opportunities.
additional_questions: What factors need to be considered when evaluating whether to move the call center?
What is the financial impact of moving the call center to Seattle?
solution: Candidate should consider ongoing costs (rent, salaries, operating expenses), transition costs (build-out, furnishing, severance, recruiting, training, lease break), and non-cost factors (strategic location advantages, talent pool, attrition, customer impact). Financial analysis should compare total costs over relevant time horizon to determine net benefit or loss of moving. Given data, moving to Seattle reduces ongoing costs but incurs significant transition costs and risks.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a comprehensive framework covering costs and qualitative factors, perform financial impact analysis, and weigh strategic considerations to recommend whether to move.

title: Volunteer Co Talent Retention Case
description: Your client is Volunteer Co, a large non-profit organization with high volunteer turnover and declining employee satisfaction. Diagnose the problem and suggest possible solutions.
additional_info: Volunteer Co employs primarily part-time volunteers and a small paid staff.
One large location is delivering fewer service-hours due to poor connection between leadership and regional staff.
Employee satisfaction has dropped significantly with high turnover.
Talent-related risks include reputational risks, black swan events, business and regulatory risks, HR risks, poor succession planning, sudden loss of key personnel, and lack of internal leadership.
Possible oversight solutions include frequent review of external risks, employee surveys, creating a responsible position, monitoring the talent pipeline, and building alignment with talent.
To capture employee voice, communication with the entire firm is necessary; surveys, focus groups, and townhall meetings are options.
Prioritize stakeholders by position/seniority, happiness, and tenure.
Possible reasons for high attrition include lifestyle, culture, and declining benefits.
Possible fixes include promoting work-life balance, shifting culture, and offering better programs.
Board is skeptical about need for change; you must summarize implementation and persuade them.
additional_questions: What major types of talent-related risk does any organization incur by letting this situation go unchecked? What steps would you take to identify or prepare for these risks?
How would you go about capturing the voice of the employees? Who would you prioritize talking to?
What are some possible reasons for Volunteer Co’s very high attrition rate? What fixes exist?
Please summarize your implementation plan and try to sway the board that change is necessary.
solution: Diagnose talent risks including reputational, HR, and regulatory risks. Use surveys, focus groups, and townhalls to capture employee voice, prioritizing key stakeholders. Identify causes of attrition such as lifestyle, culture, and benefits, and propose fixes like promoting work-life balance and culture shifts. Persuade the board by highlighting HR risks, a strategy for hearing employee concerns, plans to address issues, and next steps.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to brainstorm talent risks and solutions, structure communication plans, prioritize stakeholders, analyze root causes of attrition, and persuasively summarize implementation plans.

title: Sloan Clinic Expansion Case
description: Sloan Clinic, a large hospital in Chicago, is considering two expansion options: expanding their trauma center to reduce ER wait times or opening a convenient care clinic connected to specialty clinics. Recommend which expansion to pursue based on financials, competitive positioning, and operational considerations.
additional_info: Expansion 1: Trauma center expansion adding capacity for 50 more patients per hour, an additional surgical operating room, 25 nurses, and 10 physicians/assistants.
Expansion 2: Convenient care clinic open 12 hours/day, 7 days/week, staffed with 3 nurses and 2 physicians/assistants, treating non-emergency cases.
Without expansion, additional costs for upkeep will still occur.
Exhibit 1 shows patient allocation percentages across clinics.
Exhibit 2 shows average wait times for ER, convenient clinics, and specialty clinics.
Exhibit 3 provides initial investment, average monthly cost to patient, variable and fixed costs, and patient volumes for each option.
Exhibit 4 discusses budget constraints and potential funding sources including external donors, fundraising, and government funding if research is included.
Break-even points: Expansion 1 - 4.72 years, Expansion 2 - 3.52 years, No expansion - 0 years.
Brainstormed internal ways to reduce wait times include call ahead service, dial-a-nurse, pre-registration, and online cancellations.
additional_questions: What is the best option financially, weighing initial investment against long-term potential revenues?
How do the different expansions compare to other clinics in the area?
What are other potential ways to internally reduce the wait time for Sloan Clinic?
What recommendation do you believe is best for Sloan Clinic?
solution: Both expansions could work, but given that 35% of patients do not need trauma care and competitors have convenient care clinics, opening a convenient care clinic is a logical approach. Financial analysis including break-even points supports this. Internal operational improvements to reduce wait times should also be considered.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze financial data, compare competitive positioning, brainstorm operational improvements, and provide a clear, data-driven recommendation.

title: Phone Co Technology Infrastructure Upgrade
description: Phone Co, a leading cellphone provider, needs to upgrade its fragmented technology infrastructure to support flexible contracts and improved customer self-service. Help select a vendor, manage stakeholders, handle employee resistance, plan data migration, and ensure successful implementation.
additional_info: Phone Co offers over 3,000 separate plans with limited backend capabilities.
Customer data is tied to phones, not individuals.
Website is difficult to use, increasing reliance on in-person reps.
Vendor selection factors: business fit, ease of implementation, technical performance, scalability, timing, costs.
Vendor selection steps: identify strategic goals, assess current capabilities, develop future state, build roadmap, develop RFP, select vendors.
Major stakeholders include Phone Co leadership, project liaisons, SMEs, development teams, front-line managers; Deloitte partners, managers, consultants; vendor leadership and development teams.
Employee resistance exists among low to mid-level employees skeptical of the overhaul.
Communication channels include CEO messaging, focus groups, mass communications, and advocacy teams.
Data migration options: big bang (unsuitable), pilot program, phased rollout with segmentation by customer loyalty.
Risks include data corruption, revenue loss during migration, and employee disengagement.
additional_questions: What factors would you consider when selecting a vendor, and what steps would you take?
Who are the major stakeholders in this process?
How should the CEO handle employee disenchantment and build consensus? What communication channels should be used?
How would you implement the customer data migration strategy given risks and urgency?
Summarize your plans and convince the teams that this project can be successfully implemented with Deloitte.
solution: Select vendors based on strategic fit, technical performance, scalability, timing, and cost following a structured process. Identify and engage all key stakeholders across Phone Co, Deloitte, and the vendor. Address employee resistance by CEO-led communication, branding change positively, quick wins, and inclusive planning. Use a pilot program followed by phased rollout for data migration, segmenting customers by loyalty to reduce risk. Summarize the necessity of change, vendor selection strategy, people management plans, and risk mitigation to convince teams.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate structured vendor selection, stakeholder mapping, change management communication, risk-aware migration planning, and persuasive summarization.

title: Practice Case #1 – Bubble Gum Manufacturer
description: You are a consultant working for a bubble gum manufacturer. The CEO of the gum manufacturing company is concerned because his company is experiencing declining margin. My questions to you are: (1) the reasons behind declining profitability (2) your suggestions for improving profitability
additional_info: Sales have been increasing
If asked about cost composition, ask the interviewee to brainstorm potential cost buckets: COGS: gum, sugar, flavor, smell, color; Labor; SG&A

Raw material cost has not changed (i.e. the unit cost is the same but total cost could be different depending on product composition. This is the key to the case so don’t give the information about the unit cost to the interviewee too early in the interview. Remember to push the interviewee so that she comes to the conclusion herself
Product portfolio – 2 types of products: flavored and flavorless gum
Both products are sold at the same price point.
Composition products: Flavored gum raw material: gum, sugar, flavor, color dye; Flavorless gum raw material: gum, sugar, color dye
Gum, Sugar, and Color Dye raw materials are same in size for each of the two products.
solution: Profitability has declined because sales of flavored gum have been increasing, which means that raw material consumption is also increasing because flavored gum requires the additional flavor component. This added with the fact that the price of flavored gum is the same as non flavored gum, means that, essentially, costs are now rising while revenues are not. To improve the profits of the company, interviewee should come up with 4-5 suggestions (raising price of flavored gum, sourcing cheaper flavors from other suppliers, negotiate with existing suppliers to reduce the flavor cost, vertical integration of flavor manufacturing company).
evaluation: The key focus of this case is in the product mix. Interviewees should explore sales trend, cost trend, cost composition, product mix. The conclusion should be concise. For improving profits, good candidates present short term (quick wins like negotiating with suppliers or sourcing cheaper flavors) and long term options (vertical integration). When discussing price change, candidates should consider risks such as customer reaction and propose ways to justify price increases (e.g. value-added products, marketing).

title: Practice Case #2 – Widget Manufacturer in Brazil
description: A widget manufacturer in Brazil has a 60% national market share and has a leadership position in South America. However, it only has a 5% international market share. The top 20 clients buy 80% of its products and the manufacturer is concerned with increasing margins and exploiting new sources of revenue. Their CEO has asked you to gather data and brainstorm potential ideas for increasing margins and market share.
additional_info: Market is growing at 5%
No significant competitors in the region
Greatest threat comes from U.S., Chinese and Indian imports
Premium pricing for national customers
Plants are operating at 100% capacity
Plants can’t shift from producing one product to another
Currently company sells products directly to customers
Interviewee should ask to understand the products and profitability of each. Use Exhibit A when asked.
Interviewee should ask about the breakdown of international and national markets. Use Exhibit B when asked.
Ask them to calculate profit margins (should use the unit costs in Exhibit A). Assume no freight costs, taxes, etc.
solution: Product A is more profitable nationally while Product B is more profitable internationally. For national markets, the client should consider reducing prices to gain market share from India, China, and the U.S. to maintain their leadership position. They should also consider developing new products in partnership with local clients and establishing new distribution channels to better serve smaller clients. Internationally, the client should seek support from the government to aid in the export of products, such as creating commercial partnerships at the government level. They should also consider acquiring a company in the markets they would like to enter as well as setting up distribution channels to reach these customers.
evaluation: The interviewee should understand the market dynamics, which products are more profitable than others and where this happens, and should brainstorm to figure out how to exploit this information.

title: Practice Case #3 – Market Growth Strategy in Retail Banking
description: A bank based in a developing country in Southeast Asia has hired us to determine how it can grow in the local market, specifically in retail banking. What are the factors you would look at to assess the situation? What is your recommendation for our client?
additional_info: Competition/Competitive Landscape: Market Share by bank and product; Client is one of the top three market share leaders. Show Exhibit 1 & 2.
Observation: Client maintained market share in deposits but lost market share in credit cards. Client lost share in auto loans but is doing very well in home loans.
Product and pricing competitiveness: All products are priced competitively at market level.
Brand name and reputation or customer awareness: Client has good brand name reputation and recognition.
Branch and ATM prevalence or distribution of branch network: Client has wide distribution of branch and ATM network.
Macroeconomic Trends: Employment rate or population growth; Number of people coming into employment age is at an all time high in the coming 5 years.
Foreign investments are projected for healthy growth with increase investments in Business Process Outsourcing.
Interest rate trend: Interest rate has declined dramatically in the last 5 years from over 10% to the current level of 4%.

Disposable income or GDP trend: 40% of the population lives at or below poverty line. Another 40% makes less than $500 per month. Top 15% makes less than $2,000 per month. Remaining top 5% is the richest population in the country whose lifestyle resembles those of upper-middle class in developed countries.
Disposable income and GDP is projected to continue growing at about 11% per annum.
Home/car ownership and real estate development growth: Expected to increase in the coming 5 years.
Rural to urban migration: Migration rate is stable.
Products: savings, checking, time deposit, credit cards and loans.
Profitability or revenue by product, including consideration of price and volume: Most profitable products are credit cards and loans.
Product penetration in population: 30% of population has deposit accounts. 6% of population has credit cards. 1.5% of population has bank loans.
Client Capabilities: Sales and marketing are traditional and conservative. Client does not conduct direct marketing activities to sell credit cards. Sales force is stretched and training is lacking.
Information technology: Information on customers is often out-dated.
Research and development/innovation: R&D is competitive and client is a market leader in product innovation for a few products.
Customers: Majority of customers only purchase one bank product. Large majority of customers also have <100K in average daily balance in their deposit accounts (see Exhibit 3).
Retention or attrition rate: Attrition rate is in line with market average.

Possible growth drivers: 1. Steal share from competition; 2. Increase retention of current customers; 3. Cross sell current products to current customers; 4. Up sell current products to current customers; 5. Develop new generation of customers.
solution: 1. Growth efforts should be concentrated in cross selling more profitable products to current customers since average number of products held by customers is low. 2. Client should adopt more aggressive sales and marketing techniques to gain more market share in credit cards. 3. Client should partner with or market to employers to capture a new generation of bank customers fueled by employment growth, and partner with real estate developers, as well as car dealers, to finance future home and car purchase.
evaluation: Candidate should spend most of his time brainstorming all the factors that should be considered to understand the situation. He will locate problems and potential solutions by probing interviewer for information. After all or most of the information is given, candidate should make his best guess on what the potential recommendations should be for the client. Any logical recommendations supported by information given or any assumption he may make will be accepted. A good candidate should be able to summarize his understanding and make a logical hypothesis on potential recommendations.

title: Practice Case #4 – Retirement Homes
description: Our client is a company that owns and operates retirement homes in US. They just opened a new retirement home in Chicago about a year ago and they have concerns about its profitability. So, we were hired to fix the problem.
additional_info: The new home is located in downtown Chicago and all the previous ones were located in outside of Miami.
Revenues don’t pose a problem (demand is high and price is relatively high). Explore costs.
Client owns land in all of the retirement homes.

Revenue elements for OLD (typical) retirement home vs NEW: Units 800 vs 400; Revenue/unit $500 vs $1000; Total available revenue $400,000 vs $400,000; Vacancy 20% vs 10%; Actual revenue $320,000 vs $360,000.

Costs for OLD vs NEW retirement homes: Amenities Total $48,000 vs $54,000 (Fixed component $24,000 vs $30,000; Variable/month per apartment $30 vs $60; Total Variable $24,000 vs $24,000), Maintenance Total $100,000 vs $80,000 (Variable/month per apartment $125 vs $200), Utilities Total $38,400 vs $45,000 (Variable/month per apartment occupied $60 vs $125; Apartments occupied 640 vs 360), Medical Facility Total --- vs $60,000, SG&A Total $80,000 vs $60,000, Total Costs $266,400 vs $299,000, Total Margin $53,600 vs $61,000, Gross margin % 16.8% vs 16.9%
solution: The cost of operating the new retirement home is higher, however it alone tells nothing about the problem our client is facing. The new retirement home not only has higher costs, but it also has higher revenues. Consequently, margins generated by each home are pretty consistent and, if anything, the new retirement home generates higher margin in both absolute and relative terms. Having analyzed the performance of the new retirement home and compared it to the performance of a typical retirement home that our client operates, we reached the conclusion that our client has no profitability problem. The new home generates $61,000 margin versus $53,600 in old home or about 17% in both cases. On the other hand, there are some significant differences in cost structure for the new and old retirement homes, so we can study it in more depth and potentially reduce costs in both of them to generate higher margin.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost structure carefully, calculate margins, and compare new vs old homes. The key is to realize that despite higher costs, the new home has higher revenues and similar margins, so profitability is not the issue. Candidate should also identify differences in cost structure for further cost reduction opportunities.

title: Practice Case #5 – R&D Portfolio
description: You are part of our team working for a Pharmaceutical manufacturer. We've been asked to look at their current R&D portfolio and assess whether they're maximizing their potential with their current projects. My questions to you are: (1) How can you help them decide what information you would need and (2) What components do you think would round out a portfolio for this company?
additional_info: Current staffing needs are at equilibrium and layoffs and hiring are not expected to be necessary.
Encourage brainstorming of categories for the company to consider when developing their R&D portfolio, such as investing in current projects versus investing in creating new business.
additional_questions: If you were to look at these two areas, how would you suggest breaking up the expenditure?
solution: The company should be considering looking at multiple areas of its business such as whether the R&D is for new business or current business, new technologies versus current technologies, spread of projects across business units, big projects versus small projects, high dollar investment versus low dollar investment, short duration projects versus long duration projects, etc. The interviewee should create a 2x2 diagram comparing two chosen categories and allocate percentages of expenditure accordingly.
evaluation: A successful interview would follow this logical path: (1) Develop a small framework from which they follow, including categories like revenues and expenses, regulatory environment, internal issues; (2) Ask appropriate questions based on this roadmap; (3) Provide a concise conclusion focusing on key portfolio categories; (4) Create a 2x2 matrix with reasonable percentage allocations. The math should be correct and the total should sum to 100%.

title: Microfinance in India
description: Our client is a large microfinance institution in India that has seen its client base (largely rural women earning less than $3 a day) growing at over 150% a year. However, the rapid growth has started to generate criticism over perceived high interest rates and harsh collection tactics of loan officers. This came to a head when the media and (local & state) started to blame farmer suicides on microfinance-induced over-indebtedness and harsh collection practices. What should our client do in response?
additional_info: Microfinance is a huge untapped market in India. Demand met is $5Bn of $55Bn.
Microfinance Institutions (MFIs) cropped up in the early-late 1990s as non-profit institutions (NGOs).
In the early-to-mid 2000s, these NGOs began to convert to for-profit institutions in order to access commercial capital that would help them scale.
The average MFI interest rate is between 25-30%.
Because many MFIs are now for-profit, critics contend that MFIs are making money off the poor through their 'high' interest rates.
At that time, our client provided financial services to around 1 million women clients in 5 states in India.
Loan officers are all from villages themselves, and are often incentivized according to number of collections and drop-out rate.
Main critiques are: 'high' interest rates and harsh collection tactics.
Our client (and other private MFIs) compete with government providers of microfinance – majority of government voter base is rural poor, so private MFIs are stepping on purview of government.
Other private MFIs are allies, though two have engaged in harsh collection practices. Our client has disassociated from them.
Government programs are highly subsidized (World Bank), so can charge much lower interest rates. Still, their default rates are much higher.
Cost structure of MFI shows admin costs and cost of lending are extremely high, and profits are less than 2%.
Compared to government programs, our client has a lower cost of borrowing because loan officers go to clients weekly and clients do not take bribes.
Default rate is less than 2%.
Typical investment by a woman MFI client is to buy a buffalo. ROI calculation provided.
Interest rates seem justified given cost structure and ROI for clients.
solution: Client should begin vigorous PR and marketing campaign emphasizing transparency of rates, cost of borrowing among competitors, and ROI of clients. Operationally, client should not reduce interest rate as 2% profits serve to fuel organization growth and benefit a larger proportion of the rural Indian population. Rates are in line with other private MFIs. If competitor private MFIs drop interest rates, client might face loss of portfolio/clientele and increased criticism. Continue to explore innovations to streamline processes to reduce interest rates and renegotiate cost of lending terms with banks.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to analyze cost structure, competitive landscape, client ROI, and stakeholder perceptions. Ability to quantify ROI and understand trade-offs between social impact and profitability is key. Candidate should propose balanced recommendations considering operational, reputational, and financial factors.

title: China Outsourcing Opportunity
description: The client is a national plastic manufacturer in the US. Their customers are supermarkets and discount retailers. They are looking to outsource manufacturing to China but the CEO is concerned because no one else in the industry has done this yet. Should we recommend to our client to outsource to China?
additional_info: Client has 3 product lines: freezer bags, plastic plates and utensils, and specialty plates and utensils.
Costs in US and China differ by labor, materials, overhead, and transportation.
Quality varies by product line: freezer bags have lower quality in China; plastic plates and specialty plates have equal quality.
Consumer preferences: freezer bags are commodity but quality very important; plastic plates are commodity and quality not an issue; specialty plates are seasonal and trend sensitive.
PR/Brand risks are minimal but should be considered.
Outsourcing to China can save approximately 25% of costs for certain product lines.
solution: Recommend outsourcing the plastic plates and specialty plates to China due to cost savings and quality parity. Keep freezer bags production in the US due to quality concerns. Consider changes in fixed overhead costs, potential to rent out freed capacity, and PR/brand image risks. Additional benefits include potential access to growing Asian markets.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze cost components, quality and consumer preferences, and risks including brand and PR. Ability to perform cost calculations and weigh qualitative factors is important. Candidate should provide a nuanced recommendation balancing cost savings and quality concerns.

title: Caskets
description: A casket company is considering buying another company. What factors would you consider in assessing how much they should pay? Ignore synergies and financing; focus specifically on how to put a value on this company.
additional_info: Acquirer is market leader.
Target has 10% of the market.
Estimate deaths per year and percentage that use caskets.
Use pricing to estimate revenues.
Use costs to estimate profitability.
Average life expectancy is 80 years; US population is 300 million.
Deaths per year estimated by dividing population by life expectancy, but actual deaths are lower due to age distribution.

DCF valuation approach: add back non-cash expenses, subtract capex and working capital changes, add back interest expense, project cash flows for 5-7 years, calculate terminal value, discount cash flows using appropriate rate.
solution: Value the company using a discounted cash flow (DCF) approach based on projected net income adjusted for non-cash expenses, capital expenditures, and working capital changes. Estimate market size by calculating deaths per year and market share. Use pricing and cost data to estimate revenues and profits. Calculate terminal value and discount cash flows to present value.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to estimate market size, understand valuation concepts, and apply DCF methodology. Ability to question assumptions (e.g., uniform age distribution) and incorporate them into analysis is important.

title: Bottled Water Market
description: Our client is a leading beer manufacturer experiencing stagnant sales in an increasingly competitive industry. A high-level executive has noticed a steady increase in bottled water consumption. The client believes the assets required to produce bottled water are similar to beer and wants to evaluate whether to enter the bottled water market to achieve sales and profit goals. Help the client determine if the breakeven point seems reasonable and how to proceed with bringing the product to market.
additional_info: Annual growth rate of bottled water market is around 10%.
Average price per 12oz bottle is $1.50.
Current market size is $1.5B or 1B bottles.
Target customers are men and women aged 15-65 leading active lifestyles, growing at 5-6% CAGR.
Partners require $20M upfront investment to begin venture.
Production cost per bottle is $0.50; distribution cost per bottle is $0.20.
Breakeven point is $20M / ($1.50 - 0.50 - 0.20) = 25M units.
Plants currently running at full capacity producing beer; building new facility would cost $50M.
Client has $25M left in cash and no access to capital markets.
Potential partners include soda and juice manufacturers and other beer manufacturers with excess capacity to produce up to 200M bottles per year at cost less than $25M.
Client has an extensive and efficient distribution network overlapping many bottled water outlets.
Client recently invested in a new marketing campaign.
solution: The breakeven market share is 2.5% (25M units / 1B units). Given the market growth and client’s distribution capabilities, entering the bottled water market is attractive if the client can secure production capacity through partnerships without large capital expenditure. The client should consider outsourcing production to partners with excess capacity to minimize upfront investment and leverage existing distribution and marketing strengths.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market size, growth, breakeven calculations, capital constraints, and competitive landscape. Ability to synthesize financial and operational factors and propose feasible entry strategies is key.

title: De Beers Retail Venture
description: De Beers, a leader in diamond exploration, is considering entering the retail business to sell finished diamonds (e.g., engagement rings). Should De Beers enter the retail market?
additional_info: De Beers currently operates in exploration, extraction, and distribution but not in polishing or jeweling.
They plan to continue selling raw diamonds to polishers and buy finished diamonds from jewelers.
Customers are wealthy individuals, typically located in large metropolitan areas such as New York or London.
Fixed costs include real estate rent (£300 per square meter, 5,000 square meters), salaries, security, insurance, fixtures, etc.
Product prices and gross margins for 5 products are provided; candidate must calculate profit and breakeven units (~60,000 diamonds per year).
Market assumed healthy; competition can be ignored for this case.
additional_questions: What are the main fixed costs for the retail venture?
Calculate the gross margin and profit for each product.
How many diamonds must be sold to break even?
Is it feasible to sell 60,000 diamonds per year in one store?
What strategic recommendations would you make based on the feasibility?
solution: De Beers should reconsider its retail entry strategy because selling 25 diamonds per hour per store to break even is unlikely. The biggest cost driver is the large store size (5,000 m2). Recommendations include reducing store size to lower fixed costs, focusing on the most profitable products (P5 and P2), and exploring other locations.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate understanding of the value chain, perform breakeven analysis, assess feasibility of sales volume, identify cost drivers, and provide strategic recommendations.

title: Hospital Administrative Software
description: Our client sells hospital administrative software and has seen declining sales. Why have sales dropped and what recommendations would you provide?
additional_info: Market replacement rate is every 10 years.
Each software sale is worth $9M, paid in three $3M installments over 3 years.
Only one software product is needed per hospital.
Market is growing at 15%.
Competitors can install their products within 2 years.
Client's product is complex with more features than needed, leading to longer installation times and costly patches.
Customers prefer simple, easy-to-use products.
R&D adds features without market research.
Sales force reports difficulty selling; installations can be delayed up to 4 years.
solution: Recommend simplifying the product to reduce complexity and installation time, lowering costs associated with delays. Implement control policies in R&D to prevent unnecessary feature additions, reducing costs and aligning product with customer needs.
evaluation: Candidate should size the market, analyze external and internal factors affecting sales, identify product complexity as a key issue, and propose actionable recommendations.

title: Jamaican Land Investment
description: Our client is considering buying 10 acres of land in Jamaica for $3,000. Should they invest in this land for agricultural use to achieve a $4,500 profit within two years (excluding purchase price)?
additional_info: Land price: $3,000 for 10 acres.
Fixed costs: $500 initial setup (year 1), $350 per year salaried labor.
Market demand per year: 5000 trees, 1000 shrubs, 1000 fruit, 2500 exotic flowers.
Competitors have 60% tree share, 20% shrub, 85% fruit, 90% exotic flower share; remaining shares available to client.
Plants per acre: 10 trees, 25 shrubs, 75 fruit, 50 exotic flowers.
Price per plant: Tree $50, Shrub $35, Fruit $15, Exotic $25.
Variable cost per plant: Tree $30, Shrub $25, Fruit $11, Exotic $17.
Cannot mix plant types on the same acre.
additional_questions: How would you price each plant?
Would you use all 10 acres for the most profitable plant if demand exists? Why or why not?
solution: Assign acres starting with most profitable plants (exotic flowers, fruit, shrubs) to meet demand and maximize profit. Total profit over two years exceeds $5,500, surpassing the $4,500 target. Recommend investment but advise investigating growth trends and risks like natural disasters.
evaluation: Candidate should clarify objectives, calculate demand and profitability per acre, allocate land efficiently, and assess financial viability and risks.

title: Academic Performance of Students
description: A city public school system with 130,000 students has average exam scores much lower than the rest of the state. How do you improve academic performance?
additional_info: There are 13 grades: Elementary (KG-6), Junior High (7-8), High School (9-12).
Each student orders 2 math books; 75% arrive on time due to warehousing and online order inefficiencies.
Inefficiency split: 75% warehousing, 25% online orders.
Proposed improvements: warehouse efficiency up 50%, online orders up 80%.
Students per grade are equally distributed.
additional_questions: What are the implications of moving 6th graders from elementary to junior high?
How does this affect capacity utilization and costs?
solution: Improve warehousing and online order systems to increase on-time delivery of textbooks. Consider capacity utilization when moving grades; moving 6th graders reduces elementary capacity utilization from 80% to 70%, potentially increasing costs. Recommendations include redistributing teachers and evaluating support systems.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze school, student, and external factors; perform quantitative analysis on book delivery; assess capacity utilization impacts; and propose actionable improvements.

title: Mobile Phone Insurance
description: INSURANCECO, a monopoly insurer for large US mobile carriers, wants to increase revenues. Currently, 20% of a carrier's 40 million subscribers have insurance. What is the impact of increasing insurance subscribers to 25%?
additional_info: INSURANCECO provides insurance to carriers but does not charge carriers directly; carriers collect premiums from customers and pass on to INSURANCECO.
Customers pay a $5 monthly fee and a $50 deductible; phone replacement cost is $200.
Breakeven on a customer replacement takes 30 months.
Mobile industry subscriber base is not growing; providers focus on increasing ARPU.
Only 20% of subscribers currently have insurance; many consider it unnecessary.
additional_questions: What channel strategies can INSURANCECO use to increase subscribers?
What are revenues and costs per customer?
What is the revenue impact of increasing insurance subscribers from 20% to 25%?
solution: Increasing insurance subscribers from 20% to 25% adds 2 million subscribers, generating an additional $10.18 million per month for the carrier. INSURANCECO should explore channel strategies such as selling insurance at point of phone sale, direct sales, and business sales. Reducing phone replacements is critical to controlling costs.
evaluation: Candidate should understand the value chain, calculate revenue impact, identify channel strategies, and recognize cost drivers like fraud and replacements.

title: Organic Pizza Crust
description: A natural foods company with $35M annual sales is considering launching an organic pizza crust mix. Should they proceed?
additional_info: Organic food market is growing at 25%; organic is 10% of total food market.
Client would be first mover in organic pizza crust mix market but competes with regular pizza crust products.
Price: $3.50 per unit; variable costs: $1.20 ingredients, $1.50 sales & marketing, $0.30 labor.
Upfront investment: $30,000 for new equipment.
Client has full value chain capabilities.
Estimated demand: 20 million units per year (based on 100M households, 2 pizzas made at home per year, 24 pizzas consumed per household annually).
solution: Recommend launching the organic pizza crust mix. The client needs to sell only 60,000 units to break even in a market demanding 20 million units annually. The organic market is growing rapidly, and the client has the capabilities to succeed. Suggest launching first in gourmet or natural food channels for higher margins and less competition.
evaluation: Candidate should size the market, analyze costs and margins, perform breakeven analysis, assess feasibility, and consider strategic channel choices.

title: Traffic Signal Company
description: A small California-based company that installs and maintains traffic signals is considering expanding into the Manhattan market. Should they enter?
additional_info: Client does not manufacture signals, only installs and maintains.
Goal is to increase revenues and profits; no specific ROI target.
Market size: Manhattan has approximately 2,000 intersections (80 streets long x 25 streets wide).
Each intersection has one traffic signal system lasting 20 years; 1/20 replaced annually.
Installation market estimated at $10M annually; maintenance market at $30M annually.
Contract duration: 3 years.
Installation cost per system: $100,000; maintenance cost per system: $15,000 per year.
Labor costs and time provided for installation and maintenance.
Contracts awarded by bidding; incumbent is largest NE traffic signal company.
additional_questions: Calculate market size and revenue streams.
Calculate NPV of the project.
Discuss strategic importance and bidding strategy.
solution: Entering Manhattan is profitable with an $88M NPV over 3 years. Strategic importance of Manhattan market justifies bidding aggressively, possibly at a loss, to gain market presence and future opportunities. Risks include lack of experience in large cities and competitor bidding behavior.
evaluation: Candidate should size market, calculate costs and revenues, compute NPV, assess strategic factors, and recommend bidding strategy.

title: Travel Channel
description: A cable channel specializing in travel currently lacks audience and is unprofitable. Which audience demographic should it pursue to increase profitability?
additional_info: Three demographics: Luxury Travelers (80,000 viewers), Adventure Travelers (50,000), Budget Travelers (150,000).
Revenue per ad slot per 1,000 viewers: Luxury $30, Adventure $35, Budget $10.
100 ad slots per day, 365 days per year.
Cable companies pay 25% of subscription fees; total yearly subscription fees collected: $70M.
Total annual costs: $100M.
Client wants profit above $5M.
solution: Recommend targeting Luxury Travelers. Calculations show $5.1M profit, exceeding current earnings. Further exploration should include additional revenue streams (licensing, sponsorships), competitive landscape, and cost reduction.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue streams, calculate profits for each demographic, compare results, and recommend the most profitable segment.

title: Best Buy
description: Best Buy faces declining sales and rising inventory approaching Christmas. What short-term actions should they take to increase profitability and what long-term strategies should they pursue to stay competitive?
additional_info: Circuit City has declared bankruptcy.
Walmart is the main competitor.
Zero margin on big products like laptops and flat screens.
Geek Squad generated $1bn revenue last year.
Considerations include inventory management, cost control, sales force compensation, internet sales, store experience, and supplier relations.
additional_questions: How should Best Buy respond to Circuit City's closing? What are the risks and opportunities?
solution: Short-term: cut costs, reduce inventory, possibly lower prices. Long-term: enhance in-store experience, promote Geek Squad, consider strategic acquisitions of Circuit City assets or locations. Circuit City's demise is both a risk and opportunity.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze inventory and cost issues, competitive landscape, channel strategies, and strategic options regarding Circuit City.

title: All-Mart Romania Market Entry
description: All-Mart, a discount superstore similar to Walmart, plans to enter the Romanian market by opening a store in Bucharest. Should they proceed?
additional_info: Bucharest population: 2 million.
Average salary per person: 1350 RON/month.
Spending habits: 30% food, 40% rent/utilities, 5% clothing, 25% various.
Electronics spending: $500 per person annually.
Currency conversion: 3 RON = 1 USD.
Existing competition: 5 superstores cover 30% of market; rest served by smaller stores.
Costs in Romania are 68% of US costs overall, with food and salaries significantly cheaper.
solution: Market size and cost advantages suggest potential profitability. Candidate should consider market capture realistic share (~10%), cost savings from local sourcing and lower salaries, and strategic fit with All-Mart's culture and experience.
evaluation: Candidate should size market, analyze costs, assess competition, and evaluate strategic fit.

title: Loonilever China Skincare Market Entry
description: Loonilever, a major consumer products company, wants to enter China's skincare market, where B&G is the leader. Can Loonilever achieve $100 million revenue in two years in this market?
additional_info: Loonilever operates in multiple consumer product categories and leads in China's laundry segment but not skincare.
Skincare is more profitable (~75% gross margin) than laundry (~30%).
Market shows significant potential for future growth.
Client seeks to understand market size, segmentation, and key players.
solution: Candidate should clarify revenue targets, size and segment the market, analyze competition, and develop an entry strategy focused on achieving $100M revenue in two years. Specific solution details are not provided in the excerpt.
evaluation: Candidate should ask clarifying questions, build an issue tree, analyze market and competition, and propose a feasible entry strategy.

title: Franchising Gyms
description: What are potential cost cutting opportunities the client can pursue? What opportunities are more actionable?
additional_info: Rent, equipment and labor are the largest expense categories.
Rent: Re-negotiate current contracts, move to less optimal locations (Actionability: Low).
Equipment: Use equipment longer, purchase used equipment, rent equipment, consolidate purchases, increase buyer power (Actionability: Moderate).
Labor: Decrease staffed hours, utilize phone support, post more FAQs (Actionability: Moderate).
solution: Focus on revenue growth opportunities and cost saving opportunities that are most actionable, especially in equipment and labor. Consider focusing on rural market segment due to lower costs and competition. Consider M&A opportunities to gain scale and improve cost structure.
evaluation: Candidate should identify major cost buckets, assess actionability of cost cutting options, and recommend focus areas with justification.

title: Franchising Gyms - Segment Analysis
description: Which segment offers the greatest opportunity on a profitability basis? What are the key factors?
additional_info: Rural segment has lower rent and labor costs, high availability of gym space, and low competition.
Urban segment has high rent, low availability, high competition.
Suburban segment is medium across factors.
solution: Rural segment is most attractive due to lower costs and low competition despite lower fitness interest and income.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze segment profitability drivers including costs, competition, and market factors.

title: Greeting Card Manufacturer
description: A greeting card manufacturer has experienced decreased profit. The CEO has asked you to figure out why.
additional_info: Greeting cards are stocked at grocery stores and pharmacies.
Cards are seasonal; unsold cards are shipped back at manufacturer's expense and discarded.
Greeting card industry has moderate growth; competitors have steady or slightly increased profit.
Manufacturer earns revenue only when cards are sold to consumers.
COGS: $1.50, SG&A: $0.25, Shipping: $0.05 (only if card unsold), Fixed Costs: $200,000.
solution: Improve revenues by ensuring wide selection and supply at retailers, balancing goodwill and costs of unsold cards. Consider salvage value and consumer preferences. Breakeven sales volume is 160,000 cards at $3 price.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost drivers, understand industry dynamics, and calculate breakeven.

title: UK Asset Manager
description: Our client is a private equity firm considering acquisition of a UK asset management firm that has seen a 33% drop in AUM in 18 months. Determine whether this is a good acquisition.
additional_info: AUM includes retail, institutional, and alternative funds (~75% retail and institutional).
Firm operates mainly in UK with smaller presence in Germany and Spain.
Target is 10th in market share in both institutional and retail segments.
Target lost a large institutional investor in 2008 causing capital outflows.
UK asset management market size is £3.4T.
Market growth driven by market performance, GDP/wealth, and net capital flows.
Target's funds have average annual returns of 4.5%.
additional_questions: How many years would it take for the funds to double at 4.5% annual returns?
What is the valuation and how does it compare to peers?
What are the risks related to client withdrawals and market position?
solution: Recommend against acquisition assuming no valuation issues due to client capital withdrawals and similar financial performance to peers. Suggest further analysis on alternative investments, geographic markets, and fund manager performance.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a framework covering market, competitors, customers, company profitability, and risks. Should perform math on fund growth and interpret data.

title: Mighty Mining Company
description: Your client is a global mining company with a location in South Africa performing below average financially. Identify the problem and recommend solutions.
additional_info: Plant is 160 miles inland; minerals transported by trucks to port then shipped by barge.
Minerals are commodities with low margins.
Transportation costs are significantly higher than other African plants.
Trucks may not be full, poor route planning, hijacking, bribes may occur.
Port closes at 10pm; trucks arriving after must wait until 4am.
Minimum roundtrip travel time is 7 hours; plant owns 20 trucks; barge needs 30 truckloads to ship.
additional_questions: Why might transportation costs be higher at this location?
What does the distribution of truck trip times indicate?
What recommendations would you make given port hours and truck capacity constraints?
solution: Identify bottlenecks in supply chain including transportation inefficiencies and port hours. Recommend short-term operational improvements (driver incentives, shift changes), long-term lobbying for 24-hour port operations, evaluate storage facilities near port, and consider fleet upgrades or leasing.
evaluation: Candidate should identify operational issues, analyze cost drivers, interpret data distributions, and propose practical short- and long-term solutions.

title: The Reel Deal
description: A movie studio client wants to assess the viability of digitizing its movie reels and making them available online for a fee.
additional_info: Expected revenue: $200 per clip, 100 clips per movie.
Revenue split: 50% goes to royalties.
Estimated 25% yearly utilization rate.
Fixed cost to digitize: $30M.
Variable cost per utilized clip: $18.
Competitor demand: 8,000 customers per year.
additional_questions: What would be the revenue per movie per year?
Can the studio break even within 2 years assuming only fixed costs?
Can the studio break even within 2 years including variable costs?
solution: Revenue per movie per year is $2,500. Break-even requires 12,000 customers ignoring variable costs, and 14,634 customers including variable costs. Given competitor demand of 8,000, break-even is challenging but potentially achievable.
evaluation: Candidate should perform revenue and breakeven calculations, consider variable costs, and assess market demand realistically.

title: Publishing Company
description: A publisher sells romance novels to bookstores who return unsold inventory for reimbursement. A bookstore offers a deal: a 10% discount on wholesale prices in exchange for no returns. Should the publisher accept?
additional_info: Putter sold 10,000 books to this bookstore in 2008.
Books cost $5 to make, sold at $10 wholesale.
Bookstore returned 20% of books previously.
Salvage value for unsold books is zero.
Projected new order is 7,500 books at $9 wholesale price.
Sales have been flat for a decade.
additional_questions: Is the deal financially beneficial considering profit and cash flow?
What are the risks and strategic implications of accepting or rejecting the deal?
solution: Profit remains the same pre- and post-deal ($30,000), but cash flow and market share decline. Recommendation is not to accept the deal due to lower cash flow and potential negative strategic effects.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate profit and cash flow impacts, consider strategic risks, and weigh trade-offs.

title: Retirement Apartment Complexes
description: The client owns 25 retirement apartment complexes in southern US states. Should the CEO expand to northern cities or focus on the South?
additional_info: Northern market has unmet demand and no competition.
Southern properties have 800 units with 80% occupancy and $500 rent/mo.
Northern properties have 400 units with 90% occupancy and $1,000 rent/mo.
Costs differ: maintenance, amenities, utilities, SG&A, medical facility costs vary by region.
Profit margins are roughly equal (~16.8%) in both regions.
additional_questions: Which region is more profitable on a per building basis?
What strategic factors should influence the expansion decision?
What risks and next steps should be considered?
solution: Both regions have similar profitability; recommend expanding in the North to capture unmet demand and be first mover. Improve South profitability by adding medical centers and reducing costs. Strategic considerations and risks should guide final decision.
evaluation: Candidate should perform profitability math, compare costs and revenues, and incorporate strategic reasoning.

title: Big Yellow Bus
description: A private equity fund is considering acquiring Big Yellow Bus Co, a leading US school bus manufacturer. Should they proceed?
additional_info: BYB is #1 by revenue, #2 by volume; prices are 20% higher than competitors.
Market growth steady at 3%, recently 6% due to emissions regulations.
Customers are local US cities and towns.
Market size: ~400K buses, 40K sold per year.
Competitors have cost advantages due to ownership by large industrial firms.
BYB's gross margin and operating margin are lower than competitors.
additional_questions: What is the size of the US school bus market?
What does the competitive landscape imply about BYB's position?
What are the risks and recommendations regarding the acquisition?
solution: Do not recommend acquisition due to BYB's declining market share, cost disadvantages, and competitive pressures despite attractive pricing. Risks include economic changes and potential synergies if PE owns related companies.
evaluation: Candidate should size market, analyze competitive position, assess financials, and provide strategic recommendation.

title: Car Company
description: Frank owns a manufacturing company making unibody car frames with one OEM customer. The OEM will not renew the contract due to new welding technology requirements. What should Frank do?
additional_info: New welding technology will be mandated by regulators.
Frank's plant does not support new technology.
Frank's company revenue is $5M with $4.5M costs.
Upgrading plant costs $10M; supply chain savings possible of $100K/year.
Competitors have contracts with other OEMs and use new technology.
Frank is 65 years old and wants to spend more time with family.
additional_questions: What are Frank's options to respond to lost revenue?
What are the costs and benefits of upgrading the plant?
Should Frank exit the market or invest in upgrades?
What strategic considerations affect Frank's decision?
solution: Recommend Frank exit the market due to high upgrade costs, inability to recoup investment in 2 years, and age. Suggest developing exit strategy including potential sale or asset salvage. Explore if any assets can be repurposed.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze value chain, costs, customer options, competition, and context to recommend strategic exit or investment.

title: Zenith Hotel
description: Zenith Hotel is a global hotel chain with 50 hotels in 20 countries. The company is evaluating the construction of a new hotel in the Bahamas. Zenith has come to us asking whether it should and can move forward with the project.
additional_info: The hotel will have 400 rooms.
Start-up costs are $500M.
Operating costs are $20M per year.
Assume a 10-year horizon.
Assume 350 days in a year at constant rates.
Bahamas receives 50,000 visitors per month.
Average occupancy is 75%.
Assume 3 people per room.
Assume 7 day average stay.
Regulatory/country-specific issues are not a concern.
Insurance and marketing costs should be included in ongoing costs.
additional_questions: How much would Zenith need to charge on average per room to break even?
What is Zenith’s implied market share?
solution: Breakeven price per room per night is $500, calculated as total costs ($700M) divided by total room nights over 10 years (400 rooms * 350 days * 10 years = 1.4M room nights). Implied market share is 7.2%, calculated by occupancy (400 rooms * 75% = 300 rooms), multiplied by 3 people per room, times 4 weeks per month, divided by 50,000 visitors per month. Recommendation is that Zenith should proceed with the construction as gaining a 7% market share is reasonable, but the assumption of 350 days occupancy per year is aggressive. Risks include consumer willingness to pay $500/night.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure a market entry feasibility case, analyze fixed and variable costs, perform breakeven and market share calculations, consider internal capabilities and external market factors, and provide a reasoned recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: Whisky Brand Turnaround
description: Our client has been in business for close to 90 years. The original founder started in the whiskey business and over time expanded the product line so that today it is a multi-million dollar business with less than 5% of sales coming from whiskey. The whiskey market in the US has been declining at 2% over the last 20 years and our client’s brand has been declining at the same rate. Last year, however, our client’s brand declined by 15%, despite selling 10m bottles. Our client has come to us to understand what has happened and how to grow the brand back without lowering the profits they were making on it.
additional_info: Internal reasons for decline could include reduction in marketing spend, decline in quality of marketing or product quality, product availability (production/supply chain).
External reasons could include negative PR, new competitor launch, increase in competitor marketing, pricing effects.
Marketing spend hasn’t changed significantly, but competitors have raised prices in their premium category and launched own brands at lower prices.
Competitors have captured the lucrative top end of the market and price sensitive consumers with own brands.
Client’s product is a premium product based on cost to produce; suggested price increase to $19-$20 to be comparable to competitor premium brands.
If price is increased to $20, client expects to sell 8m bottles; costs are 75% variable and 25% fixed.
Increasing price to $20 would increase total profits by $22m (~30%).
Potential risks include competitor response, optimistic sales estimates, consumer perception, and impact on wider liquor portfolio.
solution: Recommend increasing the price to $20 per bottle, which would increase profits by approximately 30%. However, be cautious of competitor reactions, consumer perception, and optimistic sales forecasts. Next steps include validating data, creating marketing and advertising plans, and preparing the market for the price increase.
evaluation: Candidate should identify internal and external reasons for decline, analyze competitor pricing and marketing strategies, calculate profitability impact of pricing options, and discuss risks and next steps.

title: Copper Ore Mining Investment
description: Our client Excavator Mining Co is considering developing a copper ore mine site. They can develop alone, enter a joint venture (JV) with competitor Drillhammer Mining Co, or not develop at all. Drillhammer owns a nearby site that could also supply the market. Should Excavator develop alone, with Drillhammer, or not at all? What arrangements should be made with Drillhammer, if any?
additional_info: Excavator site capacity: 1 million tonnes per annum (standalone), 2 million tonnes under JV; 20 year mine life.
Drillhammer site capacity: 1 million tonnes per annum; 20 year mine life.
Copper ore price: $50 per tonne.
Operating costs per tonne vary by scenario (Excavator alone: $35; Drillhammer alone: $53; JV: $30).
Capital investments: Excavator alone $180M, Drillhammer alone $140M, JV $340M.
Total value: Excavator alone $120M, Drillhammer alone -$200M, JV $460M.
JV creates highest overall industry value.
Discussion on how JV value should be split considering next best alternatives.
Without JV, Drillhammer loses money and will not invest; Excavator alone value $120M.
With JV, total value $460M; JV adds $340M value to industry.
Suggested split: Drillhammer $170M, Excavator $290M.
Considerations: history of JVs, alternative uses for mine sites, infrastructure sharing.
Bonus: sensitivity to copper price changes.
additional_questions: Is a straight 50/50 split of JV value fair?
How should the JV value be split considering each party's alternatives?
How would changes in copper prices affect the decision?
What if Drillhammer’s site becomes profitable?
solution: Excavator should pursue the JV and negotiate to receive at least $290M worth of value. The JV creates significantly more value than either party developing alone. The value split should reflect each party’s next best alternative. Sensitivity to copper prices should be considered.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate financial attractiveness of each option, understand JV value creation, analyze negotiation positions, and discuss sensitivity to key assumptions.

title: Telecom Service Provider
description: Our client is a telecommunications service provider experiencing customer retention problems. What do you think can be drivers of such a problem?
additional_info: Drivers of retention problems include voluntary (deliberate and incidental) and involuntary reasons.

Voluntary drivers: technology (handsets, features), economics (per minute, activation, subscription fee), quality (coverage, call quality, customer service, billing), social factors (image, family and friends), convenience (internet, retail channels).
Involuntary drivers: fraud, non-payment.
There are 3 major players in the market; client has highest coverage but more expensive voice services.
Market size: 100M customers; market growth 1%.
Market shares: Client 50%, Firm A 30%, Firm B 20%.
Current churn rate 3%, expected to increase by 50% due to recession.
To maintain current subscribers, client must steal 1.75M customers from competitors.
additional_questions: What can our client do to decrease the churn rate?
In a recessionary environment with increased churn, how many customers must the client steal from competitors to maintain subscriber base?
solution: Client should implement customized price plans, predict defection and create targeted offers, develop loyalty and referral programs, improve coverage, and customize communication. To maintain subscriber base during recession, client must acquire approximately 1.75 million customers from competitors.
evaluation: Candidate should identify drivers of churn, propose retention strategies, perform churn-related calculations, and understand competitive dynamics.

title: Major Magazine Publisher
description: Your client is a major magazine publisher owning a Women’s magazine and a Personal Finance magazine. They are considering launching a new magazine about Fine Living targeted to Wealthy Males in the U.S. What is the market size, and should they do it?
additional_info: Goal: hit $10M in annual revenue.
Resources: client has resources to launch.
Magazine offered only in print, purchasable via yearly subscription (12 issues) or retail (one issue).
Target geography: U.S. only.
Costs can be ignored in this analysis.
Population: 300M in U.S., 50% male = 150M males.
Age groups evenly split into 0-20, 21-40, 41-60, 61-80.
Wealthy percentages: 10% for 21-40 and 41-60, 5% for 61-80; under 20 too young.
Market split 50/50 subscription and retail.
Subscription price: $3 per issue; retail price: $5 per issue.
Subscription: 12 issues/year; retail: 4 issues/year.
Market not growing; competition: GQ 60%, others 40%.
Survey shows client can capture 5% of market.
additional_questions: What is the market size (in people)?
What is the market size (in $)?
Should they launch the new magazine?
solution: Market size is approximately 9.4 million wealthy males. Total market revenue is about $263.2M. Capturing 5% market share yields $13.16M revenue, exceeding the $10M goal. Recommendation is to proceed with the launch, considering risks such as costs, cannibalization, and synergies.
evaluation: Candidate should perform market sizing, revenue calculations, and provide a recommendation with risk considerations.

title: Tulsa Hotel - OK or not OK?
description: Our client, a major hotel chain, is considering acquiring an existing hotel in Tulsa, OK for $20M and expects a 20% ROI over three years. Should they make the investment?
additional_info: Assume single occupancy, 50 weeks/year (350 days).
Weekend visitors: 600/day, 50% stay at hotel.
Weekend occupancy rate: 60%; weekday occupancy rate: 75%.
Weekend guests: 75% individuals, 25% groups; weekdays: 40% individuals, 60% groups.
Group room rate: $120/night; individual room rate: $150/night.
Cost per occupied room: $30/night.
Fixed costs: $5750/night.
No seasonality or growth; ignore time value of money.
Additional scenario: weekend 100 rooms at group rate $100, 300 individual rooms at $150; weekday 200 rooms group rate, 200 individual rooms.
Goal to increase revenues with constant costs; advertising campaign costing $1.5M over 3 years considered.
additional_questions: Should our client make the investment?
What is the average revenue per customer per day?
What are some ways to increase revenues assuming costs are constant?
How many additional guests are needed to break even on a $1.5M advertising campaign? Should they launch it?
solution: Projected profits over 3 years are $33M, exceeding the required $12M for 20% ROI on $20M investment; recommend acquisition. Average revenue per customer per day is approximately $130. Revenue can be increased by raising room prices, partnering with local venues, advertising, expanding facilities, or adding dining options. To break even on the advertising campaign, 15 additional guests per night are needed; launching the campaign is recommended.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze profitability, calculate average revenue, brainstorm revenue growth strategies, perform breakeven analysis, and consider risks.

title: The Coffee Grind
description: The CEO of a major client requests a study on a small division manufacturing automatic drip coffeemakers for the US and Canadian market. The division has steady volumes and healthy margins historically, but profits have recently declined. The CEO wants to understand what is going on and broader insights beyond basic profitability analysis.
additional_info: Production occurs at a Michigan facility operating at 90-95% capacity.
Product mix: 4-cup, 10-cup, 12-cup coffeemakers; mix steady.
No major changes in fixed or variable costs recently.
Market: new low-priced competitors have entered; client’s products are mid-price range.
Minimal new technology or substitutes.
Sales data shows increasing share of sales through national chains, especially Wal-Mart (25% of sales).
National chains have more bargaining power, squeezing margins.
Estimated market size: 10-15 million coffeemakers sold annually in US and Canada; client has 5-8% market share.
CEO asks if launching a website to sell coffeemakers would work.
Website costs: $500k annual, marketing $300k annual; average retail price $60; margin 40%; $4 handling cost per unit; customer pays shipping.
Break-even volume for website: 40,000 units (~5% of sales).
additional_questions: What conclusions can you draw from sales data by retailer category?
What are implications of shift towards national retailers?
Would a website sales channel work for coffeemakers?
What percent of sales volume is needed to break even on website costs?
What other solutions might you suggest to improve profitability?
solution: Margins are squeezed by national retailers’ bargaining power. Website sales could capture margin but require careful analysis of costs, customer behavior, and competition. Break-even requires about 5% of sales through website. Other solutions include selling division, moving production overseas, acquisitions, exclusive retail agreements, marketing to increase pull, exploring new channels, international expansion, and price adjustments.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market and channel dynamics, interpret sales data, perform break-even calculations, evaluate new channel feasibility, and creatively suggest solutions.

title: FoodCo
description: Our client is a private equity firm invested in FoodCo, a family-owned $19M branded frozen ethnic foods manufacturer in the Northeast. They want help determining how FoodCo can triple their profitability over the next 2 years.
additional_info: Current profit is $3M; target profit is $9M.
No major market changes expected.
Price is at ideal level; no flexibility.
Revenue stream is solely from ethnic food sold in 'cups'.
Cannot build more operations centers due to capital constraints despite unmet demand.
Options include expanding nationally through retail and food service channels, new products, customers, or acquisitions.
Costs categorized as Labor (50%), Equipment (25%), Administrative (20%), Other (5%).
Labor is largest and most controllable cost driver.
Training and equipment reconfiguration can reduce repair time from 22% to 5% with $500k investment.
Labor cost base approx. $8M; savings from repair time reduction approx. $1.36M.
Efficiency savings translate to 17% increase in volume and revenues ($19M * 1.17 = $22.23M).
After $500k investment, profitability approx. $7M, still short of $9M target.
additional_questions: What ideas do you have to improve efficiency?
Should the client invest $500k to reduce repair time?
How does efficiency improvement translate to revenue growth?
What is your overall recommendation?
solution: Investing $500k to reduce repair time is justified by $1.36M labor savings and 17% revenue growth. However, this alone is insufficient to triple profitability; additional growth or cost reduction strategies are needed.
evaluation: Candidate should identify cost drivers, evaluate investment trade-offs, quantify efficiency gains, translate savings into revenue growth, and provide a realistic recommendation.

title: University Apartments
description: Your client is a real estate development corporation. It is looking to build an apartment complex on a vacant lot near a leading university in Philadelphia. What are some areas that should be examined to decide whether this is a good idea?
additional_info: This is a profitability case. Discussion should quickly turn to P=R-C and the various drivers of costs and revenues.

On the REVENUE side, price and volume (number of units built, % rented) should be considered, with some discussion about different price and purchase scenarios – i.e. how long would it take to sell all units, is there seasonality of occupancy?
On the COST side, fixed and variable costs should be discussed, such as land purchase, apartment construction, utilities, labor, insurance, etc.
What is the current MARKET for land and apartments, including zoning for the vacant lot?
How do the future tenants’ willingness to pay depend on the quality of apartment units built?
RISKS such as lower than expected demand for units, drop in real estate value
additional_questions: Assuming an average apartment size, how much must our client charge in rent per apartment per month to break even within three years?

Considering that our client can only build on 80% of the lot and can build apartments on 80% of the building itself, what are some other ways that they can earn revenue on the site or otherwise increase tenants’ willingness to pay?
solution: Breakeven rent per apartment per month is calculated at $1,310 based on total costs of $9.43M, 150 units with 80% occupancy (120 units), over 3 years. Additional revenue can be earned by adding stores, restaurants, dry cleaners, gyms, garages, laundry, pet zones, and guest services to increase tenants’ willingness to pay.
evaluation: Candidate should identify key revenue and cost drivers, correctly calculate breakeven rent, and creatively suggest additional revenue streams or value-added services to increase tenant willingness to pay.

title: Vidi-Games
description: Our client is Vidi-Games, a manufacturer and retailer of video games. They are planning to enter the Brazilian market. One of the factors that has prevented them from entering that market is a tariff for importing products that can be produced in the country of 50% of production costs. However, the government is planning to lower it down to 15% in the next years, decreasing it 5% every year. What variables should they consider? What is the market size? Should they enter the market? And if so, should they export the product from China to Brazil or produce it in Brazil?
additional_info: This is a profitability problem involving industry analysis, repatriation costs, regulations, currency, inflation, culture issues, etc.
Brazil population = 200M; 50% under 35 years old; 60% poor population; average household size = 4; 75% penetration of video games in affluent young segment; 3 games per year per household.
