Production costs: China $14, Brazil $21; Distribution $6; Labor $8; Overhead $6; Other costs $10.4.
Current tariff is 50%, decreasing 5% per year to 15%.
Retail price per game is $50.
Market size estimated at 22.5M video games per year.
additional_questions: What variables should they consider?
What is the market size?
Should they enter the market?
If so, should they export from China or produce in Brazil?
What could Vidi-Games do if they needed to enter now?
solution: Currently, the business is unprofitable due to high tariffs. The break-even tariff is 35%. Since tariffs will decrease 5% per year, the company should wait 3 years to enter the market profitably. At 15% tariff, profit per unit is $3.5, totaling $78.75M annually. Investment of $100M would be recouped in just over 3 years. If entering now, options include increasing price, negotiating cheaper labor, optimizing distribution, partnering with local firms, acquiring competitors, or selling wholesale to retailers.
evaluation: Candidate should structure the problem well, estimate market size accurately, analyze profitability under different tariff scenarios, and provide a clear recommendation with supporting rationale and alternative strategies.

title: Big School Bus Company
description: You have been approached by a private equity firm trying to decide whether or not to acquire Big School Bus Company. Big School Bus Company manufactures, owns, and operates school buses across the United States. The private equity firm wants you to help them estimate the size of the market for school buses and to identify ways in which you might be able to extract more value from Big School Bus Company, if acquired.
additional_info: About 60% of US schoolchildren take school buses every day.
Every school day, about 500,000 school buses transport ~26 million school children.
School bus manufacturing industry is dominated by two players; Big School Bus Company has about 45% market share by volume.
40% of school buses are owned and operated by independent companies like Big School Bus Company; the rest by school districts.
Average school bus capacity: 20 passengers for smaller buses (20% of fleet), 70 passengers for larger buses (80% of fleet).
School buses have a lifetime of 10-15 years; thus 1/10 to 1/15 of the market is addressable annually.
additional_questions: How large is the market for school buses?
What portion of the market could Big School Bus Company reasonably capture?
How would the firm extract more value (additional profits) from Big School Bus Company?
solution: Market sizing involves estimating number of children taking buses, bus capacity, number of buses needed, and replacement rate. Big School Bus Company holds 45% market share. To extract more value, focus on operations/services side rather than manufacturing due to market maturity. Ideas include expanding customer base to businesses and wealthy parents, offering differentiated services (WiFi, homework support), alternative pricing models (per passenger or flat fee), and cost structure optimization.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to perform market sizing, segment customers, analyze market share, and creatively propose revenue and cost strategies to increase value.

title: American Beauty Company
description: American Beauty Company (ABC) is a high quality beauty products company experiencing declining revenues and market shares in its hair color product line. Retailers have complained about their products as competitor Bell International takes over. The firm has been called in to advise ABC on what to do.
additional_info: ABC sells through retail and drugstores with all manufacturing in-house and has an 800 number for customer support.
One of the biggest market segments is women aged 18-55.
Market segments data: Women $800M market size with 5% growth, Men $200M with 20% growth, Teens $100M with 10% growth.
Current market shares: Women 50%, Men 10%, Teens 30%.
Survey data shows Bell has better brand awareness and perception of quality than ABC among users and non-users.
ABC has low penetration in men’s segment.
additional_questions: How would you start thinking about this problem?
Why might ABC’s share be declining in the 18-55 women segment? How would you approach this issue?
Using the data, what sales are required for ABC to have 50% of the women’s market in 2 years?
What is the dollar market share for ABC currently and in 2 years?
What do you notice from the brand awareness and perception survey and what do you suggest ABC can do about it?
solution: Declining revenues and market share are mainly due to Bell’s superior brand awareness and perception of quality. ABC should focus on improving brand awareness through advertising and perception through promotions and joint marketing with retailers. The women’s segment is maturing; ABC should target men’s segment for growth. Teen segment is small but may offer opportunities to expand market size through innovation.
evaluation: Candidate should identify key problem areas (brand awareness, perception, market segmentation), use data to calculate market shares and growth, interpret survey results, and provide actionable recommendations.

title: Unicloth
description: Our client, Unicloth, is an Asian clothing retailer attempting to establish a profitable presence in the United States. However, since they arrived five years ago, they have struggled to achieve that goal and have engaged our firm to find out why and to recommend next steps. How can we help?
additional_info: This company sells casual clothing, think jeans, t-shirts, knit sweaters, dresses, etc.
They follow the designs of the company’s home market in Asia.
They are manufactured in China and Bangladesh.
The retail market has been stable, no economic downturns, etc.
Price: average product price is ~$40. This is in line with mid-tier competitors such as American retailer Bap and a bit below European retailer Mara.
The retailer has a US presence comprised of three mall stores plus one flagship store on 5th Avenue.
Three mall stores sell on average 1,375 items per day each.
Flagship store sells 4,500 items per day.
Profit margin on clothing sales is 30%.
Flagship store rent: $1.5M per month; mall stores rent: $200K per month per store.
Maintenance of stores, utilities, etc. cost $5M per year.
Labor costs detailed for flagship and mall stores.
Storage, sending unsold clothes back to warehouse, markdowns, etc. cost $12M per year.
Manufacturing is already done in the cheapest manner possible.
Shipping by boat instead of air could cut 5% of COGS, saving $4.5M annually.
Sharing rent with another business at flagship store could save 25% of rent, about $1.75M annually.
Adjusting designs and sizes for American market will cost $12M annually but provide $23M additional revenue per year.
solution: Currently revenues are $125M annually, costs $138M, a $13M loss. Recommendations: switch shipping from air to boat to save $4.5M; share flagship store rent with a partner (e.g., coffee shop) to save $1.75M; revamp inventory for American market at $12M cost to gain $23M revenue, netting $11M incremental income. Together, these measures yield $17.25M incremental income, turning a $13M loss into a $4.25M profit. Risks include unreliable retail partner, product acceptance, and stocking delays. Recommended next steps: study retail partner options, conduct market research for SKUs, adjust warehouse operations.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to break down revenue and cost components, perform basic calculations, identify key cost drivers and revenue levers, brainstorm practical solutions, and provide a clear, data-supported recommendation with risk assessment.

title: Brazilian Highway Concessions
description: A leading Brazilian highway concessions company is looking to expand internationally. Economic growth in Brazil has stalled, and in order to continue to grow both top-line revenues and bottom-line profitability, the client wants to diversify its portfolio and decrease its exposure to the Brazilian economy. What factors should the client consider as it thinks through its international expansion options?
additional_info: The client operates only in Brazil, has scoped opportunities in South America. Their staff speaks primarily Portuguese.
The client currently only focuses on road concessions (building and operating public roadways).
Clients are municipal, state, or national governments, winning business through competitive RFPs.
Client wants to consider all geographies, with a bias towards South America.
Consider culture and management complexity: language barriers, managerial fit, geographical distance.
Political environment: regulation for privatization, political volatility, receptiveness to business.
Pipeline and economic prospects: project pipeline, infrastructure needs, country growth.
Competitive environment: current players, concentration, competition on bids.
Opportunities outside South America are not worth pursuing due to cultural and managerial complexity.
Top South American markets: Mexico, Colombia, Chile, Peru, possibly Argentina.
Market entry options: greenfield/primary investment, joint venture (JV), or acquisition (M&A).
No viable JV opportunities; client must choose between primary investment or M&A.
Primary investment: higher control, limited market knowledge.
M&A: more expensive, local market knowledge, lower idiosyncratic risk.
Quantitative data provided for primary investment vs. M&A including revenue, opex, investment, payback, ROIC.
additional_questions: Based on the graph, which markets should our client focus their efforts on? Which should it definitely eliminate?
Assuming the client chooses to enter one or more of these markets, how should it approach market entry?
What are the primary pros and cons of each market entry approach?
If you were going to evaluate the two opportunities side by side, which inputs would you need to compare the value of each investment?
Once interviewee has come up with an ROIC, ask them for their conclusion.
What should the client do?
solution: The client should enter a South American market (preferably Mexico, Chile, or Colombia) through a primary investment. These markets have cultural similarity, low managerial complexity, large pipelines, and attractive business environments. Primary investment yields higher ROIC (40%) and shorter payback (7 years) compared to M&A (20% ROIC, 8.33 years payback). Follow-on steps include negotiating M&A prices, sensitivity analysis to macroeconomic factors, and assessing likelihood of winning bids as a primary investor.
evaluation: Candidate should identify relevant factors for international expansion, interpret graphical data to prioritize markets, compare market entry modes qualitatively and quantitatively, calculate ROIC and payback, and provide a clear recommendation with rationale and next steps.

title: Snack Foods Acquisition
description: A US snack foods company specializing in snacking peanuts, Peanut Co., is planning to acquire another company specializing in snacking almonds, Almond Co. Peanut Co. is currently the market leader in snacking peanuts, but the overall segment is growing slowly compared to the market and they want to diversify. They have hired you to tell them whether this is a good idea.
additional_info: We are only looking at the snacking almond market; almonds for cooking are excluded.
The almond industry is growing and considered higher in nutrients compared to peanuts.
Almond Co.’s current market share is 10%, profit margin 50%, revenues $1B, profits $500M.
Purchase price for Almond Co. is $1.5B.
Market sizing assumptions: US population 300M; 1 packet = 16 oz; price per packet $2.
Population segments with different consumption frequencies provided.
Potential benefits: cross-sell to peanut customers, leverage distribution, extend innovation.
Potential risks: cannibalization, brand dilution.
Payback period calculated as 3 years; doubling purchase price and halving market share increases payback to 12 years.
solution: Either acquiring or not acquiring Almond Co. can be justified. Acquisition benefits include a large and growing market, quick payback (3 years), and synergy potential. Risks include cannibalization and brand dilution. If acquiring, next steps include negotiating deal price and defining innovation transfer. If not acquiring, risks include competitor advantage and weaker position; next steps include exploring other acquisition targets.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to size market, calculate payback, assess profitability and synergies, weigh risks and benefits, and provide a balanced recommendation with supporting data and next steps.

title: U.S. Shoe Manufacturing
description: A major U.S. shoe manufacturer is currently manufacturing its entire product line domestically. Because of increased labor costs and competitive pressure, the manufacturer is now interested in understanding whether it should offshore some or all of its production and, if so, where it should offshore to and what percent of its total product line should be manufactured onshore vs. offshore. What factors should the client consider as it compares onshore to offshore manufacturing?
additional_info: Client also manufactures some apparel and sells in developed markets (North America, Europe, Australia).
Most competitors do not offshore due to manufacturing and managerial complexity.
Lower quality shoes tend to be manufactured in China, Southeast Asia, Central America; high quality in Eastern Europe.

Factors to consider: demand volatility, growth, diversity; competition; service level; access to human capital; manufacturing and general infrastructure; process innovation; supply volatility; lead time; supplier availability; labor and total costs; capital investments; tariffs, quotas, trade agreements; exchange rates; political stability; cultural affinity.
Unit profitability formula: profit = retail revenue – (COGS + labor + SG&A + transportation + quality + tariffs + retail margin).
Data provided comparing U.S. and Vietnam manufacturing costs and profitability.
Vietnam manufacturing is 50% more profitable per unit but has higher defect rates, transportation, and tariff costs.
Vietnam lead time is 3 months vs. 1 month in U.S., affecting working capital costs.
Equation provided to calculate optimal offshoring fraction considering demand volatility, holding cost, and cost advantage.
Recommended manufacturing split: 80% Vietnam, 20% U.S.
additional_questions: What additional factors should the client add to the unit profitability model to make the right sourcing decision?
Is it possible that the client should split manufacturing across the two options, and if so, what proportion should be made offshore vs. onshore?
solution: Client should manufacture 80% of shoes in Vietnam and 20% in the U.S. to hedge against demand volatility and cost of capital. Vietnam offers a $5 per unit cost advantage driven by lower COGS and labor, partially offset by quality, transportation, and tariffs. Longer lead times offshore increase working capital costs, justifying partial onshore production. Follow-on steps include sensitivity analysis on quality, transportation, tariffs, and demand volatility.
evaluation: Candidate should identify relevant cost components, perform unit profitability calculations, incorporate lead time and working capital considerations, apply quantitative models to determine optimal sourcing split, and provide a clear recommendation with sensitivity considerations.

title: Chicago Parking Meters
description: The city of Chicago is planning to sell the rights to all of its parking meters for 20 years to a private company. The idea is that in exchange for a lump sum, the city of Chicago would turn over the operation and revenue stream of its ~40,000 parking spaces to a private operator. Your consulting firm has been hired by the Company Parking GenNext to give a reasonable price for the rights to collect all money from Chicago’s meters over a 20 year timeframe to win the competitive bid. How would you go about estimating it?
additional_info: Money collected on parking tickets goes to the city, not the company.
Chicago has ~40,000 metered spaces divided into three rings with different prices per hour and meter percentages.
Ring I (Downtown Loop): $3/hour, 3% of meters, usage 12 hours/day.
Ring II (Central Business District): $2/hour, 16% of meters, usage 8 hours/day.
Ring III (Suburbs + Others): $1/hour, 81% of meters, usage 4 hours/day.
Assume weekend and weekday usage are the same, 30 days/month, 12 months/year.
One-time investment to upgrade meters: 5,000 new meters replacing 40,000 old ones (each new meter handles 8 spaces), cost $20K each.
Recurring expenses: labor (1 person per 10 machines or 80 spaces), wage $10/hour, 8 hours/day, 300 days/year; maintenance $200 per machine per month.
Growth rate assumed zero for calculations.
Time value of money and discount rate considerations may be ignored or included.
Sample numerical solution estimates 20-year revenue ~$2 billion, costs ~$600 million, net present value ~$1.4 billion.
Creative considerations include alternative pricing strategies (e.g., surge pricing), technology advancements (mobile apps), and reputation/image management.
solution: Estimate annual revenue by multiplying number of meters, price per hour, hours used per day, days per year. Calculate one-time upgrade costs and recurring expenses (labor, maintenance). Sum costs over 20 years and subtract from total revenue to estimate net present value (~$1.4 billion). Consider alternative pricing strategies and technology to enhance revenue. Recommend bid price based on NPV and strategic considerations.
evaluation: Candidate should structure revenue and cost estimation clearly, perform accurate calculations, consider growth and discounting, brainstorm innovative pricing and service ideas, and provide a justified valuation recommendation.

title: High engineer attrition at SLS Oil & Gas Services
description: Our client SLS is one of the world’s largest oil and gas services providers operating in 85 countries. They are seeing a very high attrition rate among their Field Engineer (FE) population across several offices globally, with the Mumbai office in India being one of the most affected. The CEO is concerned and has asked you for advice specifically for the Mumbai office. She hopes that if the problem can be fixed in Mumbai, similar fixes can be implemented everywhere else. What are the causes of high attrition and what recommendations would you make?
additional_info: Field Engineers work with specialized oilfield equipment offshore on rigs owned by clients.
FEs are hired from top engineering schools, with compensation and global travel as key differentiators.
Attrition rate in Mumbai was 28% in 2016.
Attrition defined as number of FEs quitting in a year divided by average number of FEs.
FEs mostly leave to pursue Masters or change industries.
Mumbai office has 25 rigs with constant activity over 5 years.
Number of engineers has been constant; number quitting has increased; transfers out decreased to zero; transfers in increased.
Utilization (% FEs not on vacation) and effective utilization (% FEs offshore) trends show widening gap, indicating inefficient use of FEs in office.
Only 1 or 2 FEs needed in office daily; many FEs in office not making bonus.
Math question: calculate number of FEs expected in office daily and how many can be sent on vacation given operational needs.
solution: Causes of high attrition include poor offshore conditions (food, internet, accommodation), inefficient staffing leading to fewer bonuses, lack of team spirit, and possibly management decisions reducing transfers out. Recommendations include short-term actions like more time off, team-building, hiring more engineers; long-term actions like revamping staffing model, hiring culturally compatible FEs, and reviewing compensation. Calculations show that with 50 FEs, 14 can be sent on vacation while meeting operational needs.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to understand operational context, analyze qualitative and quantitative data, identify root causes of attrition, and propose actionable short- and long-term recommendations supported by data.

title: Salt Lake City Airport
description: Our company is pitching to the President of the Salt Lake City International Airport next week, hoping to earn a big contract with the Airport moving forward. The SLC Airport is the only commercial airport for more than 2.5M people in the greater Salt Lake City area, is the 21st busiest in the nation with 650 flights per day, and is owned entirely by the City of Salt Lake. The Airport President is a mayor-appointed individual who oversees all aspects of the airport’s operation.
additional_info: A passenger is measured by a person who passes through the airport, either to board or deplane an aircraft. People who are passing through the airport on a layover switching planes are considered one passenger.
Taxi drivers, Uber drivers, and people picking up their friends are not passengers.
Pilots, Flight Attendants, and TSA Workers are not counted as passengers; only people with a paid ticket to fly are counted.
Each visit to the airport counts as a passenger. For example, a family of four traveling roundtrip counts as 8 passengers.
The airport has 650 flights per day.
Average plane has 30 rows of 6 seats per row (180 seats per flight).
Most flights are 75-80% full.
Population in the greater Salt Lake City area is 2.5 million.
The airport does not currently have a sit-down restaurant or a lounge.
The restaurant would bring in a $15 check per passenger and cost $6 per passenger.
The lounge would cost $30 per passenger who enters and cost $12 per passenger to provide.
The money to build both projects has already been accounted for through municipal bonds, so construction costs can be ignored.
additional_questions: What are the most important things the Airport President must think about?
How many passengers does the SLC Airport handle annually?

The President of the airport is thinking about which of two projects to undertake: open a sit down restaurant or open a lounge in the terminal. Which of the two options would you suggest the President pursue?
solution: The airport handles approximately 31.5 million passengers annually based on flight and population estimates. The lounge is the preferred project because it offers higher profit per passenger ($18 vs $9), aligns better with the majority business traveler passenger profile, and is favored by airport vendors who would not want a sit-down restaurant to cannibalize their business.
evaluation: Candidate should structure their thoughts clearly, consider stakeholders (passengers, airlines, vendors), use airport-related terminology, perform reasonable math to estimate passenger volume, and weigh financial and qualitative factors in project recommendation.

title: Clothing Chain Goes Global and Local
description: Yermakov Ltd, a US-based clothing company with stores in 65 countries and 100,000 employees, is localizing its image by acquiring a dozen high-end boutiques and specialty clothing shops. Six months into this post-acquisition era, Yermakov is experiencing 'growing pains' with many acquired employees declining to continue, and lower than expected customer acquisition and retention in local fashion markets. Yermakov seeks help stabilizing their workforce and communicating a compelling change agenda.
additional_info: The acquired companies are much smaller, with the largest employee base being 120 people.
Employees of acquired companies have deep, specialized knowledge and passion for local fashion.
The other consulting firm that helped analyze acquisitions is no longer working with Yermakov, but a point of contact remains.
Most acquired companies are located in countries where Yermakov already has a presence.
Employees generally had about a month’s notice before acquisition.
Yermakov does not have an in-house team with expertise in local fashion.
additional_questions: Why did you prioritize these three work streams?
If you had to prioritize one work stream, which would it be and why?
What is the biggest risk with your plan and how will you minimize it?
How would you deal with the challenge of different technology platforms across acquired companies?
solution: A strong approach includes three prioritized work streams: Communication/Stakeholder Analysis and Change Management; Talent Management including gap analysis and career pathing; and Culture/Employee Engagement. Recommendations include clear communication of vision and benefits, two-way communication channels, competency modeling, training, balancing cultures, and measuring progress via surveys and engagement metrics. Addressing risks and leveraging firm-specific resources are important.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure a human capital framework, prioritize work streams, address risks, incorporate firm resources, and link human capital initiatives to business outcomes such as retention and productivity.

title: Pharma Company Goes International, Outsources Benefits, Integrates New Technology
description: Waltham&Rose, a US-based pharmaceutical company, is expanding internationally to Germany and France, increasing employees by 50%. To ease workloads, they consider outsourcing health benefits management and integrating a cloud-based CRM software for all employees, many of whom have worked there for decades. They seek help developing and implementing a seamless CRM adoption process and ensuring new European employees feel part of the company.
additional_info: No firm plan yet for senior executives relocating to Europe.
No in-house expertise on European business regulations.
Workforce is largely middle-aged employees with long tenure.
No plan yet for outsourcing healthcare benefits; outsourcing is new for the company.
additional_questions: Why did you prioritize these three work streams?
If Waltham&Rose can’t afford to do as much business initially, which work stream would you prioritize?
How could you set the stage for additional business opportunities later?
What is the biggest risk with your plan and how will you minimize it?
How will you address reluctance to invest in training?
solution: Recommended work streams include Organizational Gap Analysis and Job Design; Training and Learning Strategy; and Technology Adoption. Activities include predictive competency modeling, revamped job descriptions, recruitment strategy, training tailored to diverse learning styles, technology adoption plans with communication and contingency, and evaluation via usage and engagement metrics. Leadership involvement and addressing outsourcing transition risks are critical.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure a human capital framework, prioritize work streams, address risks, incorporate firm resources, and link human capital initiatives to business outcomes such as adoption rates and employee engagement.

title: Competitive Strategy in Mining (Commodities)
description: Our client is an Australian mining company, the largest producer of iron ore sold exclusively to China, producing 230 million tons per year at $27/ton cost. The total Chinese demand is 980 million tons per year. The client has won a concession to mine a new adjacent site, increasing production to 360 million tons per year (an additional 130 million tons). Is this expansion worth doing?
additional_info: Board approves projects with payback in less than 5 years (no discounting).
Market demand is expected to remain flat at 980 million tons per year.
Upfront investment required is $3 billion.
Cost of new production is the same as current ($27/ton).
Competitive landscape limited to iron ore and China for this case.
Exhibit A shows China's cash-cost curve for iron ore supply including royalties and ocean freight.
additional_questions: Evaluate if the project has payback in less than 5 years.
How will competitors react to the increased production?
Assuming competitor B can also increase production, what should our client do?
solution: The new equilibrium price will drop from $60 to about $50/ton due to increased supply. The client’s profit increases by $690 million per year, yielding a payback period of ~4 years, which meets the board’s criteria. Competitor B will lose margin and may react by increasing production, reducing production, cutting costs, or pursuing M&A. Considering competitor B’s possible expansion, the dominant strategy for the client is to expand production to minimize losses or maximize gains regardless of competitor actions.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze price-volume-cost dynamics, calculate payback period, anticipate competitor reactions, consider strategic options, and recommend a dominant strategy with supporting quantitative analysis.

title: Phighting Phillies
description: Our client, Alpha Capital, is a private equity firm that is considering buying the Philadelphia Phillies. The current team owners approached Alpha about purchasing the team for $1.1B. Alpha engaged our firm in the due diligence process and wants us to help them understand: A. What is the team worth? B. Should they make this investment?
additional_info: Fund structure: This asset will go into a new fund without hold period constraints or previous investments.
Hurdle rates, hold periods, other constraints: Assume these are not major issues.
Impetus / motivation for the deal: We want to maximize our investment. Also assume that fund leadership is filled with avid Phillies fans who want the team to be successful.
How does the team make money? Major revenue streams are ticket sales, concessions, merchandise, media rights, and advertising/sponsorships.
What are the major cost drivers? Player salaries, front office costs, sales & advertising, and stadium/facilities costs.
Does the team own or lease its stadium and other facilities? Assume the city of Philadelphia owns these and we are paying annual leasing costs.
Should we consider revenues and costs associated with Spring Training and the farm system? No, exclude these from analysis.
Are there other potential bidders for the team? Assume there are not other bidders and we have the first shot at buying the team.
Current Revenues: Ticket Sales $80M, Concessions $40M, Merchandise $10M, Media Rights $120M, Sponsorships/Ads $50M, Total Revenue $300M.
Current Costs: Player Salaries $110M, Front Office Costs $20M, Sales & Advertising $50M, Stadium/Facility Costs $20M, Total Costs $200M.
Annual EBIT $100M, Taxes $0, Discount Rate 10%, Valuation $1B.

Synergies: Potential cost savings in sales & advertising $10M; potential revenue boosts from dynamic ticket pricing, renegotiated media deals, higher value sponsors, revamped concessions totaling $30M; total synergies $40M increasing valuation to $1.4B.
Other considerations: management quality, ability to execute, fan-base reaction, league approval, regulatory/legal risks, tension between investment and winning, alternative investments.
solution: The valuation without synergies is $1B, below the asking price of $1.1B. Including synergies, valuation rises to $1.4B, exceeding asking price. Alpha should consider making the investment if they can realize these synergies. Other factors such as management quality, league approval, and fan reaction must be considered. The investment is viable if synergies are achievable and risks manageable.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to build a detailed financial model including revenues, costs, and synergies; perform valuation using discount rate; identify key risks and qualitative factors; and synthesize a recommendation balancing quantitative and qualitative insights.

title: Insurance for the Underserved in India
description: The Indian insurance market is heavily underpenetrated. The government is considering using the Indian postal network to distribute insurance products to underserved populations. Analyze the opportunity and advise on the viability and profitability of this approach.
additional_info: Q1: What considerations should the government think about? (e.g., definition of underserved, profitability vs social goals, demand vs supply issues, postal network capabilities, economics).

Q2: Market sizing: The underserved population is defined as aspirers (55M households, $7,500 income, 1.5% income spent on insurance) and strugglers (33M households, $2,000 income, 2.0% income spent on insurance). Total market size for these segments is about $6B.

Q3: Indian Postal Network Penetration: There are 150,000 post offices; 1/3 (50,000) will provide insurance in year one. Each agent can sell insurance to 10 aspirer and 5 struggler households per month. This represents about 12.85% of the market.

Q4: Profitability: Government pays out 85% of premiums in claims, 10% commissions to agents, $100/month base salary per agent, and $100/month branch operating costs. Total costs exceed revenues, resulting in an $80M loss in year one.
Q5: Should the government pursue this? Given social objectives, yes. Profitability can be improved by pricing, increasing coverage, and expanding branch participation.
additional_questions: What considerations should the government think about?
How do we define underserved and what is the size of the insurance market?
What percent of the overall market can the Indian Postal Network cover?
How much money will the postal network make in the first year of launch?
Given the economics, should the government pursue with the insurance through Indian postal network? How can it improve the profitability?
solution: The postal network can cover about 12.85% of the underserved insurance market but will incur losses initially due to high claims and operating costs. Despite losses, the government should pursue the initiative to provide a safety net. Profitability can be improved by better pricing, expanding branch coverage, and increasing sales per agent.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to segment market, perform market sizing calculations, analyze operational capacity, calculate profitability including costs and claims, and provide thoughtful recommendations balancing social impact and financial sustainability.

title: The National Park Service
description: The United States Department of the Interior is concerned about low job retention among Law Enforcement rangers in the National Park Service. Explore the size of the problem and suggest ways to improve retention.
additional_info: Costs of rangers leaving include direct (recruiting, training, severance) and indirect (morale, reputation, operational inefficiencies, loss of knowledge).

In 2016, number of rangers leaving and total rangers: Law Enforcement (98 left, 2100 total), Education (175 left, 3750 total), Maintenance (215 left, 8615 total), Administrative (251 left, 9143 total).
Cost per lost ranger: Law Enforcement $35,300, Education $6,200, Maintenance $3,500, Administrative $3,500.
Law Enforcement rangers have the highest total cost impact despite fewer leaving.
Survey identified three pain points: Family Friendliness, Effective Senior Leadership, Performance-Based Rewards.
Potential solutions include better matching of assignments, leadership training, improved benefits, and performance incentives.
additional_questions: What are some of the costs to the National Parks Service of rangers leaving?
What is the cost associated with each type of ranger leaving?
Can you brainstorm potential solutions to improve retention within Family Friendliness, Effective Senior Leadership, and Performance-Based Rewards?
solution: Retention issues among Law Enforcement rangers are costly and should be prioritized. Solutions include improving family-friendly policies (local sourcing, tutoring), enhancing leadership development (mentorship, training), and revising compensation and rewards (salary, overtime). Next steps include evaluating costs and feasibility of these solutions.
evaluation: Candidate should structure costs into direct and indirect, quantify impact by ranger type, prioritize based on cost impact, brainstorm targeted solutions in given categories, and synthesize a clear recommendation.

title: Penn & Teller
description: Caesars, a major gaming and resort company, is considering whether to renew the contract with Penn & Teller, the longest-running headlining show in Vegas history, or replace it with a new act. Should Caesars renew the contract?
additional_info: Caesars cares about total bottom line profitability.
Penn & Teller perform 6 shows per week, 40 weeks per year, in a 1500-seat theater with 3 seat categories (A: 300 seats, B: 800 seats, C: 400 seats).
Average seat fill rates: A 100%, B 80%, C 50%. Prices: A $120, B $75, C $55.
Annual revenue from ticket sales is approximately $22.8M.
Annual costs include performer contracts ($4M), crew ($480K), housekeeping ($40K), props ($48K), utilities ($52K), SG&A ($180K), totaling about $4.8M.
Annual profit is about $18M with ~80% margin.
Potential improvements include pricing strategies, seat reallocation, concessions, bundling, and cost management.

Alternative is to replace P&T with America’s Got Talent (AGT) winner, which would cost $2.4M upfront, have lower revenues ($90K per show vs $95K), lower costs ($3M vs $4.8M), and a 4-year payback period.
AGT show is $600K more profitable annually but with risks of long payback and uncertain appeal.
additional_questions: How profitable is the Penn & Teller show?
What can Caesars do to make the current show more profitable?
Should Caesars switch the P&T show for one featuring the recent winner of America’s Got Talent?
solution: Penn & Teller is highly profitable with $18M annual profit. The AGT show offers slightly higher profit but requires a $2.4M upfront investment with a 4-year payback, which is risky in a competitive market. Recommendation is to renew P&T contract and explore ways to increase revenues and reduce costs.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate revenues and costs accurately, analyze profit margins, brainstorm revenue and cost improvement ideas, evaluate alternative show economics including payback period, and provide a clear recommendation with rationale.

title: Wellington Equestrian Festival
description: Your client, Mark Bellissimo, CEO of Wellington Equestrian Partners, wants to assess the total economic impact of the Winter Equestrian Festival on the community and brainstorm ways to expand the business to attract investment.
additional_info: Economic impact includes revenue from competitions (entry fees, training, stabling, veterinary, equipment), spectators, hotels, restaurants, and retail.
Estimated annual economic impact is approximately $112.5 million.

Expansion ideas include extending festival duration, multiple festivals per year, repurposing venue, increasing exhibitors and spectators, acquiring property, partnerships with hotels and tourism, media attention, sponsorships, school partnerships, new locations, and diversification.
Risks include loss of prestige, competition, costs, uncertain returns, and reputational damage.
The Winter Equestrian Festival is a 12-week event with 15,000 exhibitors, 3,000 horses, and significant prize money and vendor activity.
solution: The festival contributes over $112.5M annually. Expansion plans such as multiple festivals, increased attendance, and new locations can grow economic impact and attract investors. Risks must be managed through careful evaluation and leveraging market position.
evaluation: Candidate should identify relevant revenue streams, perform economic impact calculations, generate creative and structured expansion ideas, assess risks, and synthesize a compelling investment pitch.

title: Medical Devices Co.
description: Medical Devices Co. manufactures BloodStopper, a blood clotting product sold in liquid vials. They are considering a deal with sponge manufacturer Spongy’s to create a hybrid product combining BloodStopper in tablet form with a sponge. Should Medical Devices do the deal, and if so, what terms should they negotiate?
additional_info: Consumers: 1,000 hospitals in the U.S. The combined product offers operational efficiency but no improved medical benefit.
Pricing: WTP for liquid vial $85, sponge $10, combined product WTP is 10% higher than sum, i.e. $104.5.
Costs: Sponge manufacturing cost $2, BloodStopper liquid vial COGS $5, tablet form COGS $3.50.

Margins: Original margin for liquid vial $80 ($85-$5). To maintain margin, Medical Devices would charge Spongy’s $83.50 for tablet. Spongy’s margin on combined product would be $19 ($104.5 - $83.5 - $2).
Medical Devices owns IP on BloodStopper; sponge is a commodity with many competitors.
Deal structure options include vertical integration, volume discounts, revenue sharing.
No financial or operational targets currently set; goal is to maintain or improve profitability.
solution: Medical Devices should not proceed with the deal as proposed because maintaining its margin results in high costs for Spongy’s, reducing their incentive to market the product. Alternatives include vertical integration by acquiring sponge manufacturers or restructuring the deal with volume discounts or revenue sharing to align incentives.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze pricing and margins for both companies, assess competitive dynamics, evaluate deal structures, and recommend a financially and strategically sound approach.

title: Vivid Screens: Sales Volume & Discounting
description: SKP has collected all data comparing the contract prices at which the screens were sold and the corresponding contract volumes. Marketing has set a list price of $400 for the screen and a discount authority floor of 40% for the sales team. Analyze the discount/volume data and provide insights.
additional_info: Almost 40% of sales violates the 40% discounting floor, and there’s a peak at the stated limit—salesmen are discounting as much as they can get away with.
Weighted average selling price is $228/unit.
List price * (1 - discount) = Selling price. For example, $400 * 50% = $200.
Total revenue and volume data provided for different discount levels.
Value chain cost structure: Supplier cost $200 + $20 profit, Vivid cost $200, TV manufacturer cost $300 + $30 profit, Retailer cost $150 + $30 profit.
Retail price is $1,000.
Customer feedback indicates high willingness-to-pay and high switching costs for Vivid’s screens.
Salesforce is under pressure to meet quotas, leading to heavy discounting.
There is $150 in profit surplus distributed throughout the supply chain; Vivid retains about $28, TV manufacturer retains $72.
Vivid should consider a price increase to capture more surplus, targeting an ASP of $240.
solution: The data shows rampant discounting below the authorized floor, driven by sales incentives focused on volume rather than profit. Vivid retains less profit than the TV manufacturer despite high customer willingness-to-pay and switching costs. Recommendations include revising sales incentives to include profit-based components, implementing a more granular discount authority system, and pursuing a price increase to capture more value, targeting an ASP of $240.
evaluation: Candidate should identify discounting behavior and its impact on profitability, analyze value chain profit distribution, interpret customer feedback on willingness-to-pay and switching costs, and recommend actionable strategies including sales incentive realignment and pricing adjustments.

title: Home Inspection Co. Market Entry
description: Our client, Home Inspection Co., is a third-party independent home inspection company currently operating in Ohio. They want to determine if they should expand to more states, and if so, which state(s) and how?
additional_info: Client has narrowed down options to Colorado and Michigan.
Current revenue is $10 million.
Regulatory environment: Michigan allowed third-party inspectors 10 years ago with a 20% market limit; Colorado allowed 2.5 years ago with a 50% market limit.
Only one state can be expanded into at this time.
Market size is relatively flat.
Inspections are for single-family homes only.
Competition: Michigan has many competitors due to longer allowance of third-party inspections; Colorado has few competitors.
Household and population data provided for both states.
Inspection prices vary by state: $300 in Colorado, $400 in Michigan.
Inspection frequency: 1 inspection every 9 years in Colorado, 1 every 12 years in Michigan.
additional_questions: Analyze the Colorado market in terms of market size, competition, and distribution options.
Analyze the Michigan market in terms of market size, competition, and distribution options.
Assess risks and opportunities for market entry in both states.
Provide a recommendation on whether to expand and which state to choose.
solution: Colorado offers a $30 million yearly opportunity with few competitors but risks include geographic distance and potential regulatory changes. Michigan offers a $20 million opportunity with many competitors but benefits from geographic proximity to Ohio, reducing setup costs. Recommendation is to expand, with a detailed scouting of competitor locations and office setup feasibility in the chosen state.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to perform market sizing calculations, analyze competitive landscape, assess regulatory and operational risks, and synthesize findings into a clear market entry recommendation.

title: Engineering and Construction Company
description: A large engineering, procurement, and construction company has seen their valuation drop recently. They build large refineries and large industrial plants. They are global in nature and operate in four main regions. This company is a recent roll-up of three smaller companies that operate independently. They have $1bn in revenue. What would you think the main reasons for shrinking margins would be?
additional_info: Revenue growth has been strong organically and from acquisitions
There is a backlog of work – lots of business
Margins have been shrinking
The industry benchmark for optimal utilization is 80% and engineers make $100k each
Business Unit details: Unit 1: 1,800 engineers, 50% utilization; Unit 2: 3,000 engineers, 90% utilization; Unit 3: 1,200 engineers, 50% utilization
The company aims to increase margins by 10%

Possible concerns with integrating the three units include different corporate cultures, different global areas of operations, fundamentally different businesses, lack of sufficient reporting system, unclear communication of integration dynamics, and integration time frame
additional_questions: What would you think the main reasons for shrinking margins would be?
Will they make their margin goal? Is this a significant savings?
What would you think are some possible concerns with integrating the three units?
solution: The company can eliminate 750 engineer positions by optimizing utilization to 80%, saving $75m, which is a 7.5% margin increase, close to but not fully meeting the 10% margin increase goal. Concerns with integration include cultural differences, operational differences, reporting challenges, communication, and time frame.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to hypothesize margin shrinkage causes, structure the problem comprehensively, perform utilization and cost math correctly, and identify integration risks.

title: New Video Game – World of WarCraft
description: A well-known game designer has developed a new version of a game called World of WarCraft that consumers can play either alone or with friends over the internet. It will cost them $25m to advertise it. They require a 200% margin on their investment, should they invest the money?
additional_info: Sales channels and prices: Retail sales at $50 each, online monthly fees $10/month (50% of customers purchase for 6 months), subsequent version upgrades $30 each (33% of customers purchase)
Market size assumptions: US population 300 million, 20% are males aged 10-25, 10% of those play this type of video game, 50% of those will buy this game
Expected revenue per average customer is $90
additional_questions: What are the multiple sales channels and possible prices charged? What would the adoption rate be?
What is the expected revenue of the average customer?
Estimate the potential number of customers and the implied revenue?
solution: Estimated 1.5 million customers with $90 revenue each yields $135 million potential revenue, exceeding the $25m advertising cost and meeting the 200% margin requirement.
evaluation: Candidate should identify sales channels, estimate market size logically, calculate average revenue per customer, and assess profitability.

title: Two Power Companies
description: We are advising a large US power/utility company looking to merge with a rival. The merger would cost $300m in transaction fees and integration costs. Should they merge? Identify possible synergies and calculate savings.
additional_info: Possible synergies include decreased management costs, elimination of duplicate distribution lines, increased plant utilization, increased purchasing power, ability to lock in longer-term contracts, decreased competition, consolidated pricing, increased lobbying power
Coal purchase data: Texas buys 100 tons at $25/ton, Louisiana buys 50 tons at $30/ton
Post-merger coal price would be $24/ton
additional_questions: What are some possible synergies?
What would the possible savings be? Should they go ahead?
What would happen to your recommendation if the cost of coal was variable and went up $2/ton? List concerns and summarize.
solution: Merger saves $400k on coal costs by buying 150 tons at $24/ton instead of $25 and $30 separately. The $300m merger cost must be weighed against long-term savings and synergies. If coal price rises $2/ton, savings reduce, possibly affecting merger attractiveness.
evaluation: Candidate should identify synergies, perform cost savings math, and analyze impact of variable input costs on merger decision.

title: Drugstore Chain
description: A small drugstore chain just outside New York faces CVS entering the local market and outbidding them for real estate by paying twice as much per square foot. The CEO wants to know what CVS is doing to pay more and what the drugstore chain needs to do to compete.
additional_info: Metrics to measure: number of stores, transactions per store, revenue per store, profit per store, revenue per square foot, profit per square foot, labor costs/revenue, inventory turnover, profit per product section
CVS and drugstore both sell pharmacy and OTC general merchandise; drugstore also sells home wares
General merchandise has low to no margin
CVS: 1,000 stores, $2B revenue, $1.4B COGS; Drugstore: 25 stores, $50M revenue, $35M COGS
Average store size: CVS 1,000 sqft, Drugstore 1,500 sqft; Rent per sqft: CVS $200, Drugstore $100
SG&A 5% revenue, Labor 10% revenue for CVS; Labor 15% revenue for Drugstore
Product mix: CVS pharmacy 50%, general merchandise 50%, no housewares; Drugstore pharmacy 30%, general merchandise 30%, housewares 40%
additional_questions: What metrics would a drugstore use to measure itself?
Much of the general merchandise has low to no margin, is this surprising? Hypothesize why CVS is paying twice as much for square foot.
What are the sales and gross margins of each on a per store basis?
What is the pre-tax income on a per store basis for each?
Given the information, what would you advise the CEO?
solution: CVS pays more per square foot because it sells more profitable products in smaller stores with less labor cost. Drugstore's housewares have low profitability despite larger store size and lower rent. Drugstore needs to focus on higher margin products and improve labor efficiency to compete.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze metrics, compare profitability per store and per sqft, understand product mix impact on margins, and synthesize competitive strategy.

title: Beverage Company – Refresh Now Soda
description: A beverage company, a top three producer of soda beverages, is thinking of launching a new product called O-Natural-Flavored Bottled Water. They plan to distribute directly to retailers. What are the key risk factors they should consider and should they launch?
additional_info: Current product mix includes carbonated and non-carbonated beverages
Alternative sales channels: fairs and outdoor events, sports team marketing, health clubs, vending machines at schools and companies, spas and resorts
Considerations on marketing small quantities at higher prices vs. large quantities at big-box retailers

Concerns: economics of new product, realistic sales volume, competing brands, peer response, retailer willingness to carry, commodity nature and retailer private labels, company expertise, shelf space impact
additional_questions: What are some other channels they could sell this product through besides supermarket retailers?
What do you think would be most profitable to market: small quantities at higher prices or larger quantities at big-box retailers?
What concerns would you have with Refresh Now launching a new product?
solution: Candidate should identify multiple sales channels, weigh profitability trade-offs, and highlight risks such as competition, retailer acceptance, and cannibalization of existing products. Decision to launch depends on addressing these risks and market potential.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate understanding of new product launch risks, channel strategy, and manufacturer-retailer dynamics.

title: Town Mayor
description: The client has been elected Mayor of a town of 500k people experiencing economic decline and slight population decrease. The Mayor wants a 100-day economic revival plan. What information would you need and what is your plan?
additional_info: Possible causes of decline: poor macro conditions, tax increases, aging population, deteriorating infrastructure, poor conditions in surrounding cities, increasing crime, reduced airline flights
Unemployment currently 8%, goal to increase population by 5% and reduce unemployment to 5%
60% of population eligible to work, average company size 500 employees
State considering building a new university in the city with concerns about town demographics, location, funding, timeframe, and political implications
additional_questions: What could have caused the population/economic decrease?
How many new companies will be needed to meet employment goals?
What could be done to attract ~50 new companies/plants?
Is building a new university a good thing? Should the Mayor support it?
solution: To reduce unemployment and grow population, about 23,250 new jobs are needed, equating to ~50 new companies. Strategies include expanding current companies, infrastructure improvements, tax incentives, lobbying, and marketing. University support depends on demographic fit, funding, and timing.
evaluation: Candidate should develop a comprehensive framework, perform quantitative analysis on employment needs, propose creative economic development strategies, and weigh pros and cons of university investment.

title: APC Nutrition
description: Our client is CEO of American Products Company, a $50bn conglomerate with a $500m subsidiary APC Nutrition. APC Nutrition has moderate growth and sells a meal replacement drink, Ready-Thin. Develop a five-year growth plan, determine growth prospects, and identify issues.
additional_info: Reasons for slowed revenue growth: competitor products, ineffective evidence, market shift, decreased marketing, celebrity scandals, price changes, packaging changes, distribution changes, retailer compensation changes
Price increase scenario: raising price from $8 to $9 per 6-pack with volume impact only in big-box stores (4% volume loss per 1% price increase)
APC Nutrition plans to launch Alpha, a diabetic product at $1.50/day
Market: 20m diabetics in US, 70% diagnosed, 20% of diagnosed treat condition, 3 competitors, 20% market share expected
additional_questions: Why has revenue slowed for Ready-Thin?
If they raise prices to $9, what will revenue be?
Should they raise the price?
Would a company ever choose to see sales volume decrease?
If price raise is decided, is higher or lower per unit margin preferable?
What is the revenue potential for Alpha?
Is this a strong growth area for American Products Company?
solution: Price increase reduces revenue from $400m to $382.5m due to volume loss in big-box stores. Launching Alpha could add $306.6m revenue, a 40% increase for APC Nutrition but insignificant for the $50bn parent. Growth plan should consider competitive response, market trends, and margin impact.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue drivers, perform price elasticity math, evaluate new product potential, and contextualize subsidiary growth within parent company scale.

title: Mini-Bar
description: You are in charge of the W Hotel, which currently has no mini-bars in guest rooms. The previous manager wants to know if the revenue opportunity is large enough to justify installing mini-bars. Estimate the revenue opportunity and recommend whether to install them.
additional_info: Consider city, local area, hotel restaurants
For mini-bars: standard amenity, attracts business travelers, opportunity to sell branded items
Against mini-bars: hotel bar loses business, bartenders lose tips, need to hire staff, adjust catering, high startup and electricity costs
Hotel size assumptions: 20 floors, 15 rooms/floor, ~300 rooms (20 penthouse, 80 king, 200 regular)
Assume 75% occupancy, 350 usable days/year
Average spend per day: penthouse $30, king $15, regular $10
additional_questions: How would you structure your analysis to make a recommendation?
What are some issues for and against adding mini-bars?
Please estimate the revenue opportunity.
Is this amount significant?
solution: Estimated annual revenue opportunity is about $950,000. While relatively low compared to total hotel revenue, mini-bars may be necessary for intangible reasons such as guest satisfaction and brand standards. Profit impact may be limited.
evaluation: Candidate should make reasonable assumptions, estimate revenue logically, identify intangible factors, and assess significance relative to hotel business.

title: Vitamin Universe
description: A private equity investor is considering buying Vitamin Universe, which has 1,000 stores nationally in malls. Analyze the industry, determine whether to purchase Vitamin Universe, and identify issues of concern.
additional_info: Target audience: one-third of young men aged 18-34 who are heavy users spending $50/month
30% of these fitness buffs shop at Vitamin Universe with 50% margins
Population assumptions: 300 million total, divided into age groups
Private equity firm requires a 50% increase in profits
additional_questions: How would you structure your analysis to make a recommendation?
How does increasing brand adoption from 30% to 35% affect profits?
How does this satisfy the PE firm's 50% profit increase requirement?
How could you increase profits?
solution: Current annual profit is about $891 million; increasing adoption to 35% yields a 17% profit increase, insufficient for the 50% target. Profit growth could come from expanding product lines, new channels, operational efficiencies, and financing strategies.
evaluation: Candidate should structure market and internal analysis, perform market sizing and profit impact calculations, and propose realistic profit growth strategies.

title: Bagging Co. (Popcorn)
description: Bagging Co. makes grease-proof paper for popcorn bags. R&D discovered a way to reduce grease soakage by 10x with a simple equipment modification at negligible cost. Should Bagging Co. make the new paper?
additional_info: Production requires large upfront capital expenditure
Bagging Co. sells to bag converters who make popcorn bags and sell to packaged food companies
Value may be to end users (cleaner experience) or bag converters (reduced costs, pricing power)
Bag converters currently use double-ply bags; new paper allows single-ply
Current cost breakdown for bag converters: $0.30 for 2 sheets, $0.10 receptor film, $0.05 popcorn, $0.05 other manufacturing; total $0.50
Client cost per sheet: $0.10 (half fixed, half variable)
Grease soakage decreases 5x using single-ply new paper
New total cost for bag converters: $0.15 to $0.35
Client profit margin without technology is 33% ($0.05 profit per sheet)
Pricing range for new paper: $0.15 to $0.30 per sheet
additional_questions: Where might the value be in this product?
Where would there be more value – end users or bag converters?
How much does grease soakage decrease using single-ply new paper?
What is driving bag converters' desire for this product?
What is client’s profit margin without technology?
How should the client price a sheet of the new grease-resistant paper?
What are other applications/markets for this new technology?
solution: Bag converters benefit from cost reduction by switching to single-ply, enabling competitive pricing and margin maintenance. Client should price new paper between $0.25 and $0.30 per sheet to share benefits. Other markets include grease-resistant food storage, packaging, auto mechanics, and restaurant industry.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze value chain, quantify cost and margin impacts, develop pricing strategy, and identify new market opportunities.

title: Mattel
description: Mattel is experiencing a decline in profitability. They’ve brought you in to find out why and how to fix it.
additional_info: Decline has occurred over past 12 months
There has been a 10% decline in revenue
If specific numbers are requested, show Exhibit 1 (only cost info is in Exhibit 1)
There are five divisions of revenue; read out information from Exhibit 2, but do not show
Assume variable costs are steady
Price is constant on per doll basis ($20 each)
Price has been constant on per board game, early childhood, and educational game basis ($30 each)
A recent documentary released in January 2015 caused negative press implying Barbie dolls discourage girls from pursuing less 'feminine' career paths and reinforce societal criteria
Exhibit 1: 2014 vs 2015 revenue, COGS, SG&A, Operating Income
Exhibit 2: Revenue by Division (Board Games, Early Childhood, Educational, Barbie Dolls, Other) - do not show
solution: The decline in profitability is primarily driven by a 10% revenue decline, especially in the Barbie segment due to quantity decline caused by negative press. Recommendations include launching new product lines like 'Engineer Barbie' and 'Scientist Barbie', new marketing campaigns to change brand image, and public contributions to causes promoting women in the workplace.
evaluation: Candidate should quickly interpret Exhibit 1, organize Exhibit 2 data effectively, focus on revenue decline as primary driver, and brainstorm internal and external reasons for quantity decline. Strong candidates suggest actionable recommendations addressing brand image and product innovation.

title: Regional Electric Utility
description: Our client is a large regional electric utility company that has seen negative earnings trend in the last 12 months. They want to identify the root cause of their loss of profitability and what actions they should take to improve profitability.
additional_info: Revenue has been consistent over the past 12 months; little ability to increase prices due to competitive market
1 million customers, largely unchanged; large customer turnover each year
Deposits required to start service: market max $400, client requires $100
Costs include Cost of Goods Sold (electricity purchased), cost to serve (billing), variable service calls, cost to cut-off service, and debt collection
By law, electricity cannot be cut off until 60 days past due; cutting off costs $75
Collection costs $50 per account with 50% recovery rate
Average period of service is 24 months; 'low' credit customers stop paying after 22 months
Acquisition cost $50, cost to serve $7/month, average electricity usage 1000 KW/month, price $0.10/KW, cost $0.09/KW
Customer credit rating segments: Strong 30%, Medium 20%, Low 20%, Very Low 30%
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) for Low segment is negative (-$103)
Possible profitability improvements: require higher deposits for low credit customers, focus marketing on higher credit customers, make bills easier to pay, automate billing
solution: The root cause of loss is negative CLV in low and very low credit segments due to bad debt and collection costs. Profitability can be improved by increasing deposits for low credit customers, targeting marketing to higher credit customers, improving billing/payment processes, and automating billing to reduce costs.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate CLV accurately, focus on debt collection issues, consider customer segmentation, and brainstorm actionable improvements with associated risks.

title: Grocery Deli Company
description: A large supermarket chain has seen declining profits due to competition in groceries. They want to find ways to improve profitability in their deli department which has had flat profits.
additional_info: Deli department has two divisions: Deli Meats and Prepared Meals
Two new products introduced two years ago in Prepared Meals: chicken wings and prepared sandwiches
Chicken wings require no significant changes in equipment or manpower
Sandwiches are made to order for 2 hours at lunch and 2 hours at dinner
Exhibit 1 provides revenue and COGS for deli meats and prepared foods
Chicken wings generate $80 revenue/day, $40 COGS/day, $40 contribution/day
Sandwiches generate $80 revenue/day, $120 COGS/day, -$40 contribution/day
solution: The sandwiches product line is losing money. Recommendations include stopping sandwich production, understanding sandwich price elasticity and possibly increasing prices, limiting sandwich availability time, or batch preparing sandwiches instead of made-to-order.
evaluation: Candidate should segment revenue and costs by product, identify loss-making products, and propose practical recommendations to improve profitability.

title: Airline Food
description: Our client provides catering services to major airlines at Houston Intercontinental Airport. They are considering investing $10M in new refrigerating equipment that will reduce variable costs by 10%. Determine if they should invest.
additional_info: Current charge is $10 per meal; average COGS $5 per meal
Client requires a 3-year undiscounted payback
Client has 40% market share at HIA; 3 other major competitors
Customers sign 5-year contracts; new customer acquisition limited
Air traffic steady for past 5 years
Airline food is generally a commodity; fresher product may gain slight market share
Market size estimation involves number of runways, departures per hour, hours of operation, average passengers per plane, passenger distribution by flight type, % consuming meals
Example market sizing and profitability calculations provided
Recommendation depends on payback period and market sizing assumptions
solution: Investment decision depends on market sizing and resulting cost savings. If the 3-year payback is met, investment is recommended. Candidate should consider market size assumptions, potential to increase market share, and alternative ways to capitalize on investment such as entering new markets or acquiring competitors.
evaluation: Candidate should structure market sizing clearly, perform cost-benefit analysis, consider assumptions and risks, and provide a reasoned recommendation.

title: Chem Co.
description: Our client, Chem Co., a basic commodity chemical manufacturer with limited market share and capital, recently emerged from bankruptcy. Evaluate the sustainability of their business model and recommend strategic alternatives.
additional_info: Prices are cyclical with 7-year cycles; currently at top of cycle
Chemical X produced 100K tons/year; Chemical Y by-product at 1.5:1 ratio
Prices: X $150/ton falling 33% in 3 years; Y $175/ton falling to $100/ton in 3 years
Variable costs $50/ton combined, rising to $95/ton next year
Fixed costs $20M/year
Client cannot endure net losses
Client will not survive bottom of pricing cycle in 3 years (net loss of $4.5M)
Underlying reasons for inability to survive include deeper pricing trough, lower production volume, increased variable and fixed costs
Strategic options: 1) Acquire/move into counter-cyclical chemicals business; 2) Provide additional products/services relevant to key customers
Risks: difficulty finding counter-cyclical business, acquisition capital constraints, lack of skills for new services, need for quick acquisition
If neither viable, options include selling company, liquidating assets, or shutting down plants to reduce fixed costs
additional_questions: Can the client survive the bottom of the pricing cycle in 3 years?
What could have been the underlying reasons for the client’s inability to survive the trough of the pricing curve a few years ago?

Discuss the opportunity and risks associated with the two following options: 1) Acquire/move into a counter-cyclical chemicals business; 2) Provide additional products/services relevant to a key customer
If neither course of action is viable, what strategic options would you recommend to the client?
solution: Client will not survive the pricing trough due to negative net income. Underlying reasons include deeper price trough, lower volumes, and increased costs. Strategic alternatives include acquiring counter-cyclical business or expanding product/service offerings, both with significant risks. If not viable, recommend selling, liquidating, or shutting down plants to reduce fixed costs.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze profitability, understand cyclical pricing impact, evaluate strategic options with risks, and propose viable alternatives under capital constraints.

title: LaLa Media
description: Our client is a leading film and television studio with an unprofitable in-house postproduction division. The CFO wants to determine strategic options for this division.
additional_info: Postproduction division performs dailies, mastering, and restoration services
Postproduction is often outsourced; client is one of few studios retaining it in-house
Exhibit 1 describes services, customers, critical issues, and trends
Exhibit 2 provides revenue, profit, and margin data by service
Restoration is small but highly profitable; HEM is largest but least profitable
Services vary in revenue per job and profitability
Turnaround options include identifying causes of revenue drop, pursuing new customers, cutting costs
Divest options include valuation methods and buyer considerations
additional_questions: What information and data must be considered in evaluating the studio’s options?
Given Exhibit 2, what can you determine regarding the relative profitability of each service?
What approach would you take to evaluate the post-production services?
What additional factors should be considered in a turnaround decision? A divest decision?
solution: Evaluate profitability by service; focus on growing profitable segments like Restoration; consider outsourcing or divesting unprofitable segments; assess turnaround costs and market opportunities; consider strategic implications of divestiture including buyer impact and operational effects.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze segment profitability, evaluate strategic options with pros and cons, consider operational and market factors, and provide a clear recommendation.

title: Sugar Cereal
description: Foods Inc. sells sugar cereals through grocery stores and discount chain Big M Mart, which has grown rapidly and become their largest distributor. Evaluate the client’s distribution strategy given Big M Mart’s growth.
additional_info: Grocery stores specialize in food; discount stores offer food plus wide merchandise variety
Discount stores advertise lower prices relative to grocery stores
Big M Mart sales growing 15% annually; sales to Big M Mart are lower margin than other stores
Client is losing market share at Big M Mart to Cereal Co. and Private Label
Cereal Co. brands placed lower on shelf, more visible to children
Cereal Co. has promotional tags and advertised specials for Big M Mart customer cardholders
Cereal Co. has 50 sales reps dedicated to Big M Mart; client has 7
Buyers segmented into 60% brand loyal (not price sensitive) and 40% impulse shoppers
Brand loyalists try discounted cereals 35% of time when favorite brand unavailable; 25% leave without purchase
Product availability is a major driver of volume at Big M Mart
solution: Client is losing share at Big M Mart due to lower product availability, less promotional support, and weaker shelf placement compared to competitors. Recommendations include increasing sales force dedicated to Big M Mart, improving product placement, enhancing promotions, and ensuring product availability to capture impulse buyers and protect brand loyalists.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and margin differences by channel, assess competitor advantages, understand customer segments and behavior, and propose targeted distribution and marketing improvements.

title: Sugar Cereal
description: Our client is a sugar cereal manufacturer selling through distributors like Big M Mart. Analyze the supply chain and distribution challenges, especially stock outs, and provide a recommendation to improve sales and reduce lost revenue.
additional_info: Cereals are distributed from the factory to the distribution warehouse twice a month; the retailer then stocks shelves itself.
Our client has no direct access to in-store inventory information.
Big M Mart stores average 15% of sugar cereal brands out of stock, across all brands.
Stock outs hurt Big M Mart, who loses sales 25% of the time when a brand-loyal customer walks away without buying anything.
solution: The candidate should recommend working more closely with Big M Mart, increasing resources dedicated to the store to ensure better product placement. Stock outs for our product hurt overall sales by roughly 0.75%.
evaluation: Candidate should identify the impact of stock outs on sales, understand supply chain limitations, and provide a clear, actionable recommendation focused on collaboration and resource allocation.

title: Travel IT Disruption
description: A private equity firm wants to assess the attractiveness of entering the travel IT industry, specifically the global distribution system (GDS) market. Evaluate whether it makes sense to enter this industry.
additional_info: The industry standard is a global distribution system (GDS) that contains massive amounts of inventory information for flights and hotels worldwide.
Outside China, 80% of the industry is controlled by Amadeus and Sabre; inside China, 80% is controlled by TravelSky.
The PE firm desires EBITDA margins of at least 30%.
There are over 800 passenger airlines and 10,000 hotel chains globally.
Business model: inventory owners pay fees to GDS; GDS pays commissions to travel agents.
North American and European markets are stagnant; Asia-Pacific is growing rapidly.
Airlines and hoteliers dislike paying fees to GDS and prefer direct sales.
Sabre and Amadeus have EBITDA margins of 30-35%.
Sabre and Amadeus spend $300M-$400M annually on CAPEX.
Chinese government heavily regulates the GDS market.
Google owns ITA Matrix but has not entered GDS or inventory selling markets.
solution: The industry is not attractive for PE investment due to entrenched market leaders, government interference in China, high technology complexity with network effects, large initial investments, and malcontent inventory owners.
evaluation: Candidate should structure the industry analysis considering competitors, customers, regulations, technology, and inventory owners. They should recognize barriers to entry and risks.

title: Admedi Co.
description: Our client has acquired Admedi Co., a healthcare software company selling administrative systems to large hospitals. Identify new revenue growth opportunities.
additional_info: Three main markets: administration systems, patient administration, and physician support systems.
Hospitals make up the vast majority of the medical software market.
Industry consolidation with three large hospital networks dominating 45% of the market.
Cost controls and vendor consolidation trends in hospitals.
Software decisions made by hospital IT groups; instrument purchases by medical staff.
Competition varies by segment; physician support is fragmented.
Margins: 25-30% for administration and patient administration; 45-50% for physician support.
Competitors HTI and HCS have expanded into adjacent markets.
Admedi Co.’s marketing is regional; no dedicated sales teams for large networks.
Admedi Co.’s administration product is best in market and modular.
Excellent customer support.
solution: Physician support is attractive but has high entry barriers; patient administration is a more adjacent opportunity. Sales and marketing need improvement. Organic growth may suit patient administration; acquisition may be needed for physician support.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market attractiveness, competition, company capabilities, and growth options (organic vs inorganic). They should identify sales/marketing gaps and competitive threats.

title: S.A. Shipping
description: UPS acquired a small startup in Cape Town that delivers packages daily. Determine how many trucks to lease and which model to choose to meet demand efficiently.
additional_info: 10,000 packages delivered daily; average package size 1 cubic foot.
Operates 350 days a year.
Drivers, fuel, etc. cost $100/day per truck.
Truck 1: $200/day lease, 4x4x5 ft cargo dimensions.
Truck 2: $80/day lease, 8x2x2 ft cargo dimensions.
Truck 3: $400/day lease, double dimensions of Truck 1.
Trucks make one run per day.
Little seasonality in shipments.
additional_questions: What are some potential risks to investigate for this operation?
A year later, how can the company increase profitability without adding new truck leases?
solution: Truck 1 is optimal considering costs and capacity. Risks include driver availability, truck repairs, rental rate changes, demand variability, and safety concerns. Profit growth can come from extending working hours, optimizing routes, adding delivery personnel, offering insurance or premium services, and reducing other costs.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate truck requirements and costs accurately, identify bottlenecks, consider operational risks, and propose creative profit improvement ideas.

title: JCL Carpets
description: JCL Carpets is considering purchasing a $23M yarn printer machine that prints colors directly onto carpets. Evaluate whether this investment is worthwhile.
additional_info: Current process uses colored yarn; new process uses uncolored yarn and inks/dyes carpet after weaving.
Current production: 10M yards/year; costs $10/yard.
Cost savings with new process: 25 cents/yard net after operational shift costs.
Machine lasts 10 years.
Current customers pay $16/yard; new potential customers pay 25% more ($20/yard).
30% of current customers may opt for flexibility to decide color closer to production.
High-end market size: 70M yards/year; can capture 5% quickly.
solution: Initial cost savings alone do not justify the investment. However, targeting new customers willing to pay a premium and entering the high-end market can generate significant incremental revenue and profit, making the investment attractive.
