evaluation: Candidate should perform correct cost-benefit math, consider revenue growth opportunities, segment customers, and provide a clear recommendation.

title: Bombardier CS300
description: Bombardier’s new CS300 aircraft has a projected margin of 5%, below the industry average of 10-15%. Identify ways to increase the product’s margin.
additional_info: Revenue is fixed; price and volume are optimized.
Most expensive parts: fuselage, wings, engines.
Current fuselage costs: Labor $9M, Raw Materials $9M, Fixed Costs $2M.
Outsourcing options: Japan, China, India with varying labor, material costs, tariffs, quality risks, and shipping costs.
Japan is more costly; China and India offer cost savings but have quality and IP risks.
solution: Outsourcing fuselage production to China or India can increase margin from 5% to about 7.6%. Candidate should weigh cost savings against risks like IP theft, quality defects, regulatory changes, and PR backlash.
evaluation: Candidate should creatively identify cost-cutting options, perform cost calculations, and discuss qualitative risks and benefits of outsourcing locations.

title: Polyethylene Resin
description: Our client developed a proprietary polyethylene resin for flexible irrigation pipes. Recommend a pricing strategy for this resin.
additional_info: Client sells resin to pipe extruders, not pipes directly.
Benefits of client’s resin: lower pipe replacement cost, labor savings, less crop damage.
Value-based pricing recommended: price based on value created for customers.
Reference price for substitute resin: $0.28/lb.
20 lbs resin required per 100 ft pipe.
solution: Calculate incremental value to farmers ($3.60 per 100 ft), convert to $0.18 incremental value per pound of resin. Client can charge up to $0.46/lb (substitute price plus incremental value). A recommended price is $0.37/lb, splitting incremental value 50/50 with extruders.
evaluation: Candidate should understand value chain, quantify value created, and apply value-based pricing principles with a clear price recommendation.

title: I-35 IHOP
description: Determine the value of an IHOP restaurant located at I-35 and Cesar Chavez, considering its profitability and potential alternative uses of the property.
additional_info: 30 tables with capacity of 100 people.
Open 24 hours a day, 360 days a year.
Prices: $10 entrée, $4 side, $1.5 drink, $0 water.
Average consumption: 1 entrée, 0.25 sides, 1 revenue drink, 1 water per customer.
Customer volume: 18 off-peak hours at 20 customers/hour, 6 peak hours at 70 customers/hour.
Variable costs: labor, ingredients, royalty fees totaling $6,400/day.
Fixed costs: $1,100/day.
Tax rate: 40%, discount rate: 12%, growth rate: 2%.
Alternative use: build apartments with 180 units, rent $2.5/sq ft/month, occupancy 85%, initial investment $35M, ongoing costs $520K/year.
solution: Calculate restaurant annual net profit (~$540K), value as a going concern (~$5.4M). Calculate apartment building net income (~$9M present value). Recommend selling land if price exceeds restaurant value, considering risks and assumptions.
evaluation: Candidate should perform bottom-up revenue and cost calculations, apply valuation techniques, consider alternative uses, and discuss risks and assumptions.

title: MIMO Technology
description: A start-up has developed a prototype algorithm enabling 8x8 MIMO wireless communication, doubling cost-effectiveness of CAPEX in some cases. Determine the value of this invention and whether to proceed with further R&D.
additional_info: Current 4x4 MIMO uses OFDM to send four data streams simultaneously.
Algorithm is 40% complete; $40M spent; $60M more needed to complete in 1 year.
50% chance to patent before competitors; zero value if not patented or completed.
No impact on OPEX; no consumer performance difference.
No current competitors with 8x8; patent lasts 20 years (assume perpetuity).
Pricing: one-time fee to carriers.
Variable costs: labor $400K/day, 350 days/year.
Customers: 4 major US carriers (AT&T 30%, Verizon 30%, T-Mobile 20%, Sprint 20%).
Savings apply to 20% of CAPEX only.
Carrier CAPEX is a function of revenue; AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile spend 10% revenue on CAPEX; Sprint 5%.
solution: Candidate should estimate total addressable market based on carriers’ CAPEX, calculate expected profits considering R&D costs and probability of patent, and recommend proceeding if NPV is positive.
evaluation: Candidate should structure valuation considering market size, costs, risks, and competition; perform probabilistic valuation; and provide a clear go/no-go recommendation.

title: MIMO Technology
description: Your client is developing a new technology (MIMO algorithm) that can reduce capital expenditures (CAPEX) for US mobile carriers. Estimate the market size and value of this technology, and determine whether the client should continue investing in its development.
additional_info: US population is approximately 320 million; assume even age distribution 0-80; everyone below 10 and above 75 do not have cell phones.
Discount bottom income decile (10%) as not having phones; discount another 10% who do not want cell phones; assume 30% of cell users have 2 phones; add 10% for other connected devices.
Exhibit 1 shows number of US mobile lines by carrier from 2012 to 2016+ (includes all connected devices).
Exhibit 2 shows monthly ARPU by carrier: AT&T and Verizon $60, T-Mobile and Sprint $40.
CAPEX is 10% of total revenue for AT&T, T-Mobile, Verizon; 5% for Sprint. The technology can be applied to 20% of CAPEX, with a 50% reduction rate.
Discount rate for valuation is 20% in perpetuity.
Potential labor costs are $70M/year; initial investment costs are $60M (incremental) plus $40M sunk.
Carriers will want a portion of the value generated; price the product at 20% of the algorithm’s economic value.
additional_questions: What information can you determine from Exhibit 1?
What additional information would you like to know to size the opportunity?
Calculate the CAPEX for each carrier and the potential annual CAPEX reduction.
Calculate the valuation of the technology using a 20% discount rate.
Provide a recommendation based on your analysis.
solution: The total US mobile market size is approximately $206B in revenue with $19B CAPEX. The technology can reduce CAPEX by $1.9B annually, with a 50% collectable revenue of $950M. After accounting for labor costs and a 50% probability of success, the expected gross profit is $440M/year. Using a 20% discount rate, the DCF valuation is $2.2B, minus $60M incremental investment, yielding a maximum NPV of $2.14B. The recommendation is to proceed with development and sales, as the expected return justifies the investment despite risks and uncertainties.
evaluation: Evaluate if the candidate: 1) structured the market sizing logically, 2) asked relevant clarifying questions, 3) correctly interpreted exhibits, 4) accurately calculated CAPEX savings and valuation, 5) considered investment costs and risks, 6) provided a clear, hypothesis-driven recommendation with supporting rationale and next steps.

title: Fast Food Loyalty
description: Your client is a national fast food chain considering instituting a frequent diner program to increase revenues per store. Customers earn 1 point per dollar spent and receive a free movie ticket after 20 points. Evaluate whether the client should proceed with this strategy.
additional_info: Each store has a 50% profit margin.

There are 30,000 customers divided into two segments: frequent visitors (10,000 customers, 30 visits/year, $4 spend/visit, 20% join rate, 5 additional visits/year) and occasional visitors (20,000 customers, 10 visits/year, $4 spend/visit, 5% join rate, 2 additional visits/year).
Client has 500 stores.
Program setup costs $10,000.
Each movie ticket costs $1 upon redemption.
Program members redeem 50% of their tickets.
solution: The program generates additional revenue of $48,000 from new visits, with an additional profit of $24,000 after applying the 50% margin. Redemption costs are $8,200 and setup costs $10,000, resulting in a net profit of $5,800 in the first year and $15,800 thereafter. The program is profitable and should be considered for implementation.
evaluation: Assess if the candidate: 1) identified key revenue and cost drivers, 2) segmented customers appropriately, 3) calculated incremental visits and revenues correctly, 4) accounted for costs including ticket redemption and setup, 5) concluded with a clear recommendation based on profitability analysis.

title: Styrofoam Situation
description: Cup Co. is facing competition from Chalice Inc., which is selling at a loss to gain market share. How should Cup Co. respond to this competitive threat?
additional_info: Cup Co’s net margin is 30%, Chalice Inc. is running at a loss.
Cup Co’s profit will be eroded if they try to match Chalice Inc’s price.
Customers value service reliability most, and Cup Co ranks higher than Chalice Inc.
Importing from China may impact Chalice Inc’s service reliability.
Possible strategies include lowering prices slightly, negotiating contracts, improving design, and improving service reliability.
solution: Cup Co is better positioned than Chalice Inc. Price is not the main customer pain point, so Cup Co should not lower its price. Instead, it should defend and improve its high service reliability rating. Risks include price becoming a pain point if the economy worsens. Next steps include exploring ways to improve service reliability such as optimizing delivery routes.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate profitability, identify customer priorities, brainstorm offensive and defensive strategies, justify recommendations, and consider risks and next steps.

title: North-South Pharma Co.
description: North-South Pharma Co. owns a US plant serving US customers and an idle plant in South America. Forecast the revenue for North-South Pharma Co. and advise what to do with the idle South American facility.
additional_info: US population: 300M; 10% visit doctor; 20 refills per prescription; $0.50 per prescription; 5% market share.
South American facility has lower fixed and variable costs but higher transportation costs to serve US market.
US facility is profitable; South American facility is breaking even.
Options for South American facility include selling, repurposing, subletting, entering South American market, or shutting down.
Entering South American market involves internal challenges (hiring, training, distribution) and external challenges (market size, regulations, geopolitical risks).
additional_questions: What else can North-South Pharma Co do with the South American facility?
What are the implications of keeping the facility and entering the South American pharmaceutical market?
solution: Multiple correct answers: either sell or shut down the South American facility to cut losses or maintain it and enter the South American pharmaceutical market. Each option has risks and next steps including legal, market research, and human capital considerations.
evaluation: Candidate should independently size the market, analyze profitability, brainstorm creative options for the idle facility, and consider implications of entering a new market.

title: Fire Proof Inc.
description: Fire Proof Inc., a leading manufacturer of fire resistant apparel for government fire departments, wants to diversify its revenue and product line by expanding into equipment for other industries. How would you advise Fire Proof Inc.?
additional_info: Fire Proof has $500M annual sales, growing 4% yearly, with 3 main competitors.
Competitors have diversified revenue streams; Fire Proof has focused on fire equipment.
Internal capabilities to consider: manufacturing capacity, supply chain, industry knowledge, financial resources.
Product similarities: raw material overlap, customer overlap, production overlap.
Market attractiveness: size, growth, number of competitors, barriers to entry.
Financials: investment required, acquisition vs internal build, profit margins, break-even timing.
Exhibit 1 shows police equipment market sizes, growth rates, competitors, and production overlap.
additional_questions: Which product market is most and least attractive for Fire Proof to enter? Why?
What is Fire Proof’s projected revenue for bullet proof vests in year 3?
Will Fire Proof meet its target ROI of 15% in 3 years given a $100M investment?
What other markets and/or products can Fire Proof consider to diversify?
solution: The most attractive market is bullet proof vests due to high production overlap, large size, growth, and fragmented competitors. Projected year 3 revenue is about $240M. ROI is about 13%, slightly below target but may still be acceptable. Fire Proof should consider diversification into bullet proof vests and explore other markets such as medical accessories, construction, and recreational gear.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market attractiveness, perform revenue and ROI calculations, brainstorm diversification options, and provide a reasoned recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: High Quality Bottles
description: High Quality Bottles (HQB), a small plastics manufacturing company, wants to expand into supplying bottled toiletries (shampoos, conditioners, body wash, lotions) to local hotel chains. How would you approach finding the market size for the first year in this expansion?
additional_info: Medium-sized city with about 50 hotels, each with 120 rooms.
80% occupancy rate, average 3 stays per week per room.
Each hotel room requires 4 bottles (one each of shampoo, conditioner, body wash, lotion).
Average guest uses 2.5 bottles per stay.
Price per bottle is $0.50.
Cost data: ingredients $0.20/bottle, bottle $0.05/bottle, distribution $1.00 per case of 20 bottles, fixed labor $100k/year, capex $250k, depreciation over 10 years.
additional_questions: What is the market size of supplying hotel toiletries in this region in dollars in year 1?
What profit margin can HQB expect in the first year if it captures 100% of the market?
Considering HQB’s average profit margin on beverage operations is 25%, is this new business opportunity attractive?
What other information should the client know to determine whether this is an attractive business to enter?
solution: Market size is approximately $912k to $948k in year 1. Expected profit margin is about 25-30%, similar or slightly better than HQB’s beverage business. The opportunity is attractive but risks include raw material cost fluctuations and single region exposure. Next steps include exploring scale expansion and hedging input costs.
evaluation: Candidate should build a framework for market sizing, calculate initial and replenishment orders, analyze profitability with cost data, and assess business attractiveness with risks and recommendations.

title: Canyon Capital Partners
description: Canyon Capital Partners (CCP), a hedge fund managing $2.1B in assets, has seen declining profits over the past two years. Help the CEO understand why profits are down and recommend actions to restore profitability.
additional_info: Hedge funds earn management fees (percentage of assets under management) and carried interests (share of investment gains above hurdle rate).
Management fees reduced from 2.0% to 1.5% since 2016 due to competition.
Assets under management have increased steadily.
Investment performance was strong in 2015 but poor in 2016 and 2017.
Employee headcount increased, especially associates, while analysts decreased.
Salaries vary by role; only C-suite and portfolio managers receive variable compensation tied to profitability.
Office rent and research costs have been stable.
solution: Profits declined due to lower management fees and poor investment performance reducing carried interest revenue. Costs increased due to rising headcount, especially associates. Recommendations include increasing management fees, growing assets under management, improving investment strategies, and optimizing headcount by reducing associates and increasing analysts. Risks include investor pushback and morale issues.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost trends, understand fee structures, identify cost drivers, and provide actionable recommendations with risks and next steps.

title: Transportation Tech Co.
description: Transportation Tech Co. developed a GPS fleet tracking device for K-12 school buses. Should the client roll out this product in the US school bus market to maximize profit?
additional_info: Product transmits location and ETA info to schools, parents, and bus drivers via an app.
Revenue model includes initial device sales and technology service plans; focus here is on device sales.
Product life span is 5 years.
Purchasing decisions involve multiple stakeholders: district-level, school-level, bus drivers.
Market sizing: US has 60M school-aged children; 50% ride buses; average bus seats 50 students; 2 units per bus; $200 per unit.
additional_questions: What is the potential addressable year 1 market size for this device in the US school bus space?
What do you think the year 1 new device sales would be?
What would you expect annual revenues in year 5 to be?
solution: Year 1 market size is approximately $240M (600K buses * 2 units * $200). Year 1 sales depend on adoption rate; 10% adoption is reasonable given budget constraints. Year 5 revenues depend on market growth and adoption but no specific data provided.
evaluation: Candidate should build a market sizing framework, estimate market size, justify adoption assumptions, and reason about revenue projections.

title: Transportation Tech Co.
description: Looking at the following data, which additional outlets do you think appear attractive? Why?
additional_info: Commercial freighters and cruise liners are the two largest markets and lack dominant competitors (highly fragmented).

High growth rate in the commercial drone segment is not compelling enough to merit entry as annual segment sales would only reach $120M in 5 years (using the rule of 72, sales wouldn’t double for 7.2 years), still making it the smallest segment.

Thus, commercial freighters and cruise liners would be the two most attractive additional segments from the exhibit. This does not necessarily mean, however, that the client should enter these markets.
additional_questions: How should the client proceed?
Make a recommendation about whether the client should enter the school bus segment and why.
Address any other segments that might be attractive for the client.
What are the risks?
What are the next steps?
solution: The client should consider entering the commercial freighters or cruise liners segments as they are the largest and least dominated by competitors. However, the client should weigh risks and strategic fit before entering. The school bus segment is less attractive due to market size and competition.
evaluation: Framework, logical approach, MECE, creativity, quantitative ability, accuracy, speed, analytical approach, business acumen, insightfulness, implementability, business judgment, communication clarity, and coachability.

title: Lonely Gas Station
description: Our client is the owner of a gas station between towns A and B – 10 miles to each town. He is wondering if it would make sense to add a convenience store to the gas station.
additional_info: There are no other gas stations in town A or B.
Gas is 75% of revenue (10% profit margin) and the gas station also offers car washes (25% of revenue, 20% profit margin).
Criteria for “making sense” – 1) making profit, 2) having a better chance to hold off new competitors enter the market, 3) diversifying income.
The gas stations current customers are residents of town A and B; there are no other customers.
Market info: 1000 people in each town, 80% own cars, 50% of car owners get gas from client.
Customers get gas on average 1x/week, spend $50 each time, make 40% of all gas purchases at client’s station.
Convenience store info: Gasoline customers will spend an additional $20 at the convenience store per purchase, but will not increase frequency of purchases.
Recurring costs for convenience store: Labor $75K/year, Utilities $5K/month, COGS 50% of revenue.
50% of town population who currently are not customers (1200 non-customers) will spend $5 per week at the convenience store.
Up-front investment cost: $1M.
Client’s Discount rate: 10%.
Town population growth rate: 0%.
Expected life: perpetuity.
additional_questions: How profitable is the current business?
How profitable would the convenience store be on an ongoing basis?
What is the NPV of opening a gas station?
What does the NPV of 0 mean for our client?
What happens to the NPV if a convenience store competitor enters the market?
What are the things to consider in this decision?
solution: The current gas station business makes about $100K profit per year. Adding a convenience store would generate about $100K profit per year as well, but with a $1M upfront investment, the NPV is zero at a 10% discount rate. The client should weigh diversification benefits and competitive risks before deciding. Entry of a competitor would reduce attractiveness.
evaluation: Framework, logical approach, MECE, creativity, quantitative ability, accuracy, speed, analytical approach, business acumen, insightfulness, implementability, business judgment, communication clarity, and coachability.

title: Copier Co.
description: Copier, Co. is a national distributor of office equipment, particularly commercial-grade copiers. The firm sells the equipment directly to companies for use in their offices, as well as provides regular service including maintenance, repairs, and replacing disposable parts. Recently, the company has experienced declining profit margins; we have been hired to determine why this is happening and what to do to reverse the trend.
additional_info: Sales Process: The company bids on contracts; contracts are typically awarded to the lowest bidder. The bid consists of a per copier cost which includes 1 year of maintenance as part of sales price.
Salesmen have free reign to determine bid price and are compensated based on commission (% of overall sales).
Competition: One major competitor (American Copiers), and a new low-cost competitor (Bottom Dollar Copier) recently entered the market.
These three companies are distributors and service technicians, NOT manufacturers.
Product: One identical copier sold/distributed by multiple firms.
Profit info: Revenue depends on volume and price; costs include fixed costs (sales force base, warehousing, overhead) and variable costs (service/labor, COGS, transportation, sales force commission).
Exhibit 1 shows a negative correlation between contract size and bid price.
Exhibit 2 shows average bid prices and costs when competing against different competitors.
Contribution and profit per copier vary significantly depending on competitors involved.
additional_questions: What is causing the decline in profit margins?
Calculate profit for bids against American Copier only, Bottom Dollar Copier only, and both competitors.
Brainstorm solutions to the low bid issue.
What is your analysis and recommendation to the CEO?
solution: The new low-cost competitor and the commission-based compensation structure are driving salespeople to lower bid prices, resulting in lower margins and unprofitable contracts when competing against all three competitors. Solutions include setting minimum bid prices, changing compensation structure, offering different packages, negotiating better pricing, offering complementary products, or joint ventures.
evaluation: Framework, logical approach, MECE, creativity, quantitative ability, accuracy, speed, analytical approach, business acumen, insightfulness, implementability, business judgment, communication clarity, and coachability.

title: Maxicure
description: Your client, Maxicure, manufactures and sells an over-the-counter cough and cold medicine. Their sole plant in Kentucky is aging, and its increasing maintenance costs are leading to low margins on their products. How would you advise Maxicure proceed to solve this problem?
additional_info: There are 2-3 larger players in this over-the-counter business who have distribution across the country. Maxicure is one of them.
Maxicure sells all of its products in the US.
Objective is to reduce production costs while maintaining product quality (cost, quality and brand image all matter to customers).
Options include refurbish, rebuild same site, rebuild new location, outsource.
Data for analysis: Selling price per bottle $4.50; In-house initial investment $50M; total cost per bottle $2.00; Outsource cost per bottle $2.25 for first 20M bottles, $2.50 thereafter.
Maxicure needs to sell 110M bottles for in-house option to be more profitable than outsourcing.
Client is considering building a new plant in Indiana closer to a major distribution center and wants to convince the governor to offer tax breaks.
additional_questions: What options does Maxicure have to tackle the problem and what factors would you consider?
How many bottles would Maxicure need to sell for in-house to be more profitable than outsourcing?
How should Maxicure convince the governor of Indiana to offer tax breaks?
What is your final recommendation for the client?
solution: Maxicure should carefully evaluate the trade-offs between building a new facility and outsourcing. The break-even volume is 110M bottles. Building a new plant near distribution centers could reduce costs and improve logistics. Convincing the governor involves highlighting job creation, economic stimulation, and community benefits. Final recommendation depends on volume projections and strategic fit.
evaluation: Framework, logical approach, MECE, creativity, quantitative ability, accuracy, speed, analytical approach, business acumen, insightfulness, implementability, business judgment, communication clarity, and coachability.

title: To Automate or Not
description: After returning from a trade show, the CEO of a large grocery distribution center calls you. He enthusiastically describes a new technology which could be used to automate part of his company’s process. He asks whether you think this would be a good idea for his business. Knowing that this CEO is a tech-enthusiast who loves innovation for the novelty of it, you ponder the implications. How would you tackle this problem?
additional_info: The company does not have a specific goal in mind with this decision. This CEO trusts us and will do whatever we advise.
Shipments are made to roughly 50 grocery stores in the immediate area, and the company does 1M shipments per year.
Costs to automate: one-time outlay of $4M, plus recurring overhead, training, and additional maintenance costs of $1M per year.
The distribution process has three phases: Receiving, Holding & Picking, Shipping.
Exhibit 1 shows current hours per week: Receiving 2,000 hrs, Holding & Picking 10,000 hrs, Shipping 3,000 hrs.
Employee pay per hour: Receiving $12/hr, Holding & Picking $17/hr, Shipping $14/hr.
Exhibit 2 shows hours after automation: Receiving 2,000 hrs, Holding & Picking 5,250 hrs, Shipping 6,000 hrs.
Annual labor cost savings from automation is approximately $2M.
Break-even period for automation investment is about 3.9 years.
additional_questions: Visualize the distribution process and identify costs.
Calculate total labor costs before and after automation.
Calculate break-even period for the investment.
Would you recommend automating? Why or why not?
solution: Automation reduces labor hours and costs, saving about $2M annually against $1M in incremental costs plus a $4M upfront investment. The break-even period is about 3.9 years. Recommendation depends on CEO’s risk tolerance, strategic priorities, and financial considerations.
evaluation: Framework, logical approach, MECE, creativity, quantitative ability, accuracy, speed, analytical approach, business acumen, insightfulness, implementability, business judgment, communication clarity, and coachability.

title: Rubber Bumper
description: Rubber Bumper Co is a small family owned producer of rubber products. It prides itself on producing a limited range of products but producing the highest quality on the market. The company has recently appointed a new President who noticed decreasing profits over the last couple of years. The company only sells two products: rubber bands and condoms. Rubber Bumper has hired our firm to fix the decline in profits. What are all of the areas that need to be examined in order to identify any major issues that should be a priority?
additional_info: Rubber band market is flat; condom market is growing strongly in the US.
Rubber Bumper is losing market share in rubber bands; condom market is fragmented with smaller players gaining share.
Rubber Bumper has two factories, each producing one product, run as separate P&Ls.
Rubber band factory sells boxes of 500 rubber bands for $20; 1 pound of rubber makes 125 rubber bands.
Rubber band factory overhead is $4MM annually; cost to turn rubber into bands is $1 per pound.
Condom factory sells 4 packs for $3; overhead is $2MM annually; cost per condom is $0.10.
Rubber band factory uses 2MM lbs of rubber annually; condom factory sells 10MM condoms annually.
The bigger rubber band factory can be refurbished to produce condoms, doubling capacity but requiring $2MM investment and 1 year downtime.
Payback period for projects is 4 years.
Risks include market demand assumptions, political risks, product diversification, advertising needs, legal risks, and employee acceptance.
additional_questions: Analyze product mix and market trends.
Compare financial profitability of each factory.
Evaluate switching production from rubber bands to condoms in the bigger plant.
What are the risks involved with this project?
Summarize your findings and recommendation.
solution: Rubber band market is stagnant while condom market is growing. Financial analysis shows condom factory has higher margin. Switching the bigger plant to condom production would increase profits and pay back investment in about 3 years, within the 4-year payback requirement. Risks include market demand, political environment, product diversification, and employee concerns. Recommendation is to convert the plant with mitigation plans for risks.
evaluation: Framework, logical approach, MECE, creativity, quantitative ability, accuracy, speed, analytical approach, business acumen, insightfulness, implementability, business judgment, communication clarity, and coachability.

title: Sticky Surfactants
description: Your client, CavalierChem, is a global chemicals manufacturer. CavalierChem recently acquired a manufacturing facility that makes surfactants as part of a larger purchase of competitor assets. Surfactants are a specialty chemical used for a variety of purposes, including laundry detergent, and the client has very little prior experience with this type of product. The manufacturing facility is not currently generating profits, and the client wants your help in determining what to do.
additional_info: The client wants to make the highest return from this facility as possible in the next 5 years.

80% of CavalierChem’s revenues come from the sale of commodity plastics to other manufacturers. The other 20% comes from a wide mix of products that are either downstream or byproducts of their core business.

The manufacturing facility in question was part of a bundled acquisition of other manufacturing assets that are of strategic importance to CavalierChem. CavalierChem now wants to evaluate the surfactant factory on its own.
The market for this particular surfactant is $300M annually. CavalierChem and one other competitor are the only significant manufacturers.
Costs between the client and competition are identical, but the competition experiences 4x profits.
CavalierChem sells 1.4 million tons per year (2000 lbs/ton if asked).
75% of sales are done on contract, the other 25% are sold on the spot market.
Average CavalierChem contract price is 5.67 cents/lb.
Average Competitor contract price is 7.67 cents/lb.
Average spot price (for both CavalierChem and competition) is 5 cents/lb.
Repurpose option: 50 million CapEx, 75 million in incremental annual profits, start-up in two years.
Divest option: Highest bidder willing to pay 200 million.
solution: Recommendation: CavalierChem should renegotiate contract prices to match the competition at 7.67 cents/lb. Annual profits will grow by $42 million. Risks include some customers may not be able to afford higher prices and the market may contract. Next steps include looking into customers' cost structure and soliciting alternative bids for asset sale. The NPVs with 10% discount rate for renegotiation, repurpose, and divest options are roughly equivalent.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate structured brainstorming, identify multiple options (renegotiate pricing, repurpose, divest), use quantitative analysis to calculate incremental profits, and provide a clear recommendation with risks and next steps. Creativity, MECE framework, and ability to interpret exhibits are key.

title: NFL in Mexico
description: Your client is a wealthy former founder and CEO of a multi-national company interested in a new investment opportunity. The National Football League (NFL) wants to expand into Mexico by establishing an expansion franchise in Mexico City. This will be the NFL's first internationally based franchise, although the NFL has recently featured some games between American-based teams in Mexico City and London. The NFL is seeking owners for the team. Our client has sought our advice on whether they should pursue ownership.
additional_info: The client wants a positive NPV on owning and operating the team.
Teams make money through a league-wide share of TV-contract revenues and a mix of team-specific revenue streams such as advertising, tickets, etc.

The client founded a telecoms company known for innovation, including technology boosting mobile internet connections in high-density places. The client is no longer involved in day-to-day operations but retains a strong relationship with the company.
The client has no experience with sports franchises.
additional_questions: What are the initial capital expenditures (team purchase, stadium construction, etc.)?
What are the expected annual revenues (tickets, advertising, etc.)?
What are the expected annual expenses (player salaries, back-office, etc.)?
What is the Mexican market like for football, including substitutes such as soccer and TV/movies?
What is the government relations and support situation (public funding, mayoral support, permitting, security, infrastructure)?
What is the willingness to pay and viewing experience preferences (in-stadium, TV, mobile)?
What are the ease of doing business factors in Mexico?
solution: The candidate should analyze the NPV of owning and operating the team by gathering data on capital expenditures, revenues, and expenses. They should evaluate market demand, competitive substitutes, government support, and operational risks. The recommendation should be based on whether the investment yields a positive NPV considering these factors.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure a market entry and investment case, perform financial analysis (NPV), assess market attractiveness and risks, and communicate a clear recommendation. Understanding of sports franchise economics and international market challenges is important.

title: A Golden Ticket?
description: Should Whizzy Wilco outsource their logistics functions? If so, which bid for 3PL services should Whizzy Wilco accept?
additional_info: Whizzy Wilco's logistics costs are $800M for warehousing, $1.5B for order fulfillment, and $200M for inventory management.

Three bids received: Daintree ($550M warehousing, $1.1B order fulfillment, $350M inventory management software), Original Logistics ($600M warehousing, $1.2B order fulfillment, no inventory management software), The Long Haul ($1B order fulfillment only).
Any services not offered by 3PL must still be performed by Whizzy Wilco.
All three 3PL bids total $2B, which is a 20% savings from current $2.5B logistics costs.
Client projected $300M operating losses in 2021; outsourcing could save $500M and return to profitability.
solution: Whizzy Wilco should outsource logistics functions to save $500M and return to profitability. Since all bids total $2B, any bid is acceptable, but a strong candidate should consider non-cost factors such as vendor expertise and risks. Recommendation is to enter negotiations and start transition.
evaluation: Candidate should organize data clearly, recognize cost savings, consider qualitative factors beyond cost, tie recommendation to client goals, and provide a clear, actionable recommendation.

title: MOOC Madness
description: Our client, Greg Wilkinson, Dean of a mid-sized university business school, is experiencing declining application rates. Determine reasons for the decline and how to bolster converted admissions.
additional_info: Application rate = number of applicants; converted admissions = number of admitted students who accept.
University revenue mainly from tuition and endowments; fixed operating costs are high.
Industry trends include growth in online education and MOOCs.
Current number of applications: 50,000; acceptance rate: 20%; conversion rate: 3%.
University policy requires payback period of 4 years for large expenditures.
Experiential education program costs $3.4M, expected to increase converted admissions by 2%, average tuition $40K, 5% tuition available for new program funding.
additional_questions: What is the overall trend in secondary education? What category is most threatening?
Should Greg move forward with the experiential education program given the payback period requirement?
solution: The secondary education market is growing, but 4-year residential universities are losing market share to online and MOOC options. Greg should pursue an experiential education program to add real-world value and combat online competition. The program meets the 4-year payback period requirement based on increased tuition revenue from higher conversion rates.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze internal and external factors, interpret market trends, perform quantitative payback calculations, and provide a clear recommendation with risks and next steps.

title: Sourcing the Sauce
description: Our client, Hot Indian, a fast-food chain, needs to decide how to source their chutney sauce given capacity constraints at their Minneapolis plant and plans to open 500 new restaurants.
additional_info: Current plant capacity will be exceeded in 2 years.
Options include creating sauce in restaurants, expanding current plant, building new facility in Minneapolis, or using third-party suppliers.
Cost and capacity details provided for each option.
Operating income is $600M (20% margin) on $3B revenue.
Each restaurant uses ~1,000 units of sauce annually.
Additional consideration: client is considering adding a new menu item (lassi).
additional_questions: Which sourcing option is best for Hot Indian?
What should Hot Indian consider before adding a new menu item (lassi)?
solution: Hot Indian should build a new facility in Minneapolis to achieve the lowest cost per unit and supplement with third-party suppliers during the build. This balances cost, quality, capacity, and timeline. For the new menu item, considerations include cost, customer acceptance, operational impact, and potential sales uplift.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze cost structures, capacity constraints, timelines, and quality considerations. They should provide a holistic recommendation considering financial and operational factors.

title: Formula for Success
description: Our client, a high-end automotive brand, is considering entering Formula 1 racing either as a constructor (building a team) or as a sponsor. Determine the viability and recommend the best option.
additional_info: F1 has 10 teams, 20 races per season.
Revenue sources: F1 payouts, race winnings, advertising.
Constructor receives half of $1B F1 revenue split evenly, all race winnings, and $130M advertising revenue.
Sponsor receives no F1 revenue split, race winnings only for first 3 races, and 20% of constructor advertising revenue.
Costs include CAPEX, R&D, production, fuel, tires, maintenance, driver contracts.
Sponsor costs are percentages of constructor costs, no CAPEX or fuel costs.
Discount rate is 10%, assume perpetuity.
additional_questions: What are the potential sources of revenue?
Analyze cost structure and calculate NPV for both options.
What qualitative factors should be considered?
solution: Entering as a constructor yields a higher NPV ($75M) than sponsoring ($10M). The client should enter as a constructor if they have the cash and capability. Risks include changes in F1 (e.g., shift to electric vehicles) and competitiveness. Next steps include competitive landscape analysis and driver availability.
evaluation: Candidate should identify revenue streams, perform detailed cost and NPV calculations, and consider strategic qualitative factors. Clear recommendation with supporting data and risk assessment is expected.

title: Seven Flags
description: Our client, a mid-size amusement park chain, is considering offering a separate ticket for only the animal experience section of their Richmond, VA park. Should they proceed?
additional_info: Current ticket price is $20 for full park access.
Park attendance is about 1 million visitors per year.
Cannibalization rate of animal-only ticket estimated at 50%.
Park is open 350 days per year.
Fixed costs for new entrance, wall, and parking estimated at $2 million.
Profit margin is 20%.
Price elasticity data provided for animal-only ticket prices.
additional_questions: What price maximizes revenue for the animal-only ticket?
What is the payback period of this proposal?
What other considerations factor into the decision?
solution: Revenue is maximized at $16 per animal-only ticket, yielding ~$1.05M incremental revenue and a payback period of ~9.5 years, just below the 10-year target. Risks include sensitivity to cannibalization and opportunity cost. Recommendation is to proceed with the new ticket, while exploring additional revenue from merchandise and food sales.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze pricing and elasticity, calculate payback period, consider cannibalization impact, and brainstorm additional revenue and cost factors. Clear, data-driven recommendation is expected.

title: Maxicure
description: Maxicure manufactures over-the-counter cough and cold medicine with an aging plant causing low margins. How should Maxicure proceed to reduce production costs while maintaining quality?
additional_info: Maxicure sells only in the US; 2-3 major competitors.
Options include refurbishing, rebuilding at same or new site, or outsourcing.
Two narrowed options: build new facility next to old plant or outsource manufacturing.
Selling price per bottle: $4.50.
In-house: $50M initial investment, $2.00 cost per bottle.
Outsource: $2.25 per bottle for first 20M bottles, $2.50 thereafter.
additional_questions: What options does Maxicure have and what factors to consider?
How many bottles must be sold for in-house to be more profitable than outsourcing?
How to convince Indiana governor to offer tax breaks for building plant there?
What is your final recommendation?
solution: Maxicure needs to sell at least 110M bottles for in-house production to be more profitable than outsourcing. Building a new plant in Indiana near distribution centers is recommended if tax breaks can be secured. The client should present economic benefits to the state to obtain incentives. Final recommendation includes risks and next steps.
evaluation: Candidate should explore options comprehensively, perform break-even analysis, consider strategic location benefits, and provide a clear, data-driven recommendation with risks and implementation steps.

title: Shisha: Just Blowing Smoke?
description: The Saudi government wants to forecast potential revenue impact of taxing shisha consumption as part of economic diversification efforts. How should they approach this?
additional_info: Shisha is flavored tobacco smoked via waterpipes, culturally significant in the Middle East.
Saudi government earns revenue via licenses, sales tax, and import tariffs.
Current tobacco-related revenue is 500 million SAR annually; total government revenue is 2.5 trillion SAR.
Public consumption of shisha is banned but widely consumed at home.
Government aims to maximize revenue over 3 years.
Islamic social norms influence consumption and regulation.
solution: The government should analyze market size, consumption patterns, licensing fees, tax rates, and social norms to forecast revenue. They should consider potential changes in consumption due to taxation and regulation, and balance revenue goals with social acceptability.
evaluation: Candidate should structure analysis considering market sizing, regulatory environment, cultural factors, and revenue levers. Clear, logical approach with sensitivity to social norms is expected.

title: Digging for Gold
description: Our client is an Australian mining company, whose main product is Gold, which it sells exclusively to China. This company is the largest producer in volume in the Chinese market with 200 tons sold each year. It is also the lowest cost producer at $1000 per ounce of production costs. We estimate the total Chinese demand for Gold today to be around 1500 tons per year. Our client has won a concession to mine a new site adjacent to its biggest mine, and increases production to 300 tons per year (i.e. 100 additional tons per year). Is this worth doing?
additional_info: Board typically approves projects with payback in less than 5 years. You can use payback with no discounting for your math.
The mine is adjacent to the original mine.
The market will remain flat at 1500 tons per year for the foreseeable future.
Upfront investment required for this project is $750M.
Cost of this new volume of production is the same, $1000/oz.
Competitive landscape includes other producers with higher costs; Exhibit 1 shows China's Gold Cost Curve including royalties and freight.
Increasing capacity pushes out the supply curve lowering the sales price per ton.
In commodity market, the price is set at the cost of the last ton demanded in the market. Adding 100 more tons of supply moves price from $1200 to $1150 per ounce.
Conversion: 15 oz/lb, 2000 lb/ton.
additional_questions: If you were competitor B, how would you react?
solution: The expansion is worth doing. Incremental profit from increased production is $150M per year, leading to a 5-year payback on the $750M investment. The price per ounce will drop from $1200 to $1150 due to increased supply, but the volume increase compensates. Competitor B may lose margin but would need to increase capacity by 75% to push prices lower, so their reaction is limited. Recommend client expand and consider labor force strategy to address skilled labor shortage.
evaluation: Candidate should clarify assumptions, structure analysis around costs, revenues, investment, and market dynamics. Quantitative accuracy in payback calculation is critical. Candidate should consider competitor reactions and operational risks. Creativity and business judgment in recommendation and next steps are evaluated.

title: Canyon Capital Partners
description: Your client is Canyon Capital Partners (CCP), a long-established hedge fund headquartered in Hartford, CT. Hedge funds make money mainly out of management fees and carried interests. Over the past two years, CCP’s profits have been declining. Its CEO and founder has hired you to help her understand why profits are trending down and what should she do to restore the firm to a more profitable route.
additional_info: CCP manages $2.1 billion in assets under management (AuM) with no new inflows or outflows in the past three years.
Management fees were 2.0% per year but reduced to 1.5% since 2016 due to competitive pressure.
Carried interest is 20% of all investment gains in excess of a 15% hurdle rate.
Exhibit 1 shows AuM and investment performance from 2015 to 2017.
Exhibit 2 shows employee headcount by role from 2015 to 2017.
Costs include employee compensation, office rent, research materials, external regulation, competition, and market trends.
Salaries: C-Suite $1.5M, Portfolio Managers $500k, Associates $250k, Analysts $100k, Traders $200k, Support Staff $75k.
additional_questions: What are the trends in revenues and costs?
What are the drivers behind declining profits?
What recommendations would you make to improve profitability?
solution: Profits declined due to lower management fees and poor investment performance reducing carried interest revenues. Costs increased due to rising headcount, especially associates, while analysts decreased. Recommendations include increasing management fees cautiously, improving investment performance, reducing associate headcount while increasing analysts, and promoting merit-based turnover. Risks include investor pushback on fees and employee morale issues.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze revenue and cost drivers quantitatively, identify key issues in fee structure and investment returns, and propose actionable recommendations. Ability to interpret exhibits and synthesize insights is critical. Business judgment and understanding of hedge fund economics are evaluated.

title: PharmaCo
description: PharmaCo is a pharmaceutical company with $10 billion in annual revenue. Its corporate HQ and primary R&D centers are in Switzerland, with regional sales offices worldwide. PharmaCo is interested in entering a new, rapidly growing segment of drugs called “biologicals.” To gain the R&D capabilities requisite for biologicals, PharmaCo is considering acquiring BioLead, a biologicals start-up in Austin. BioLead is privately owned and has an estimated valuation of $1 billion. Our firm has been hired to evaluate the BioLead acquisition and to advise on its strategic fit with PharmaCo’s biologicals strategy. What factors should the team consider when evaluating whether PharmaCo should acquire BioLead?
additional_info: PharmaCo’s core business is researching, developing, and selling small molecule drugs.
Entry by acquisition is considered because R&D for biologicals is vastly different and competitors are ahead.
Factors to consider include BioLead’s drug pipeline value, number of drugs in development, likelihood of success, estimated revenues and profits.
BioLead’s R&D capabilities, scientific talent, intellectual property, and physical assets.
BioLead’s marketing and sales capabilities, including relationships with key opinion leaders.
Acquisition price of $1 billion.
BioLead’s existing partnerships with pharmaceutical companies.
PharmaCo’s capability gaps and alternatives to acquisition.
Costs throughout drug lifetime include R&D phases, production, regulatory approval, commercialization, and potential failures.
Exhibit 1 shows probabilities of success by R&D phase.
Drug SM1 is estimated to generate $10B in sales if brought to market.
Costs by phase: Phase I $160M, Phase II $125M, Phase III $75M, Filing $5M.
Production costs: Manufacturing 5%, Logistics 5%, Other 10% of sales.
Probabilities of success: Phase I 70%, Phase II 40%, Phase III 50%, Filing 90%.
Risks of integration include culture clash, communication barriers, talent retention, and lack of overlap in expertise.
additional_questions: What costs should be considered throughout the drug’s lifetime?
What is the valuation of BioLead’s drug SM1 based on given data?
What are the major risks of integrating the R&D functions of BioLead and PharmaCo?
solution: The valuation of drug SM1 is approximately $739 million after discounting for probabilities of success and subtracting production and R&D costs. PharmaCo should consider the strategic fit of BioLead’s pipeline, R&D capabilities, marketing reach, acquisition price, and alternatives. Integration risks include culture clash, communication issues, talent loss, and operational challenges. A thorough cost-benefit and risk analysis is essential before acquisition.
evaluation: Candidate should demonstrate ability to structure evaluation criteria, perform discounted cash flow valuation with probabilities, identify integration risks, and provide strategic recommendations. Attention to pharmaceutical industry specifics and comprehensive brainstorming are valued.

title: Gas Station
description: Our client is a large oil and gas company with branches all over the United States. Over the past year or so (2011-2012), they have noticed a decline in profits. What factors may be contributing to this and what can they do to alleviate the situation?
additional_info: The business has 2 segments: gas/filling station and convenience store
Gas station segment traditionally has lower profit margin
Convenience store traditionally has higher profit margin
Some customers shop at the gas stations only. Some shop at the stores only and some go to both on the same trip
The number of our client’s gas stations/stores has remained constant in the past 3 years
Our client’s gas stations’ revenues are within industry average, but prices of items in their convenience stores are higher than those of major competitors
Exhibit 1: U.S.A. average gas and oil prices, 2008-2012
Exhibit 2: Breakdown of sales and costs by segment
Exhibit 3: Consumer demand by segment
additional_questions: What factors may be contributing to the decrease in profit?
What do you think is responsible for the decrease in profit from 2011 to 2012?
What can our client do to increase their profitability?
solution: The profit decrease from 2011-2012 was mainly due to a drop in revenue in the convenience stores segment, despite an increase in gas station segment profits. The drop in convenience store revenue was caused by a shift in customer behavior, with fewer customers visiting both gas station and convenience store on the same trip. Recommendations include promoting synergy between the two segments, such as offering convenience store coupons for gas fills or gas points for store purchases, and investigating the root cause of the shift away from convenience stores.
evaluation: Candidate should identify key profit drivers, analyze revenue and cost breakdowns by segment, interpret exhibits correctly, recognize customer behavior shifts, and propose actionable recommendations focused on revenue improvement and synergy.

title: Baby Helmets
description: Our client is a manufacturer of casts and supports used in correcting bone structure. They recently developed 3 new baby helmets and would like to know if they should launch one or more of these products onto the market. Specifically, is a target profit of $1 million a year reasonable?
additional_info: Client wishes to reach the target profit of $1 million within the first year
Client handles entire manufacturing process
Baby helmets are used to correct the shape of the skull
Helmets are once-use for a duration of a month; a baby will only need to use a correctional helmet once in his/her life
Client has developed one product in each of the 3 categories of helmets (strong, medium, weak)
Current market dominated by 2 major players, each with ~30% market share
U.S. population = 320 million
Exhibit 1: Need for baby helmets by age group
additional_questions: What factors should our client consider in deciding whether or not to enter the market?
Please estimate the market size for baby helmets.
Is a target profit of $1m a year realistic?
Assuming that our client can get a maximum market share of 20%, what would be a more reasonable target profit? What can they do to achieve it?
What risks are associated with your proposed strategy?
solution: The target profit of $1 million a year is not realistic even if the client launches all 3 products and captures 20% market share, given the dominance of existing competitors. A more reasonable target profit is about $0.4 million. Recommendations include launching all three products, possibly lowering prices to gain market share, considering cost reductions via outsourcing, and potentially launching products sequentially to reduce risk.
evaluation: Candidate should estimate market size accurately, calculate profitability per product, assess market share feasibility, identify competitive landscape, and discuss risks and strategic options.

title: Animal Drug
description: Our client is a U.S. based startup company that has recently obtained FDA approval for their only product, a new steroid hormone for pigs. Research reports have predicted the animal hormone market to be very promising in China in the near future. A generous individual has offered to cover all costs for our client to relocate the entire business to China in 2015. Should they accept the offer?
additional_info: The individual’s offer is purely out of generosity; client will not have to pay back relocation costs
Hormones used on Chinese farms are currently supplied exclusively by domestic producers
Client’s new drug currently sells for $20/kg in the U.S.
Pig hormone market in China is dominated by 3 major players
Client has a good global reputation and has received several prizes for high product quality
Production costs in China vs. U.S. are roughly the same
Exhibit 1: Pork consumption in China vs. U.S.A. (2012)
Exhibit 2: Treatment of pigs with hormones in China vs. U.S.A. (2012)
Exhibit 3: Pricing and volume of drugs sold by Chinese companies, 2008-2012
additional_questions: What factors should our client consider in deciding whether or not to relocate to China?
What is our client’s potential market in China and in the U.S. in 2015?
If our client decides to relocate to China, at what price should they market their drug?
What other options does our client have?
solution: Two viable options exist: 1) relocate to China, target state-owned farms, charge at least $16/kg to be more profitable than U.S. sales, leveraging reputation to justify price and capture 2% market share; 2) do not relocate, focus on maintaining U.S. market dominance due to competitive pressures, government barriers, and pricing challenges in China.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market size and growth, competitive landscape, pricing strategy, cost structure, and strategic options including relocation and market entry modes.

title: Burrito Cart
description: Our client is the owner of a burrito cart in the city of Sunnydale. His business is profitable but he wishes to expand it and increase profitability by operating a second burrito cart in the city. Would you recommend that he does so?
additional_info: There are 2 clusters of food carts in Sunnydale: Uptown and Downtown
Client’s cart is currently uptown and he works 5 days/week, 4 weeks/month
Client charges $5 per burrito, sells no more than 50 burritos/day
On average, one customer buys one burrito per day
Prime hours of operation uptown: 11:30 am to 1:30 pm
Prime hours of operation downtown: 11:00 am to 2:00 pm
There are 100 customers/hour uptown and 200 customers/hour downtown per day
Variable cost per burrito is $1
Fixed costs for operating a new cart is $1,500/month
Upfront costs (licenses etc.) can be ignored
Exhibit 1: Customer preference in the two clusters
Exhibit 2: Map of food carts in the two clusters
additional_questions: Would a second burrito cart be profitable?
What other options does our client have to expand his business?
Our client currently has enough capacity to make a maximum of 50 burritos/day. Does this maximize their profit?
How many more burritos would our client need to sell at their existing cart in order to make the same profit as opening a new cart?
solution: Opening a second cart is more profitable uptown than downtown, but expanding capacity at the existing cart is better overall. Selling an additional 12 burritos/day (from 50 to 62) at the existing cart matches the profit of opening a new cart. Recommendations include expanding capacity at the current location and exploring other expansion options such as new menu items or different food types.
evaluation: Candidate should perform market sizing, profitability calculations, competitive analysis, capacity assessment, and provide strategic recommendations.

title: Sports Cards & Signed T-shirts
description: Our client is a vendor of sports cards with football, basketball and baseball stars. Their revenue in 2012 was $150 million. They wish to expand their business, and are thinking of 2 options: 1) selling new sports cards (e.g. golf or ice-skating stars) and 2) selling signed T-shirts with movie stars or pop singers. Which option should they go for?
additional_info: Client has no direct contact with potential athletes but has some contacts with movie star agents
Client has eyes on 8 athletes and 12 movie stars/pop singers
All featured stars are on a 5-year contract
Exhibit 1: Projected profitability of sports cards vs. signed T-shirts
additional_questions: How do the two options compare financially?
The CEO’s college buddy, Mike, has contacts with many movie stars but has not brought any revenue or signed contracts in 6 months. Should the CEO keep Mike around? Why or why not?
solution: Both options yield similar profitability with sports cards slightly higher by $0.2m over 5 years. Given existing contacts with movie stars, client should pursue signed T-shirts for easier implementation. The CEO should consider replacing Mike as VP but may retain him in a PR role due to his network and relationship with the CEO. Client should also explore other growth opportunities beyond these options.
evaluation: Candidate should compare financials, consider qualitative factors like contacts and implementation ease, and provide justified HR recommendations.

title: Diabetes Device
description: Our client is a manufacturer of medical devices used to treat chronic diseases. They recently launched a new device for treating diabetic patients. The device has two components – an injector and a disposable cartridge. They would like to know how they should sell these products in order to maximize profit. What advice would you give them?
additional_info: Production costs: Injector $40/unit, Disposable cartridge $20/unit
Both sold directly to patients with prescription
Injector reusable up to 20 times; cartridges are one-time use
Client has acceptance rate data at various prices from customer surveys
Exhibit 1: Price and acceptance rates for the two components
Exhibit 2: Cost savings per bundle at various bundling ratios
additional_questions: What prices should our client set the two components at in order to maximize profit?
Can you think of other pricing/selling strategies that may help increase profitability further?

Our client thinks bundling is a good idea to save on costs. For every 5 cartridges bundled with an injector, they are willing to take a $10 discount from the combined price. Should our client sell the two components as a bundle? If so, in what ratio?
Our client would like to compare their bundling strategy with the bundling strategies of other businesses. What other product bundles can you think of?
solution: Optimal prices are $150 for the injector and $60 for the cartridge. Bundling the products at a 1:20 ratio (injector to cartridge) increases gross margin by $16 compared to selling separately. Bundling is recommended to increase profitability. Client should conduct further surveys to refine acceptance rates for bundling ratios.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate profitability at different prices, analyze acceptance rates, evaluate bundling benefits, and brainstorm comparable bundling examples.

title: Yumy Co.
description: Our client is a fast food chain in the U.S. with annual revenues of $1.5 billion. They would like to grow their revenue, and would like advice on how they can do so. What would you tell them?
additional_info: Client has 750 stores across the U.S.
Two competitors roughly the same size as client
U.S. population is 320 million, of which 300 million visit fast food chains at least once a week
There are 52 weeks in a year (approximate to 50)
Exhibit 1: Customer segmentation
Exhibit 2: Comparison between revenues of 2 competitors
additional_questions: What is our client’s growth potential and how can they increase their revenues?
After doing some research, our client decided to introduce ice cream and milk shakes to their menu. How can they evaluate whether or not this is a good idea?
Approximately how much revenue can our client generate from ice cream and shakes?
What do you think might be causing the difference in revenues from ice cream and shakes between competitors A and B?
Because of start-up costs, our client’s competitors charge only $1/cone, whereas our client has to charge $2/cone initially to break even. What can our client do to alleviate the high costs?
solution: Client has less than 1% market share, so growth potential is high. Introducing ice cream and shakes can generate ~$75 million/year (~5% revenue increase). Initial profit margin will be low due to higher costs, so client should improve product attractiveness and reduce costs over time. Pilot studies and competitor benchmarking are recommended to evaluate the new products.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate market size and revenue potential, segment customers, analyze competitor data, identify cost challenges, and propose growth strategies.

title: Apoplexy Drug
description: Our client is a pharmaceutical company who has recently developed a drug for apoplexy. The drug has been clinically proven to be safe and effective, and is the only product that is at this stage in our client’s business. Should our client proceed with commercializing the drug?
additional_info: Apoplexy is another term for stroke
There are 260,000 cases of apoplexy per year
Treatment is only effective within the first 3 hours; 1st hour is critical
Apoplexy drugs cannot be administered until patient reaches the hospital
Client plans to charge $8,000 per use (1 use/patient)
One competitor charges $6,000 per use
Client’s drug has higher success rate than competitor’s in 1st hour, same success rate after
Exhibit 1: Patient distribution by methods of help and geographic location
additional_questions: What factors does our client need to consider?
How much revenue can the product generate per year?
If fixed costs are $240 million/year and variable costs are $5,000 per unit, how much profit can be made from the drug per year?
Our client is thinking of lowering the price of their drug to $6,000 per use to capture more of the market. Is this a good idea?
solution: Client can capture 152,000 patients at $8,000 per use, generating $1.2 billion revenue and $200 million profit after costs. Lowering price to $6,000 increases market share but results in a slight loss due to higher costs. Recommendation is to commercialize at $8,000 per use. Risks include competitive response and new market entrants; client should develop a strong marketing plan.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze market size considering treatment time sensitivity, calculate revenues and profits, evaluate pricing strategy impact on market share and profitability, and discuss risks.

title: Towels
description: Our client produces towels of various sizes and wants to reduce costs. Should they cut packaging and refining costs or stop producing extra large towels altogether?
additional_info: Costs per towel: $8, $10, $14, $20 for small, medium, large, extra large respectively
Number of towels produced per year: Small 500, Medium 1000, Large 500, Extra Large 300
Profit margins: Small 50%, Medium 40%, Large 35%, Extra Large 20%
Cutting packaging costs of small towels by $0.50 and medium towels by $0.25, plus cutting refining/decorating costs by $1 per towel
Stopping production of extra large towels would save $6,000 in costs but reduce profit by $1,500
solution: Cutting out extra large towels from production saves $4,500 net per year, which is more than the $2,800 saved by cutting packaging and refining costs. Recommend terminating extra large towel production and consider cost cuts on other towels cautiously due to risk of revenue loss.
evaluation: Candidate should calculate cost savings and profit impact, recognize profit margin differences, and recommend the option with higher net savings. Ability to brainstorm other cost reduction methods is valued.

title: Surgical Robot
description: Our client is a hospital considering investing in a new surgical robot, Robot X, which reduces human work and increases precision. Should they invest in Robot X?
additional_info: Robot X costs $3.2 million with $200,000 annual maintenance; Da Vinci Robot costs $1.5 million with $100,000 annual maintenance
Robot X allows hiring 5 fewer technicians (each paid $60,000/year)
Robot X enables 200 additional surgeries per year generating $300 extra per surgery; Da Vinci enables 50 additional surgeries
Technology replacement rate is about 10 years
Break-even analysis shows Robot X breaks even in 20 years, Da Vinci in 7 years
additional_questions: What features of Robot X should our client consider in deciding whether or not to invest in it?
Would Robot X be a good investment?
What other options does our client have for improving their services?
Aside from profitability, what other factors would likely influence our client’s decision on whether or not to invest in a robot for surgery?
solution: Robot X is not a good investment due to a 20-year break-even period, which exceeds its lifetime. Da Vinci Robot has a 7-year break-even, closer to its lifetime but still marginal. Recommend not investing in Robot X and consider other service improvements or buying Da Vinci. Also consider competitive dynamics and operator/patient preferences.
evaluation: Candidate should perform break-even calculations, compare alternatives, consider qualitative factors, and brainstorm other service improvement options.

title: Desert City
description: Our client is a city developer asked to build a water supply in a desert city in the Middle East. Should they take on the project?
additional_info: Client has built water supplies before but not in desert conditions
Client wants to start making a profit within 3 years after completion
Projected water consumption is 1 million metric tons per year
Client would be the only company building the water supply
Upfront costs total $110 million
Government may subsidize part of the upfront costs
Water usage rates vary by consumption type (agricultural, residential, industrial, commercial)
additional_questions: What facilities are associated with building a water supply?
How much would it cost to build the water supply?
Calculate the break-even point for this investment.
What options exist to shorten the break-even time?
If the government subsidizes 40% of upfront costs, would this change the decision?
solution: Without subsidy, break-even is 5 years, longer than client’s target. With a 40% government subsidy on upfront costs, break-even shortens to 3 years, meeting client’s goal. Recommend taking the project only if subsidy is secured. Risks include lack of desert experience and technical challenges.
evaluation: Candidate should identify relevant facilities, calculate costs and break-even, analyze impact of subsidies, and consider risks.

title: Call Center
description: Our client is setting up a call center in South Africa and is considering 5 different cities. Which city should they choose?
additional_info: Call center will provide product support and promote products globally
Open 24 hours a day, 5 days a week; employees work 6-hour shifts
Client willing to pay up to $1.5/hour per employee
Details on call types, call volumes, and employee requirements provided
Survey data on worker willingness to work at different wages in 5 cities
Data on cable/internet users and competitors in the 5 cities
additional_questions: What factors should be considered in choosing the call center location?
What is the minimum number of employees needed?
Which cities have enough employees at the required wage?
What non-wage reasons might cause workers to reject job offers?
Considering potential cable/internet service expansion, which city is best?
solution: Baviaans is recommended due to higher potential revenue from cable/internet customers despite slightly higher labor costs. Hantam has lower labor costs but fewer customers. Candidate should consider labor supply, costs, and market potential.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze labor supply and costs, calculate staffing needs, evaluate market potential, and consider qualitative factors.

title: Candy Stand
description: Your friend wants to open a candy stand outside Grand Central Station. Is this a good idea?
additional_info: 4 million jobs in NYC; 5% travel through Grand Central daily
Grand Central has 5 main exits with equal traffic
0.5% of people exiting buy items from stands; each buys 1 item at $1
Friend wants to work 20 days/month
Licensing costs $20,000/year; stand costs $10,000/year; variable cost $0.25/item
additional_questions: How much profit can your friend make per year?
What ways can revenue be improved?
What other factors are important for success besides financials?
If your friend disagrees with your advice, what would you do?
If your friend refuses to accept the advice, how would you respond?
solution: Estimated profit is about $6,000/year, insufficient to cover living expenses in NYC. Recommend not opening the stand and suggest looking for other job opportunities.
evaluation: Candidate should estimate revenues and costs, identify low profitability, brainstorm improvements, and handle interpersonal dynamics.

title: Bakery
description: A friend’s bakery is making losses. Help identify the problem and suggest how to bring it back to profitability.
additional_info: Bakery open Monday 10am-5pm, Tuesday-Thursday 10am-1pm
Located in a rich neighborhood with mostly local customers
No changes in overhead, rent, or labor costs
6 employees excluding owner
Sales data for bread, cookies, and cakes over several months
Customer foot traffic data by hour and day
additional_questions: What is the main reason for the drop in profits?
What should be done immediately to improve profits?
What long-term strategies can help the business?
solution: Drop in cookie sales contributed most to profit decline. Recommend short-term promotions to boost cookie sales and similar strategies for other products. Consider shifting business hours to peak customer times and use social media for marketing.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze sales and margin data, identify key issues, propose short- and long-term solutions, and communicate in a friendly tone.

title: Diagnostic Test
description: Our client developed a prenatal test for Down’s syndrome. Should they launch it on the market?
additional_info: Test is FDA approved
Two other prenatal tests exist: blood test (80% accuracy, $50) and amniocentesis (99% accuracy, $2000)
Client’s test has 95% accuracy and costs $1000 to produce
Patent expires in 10 years
1 in 1000 babies born with Down’s syndrome
US population 320 million, life expectancy 80
Most insurance policies do not cover prenatal testing
Risk levels and testing rates vary by age group
additional_questions: What factors should be considered in deciding to enter the market?
What is the current market size for prenatal Down’s syndrome tests?
What is a reasonable price for the client’s test?
Which age groups should be targeted?
How much profit can be made before patent expiry?
solution: Recommend launching the test at about $1,500 price point, targeting medium risk (age 16-35) group. Estimated 10-year profit is about $1.225 billion, averaging $120 million/year, a significant increase over current profits. Risks include market fluctuations and patient preferences.
evaluation: Candidate should estimate market size, price appropriately, segment customers, forecast profits, and consider risks.

title: Taxi Service
description: A private equity firm is considering investing in TaxiCall, a taxi service in Venezuela. Should they invest?
additional_info: Taxi industry in Venezuela is growing
TaxiCall is second largest with 33% market share
Venezuela population 30 million
Customer segmentation by income: high, high-medium, low-medium, low
Data on taxi usage probability, frequency, distance, and fare by segment
additional_questions: What factors should be considered in deciding to buy TaxiCall?
How to size the taxi market in Venezuela per year?
What factors determine taxi usage frequency?
What insights can be drawn from customer segmentation and usage data?
Which customer segment generates the most revenue?
solution: Recommend investing in TaxiCall due to large market share in a growing market. High-medium income segment generates most revenue. Candidate should analyze market size, customer segments, and revenue potential. Further financial due diligence advised.
evaluation: Candidate should consider market growth, competitive position, customer segmentation, and revenue drivers.

title: Paper Company
description: Our client manufactures white and colored paper and wants to reduce costs by 25%. What advice would you give?
additional_info: Client produces equal quantities of white and colored paper
Fixed costs like machinery and rent are hard to reduce short-term
Dyeing process is the largest cost contributor
Client buys dyes from 5 suppliers at varying prices and purchase shares
Current cost to produce paper is $32/kg
Goal is to reduce costs by $8/kg (25%)
additional_questions: What factors contribute to high production costs?
What are the major costs for the client?
How much does the client spend per kg on dyes?
If client buys all dyes from supplier B, what discount is needed to achieve 25% cost reduction?
What risks are involved in buying all dyes from one supplier?
solution: Cutting costs by 25% is unrealistic as it requires a near 50% discount from supplier B. Recommend reducing dye costs by consolidating purchases to 2-3 cheaper suppliers to balance cost savings and risk. Avoid relying on a single supplier.
evaluation: Candidate should analyze cost structure, calculate weighted costs, negotiate discounts, and assess supplier risk.

title: Hepatitis Drug
description: Our client developed a drug for Hepatitis C. Should they launch it, and if so, when?
additional_info: Two FDA approved Hepatitis C drugs and five in clinical trials
Client’s drug is in Phase III trials
All drugs are intravenous and require lifelong administration
Hepatitis C infection rate is 1%, diagnosis rate 10%, treatment rate 25%
US population 320 million
Drugs cost between $10,000 and $60,000 per dose
Government campaign may increase treatment rate by 8% per year
Some insurance companies now cover treatment
additional_questions: What is the size of the Hepatitis C drug market?
Why is the treatment rate low?
How long will it take for treated patients to double given an 8% annual increase?
Why would insurance companies cover expensive treatment?
solution: Market size estimated at $2.8 billion. Low treatment rate due to asymptomatic nature, cost, and delivery method. Treatment rate doubling in 9 years (rule of 72). Insurance coverage may reduce long-term costs by preventing complications. Recommend launching drug considering market and growth.
evaluation: Candidate should estimate market size, analyze patient behavior, forecast growth, and consider payer incentives.
